topics.title: Text and Binary Files - Reading and Writing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Text and Binary Files - Reading and Writing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PCCBL508 – Computational Statistics Lab**, a **file** is a named storage location on a secondary storage device (HDD, SSD) that persists data beyond the lifetime of a running program. Files are classified into two fundamental categories:

- **Text File**: A sequence of **human-readable characters** encoded using standards such as **ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange)** or **UTF-8 (Unicode Transformation Format – 8-bit)**. Newlines are represented by special control characters (e.g., `'\n'` on Unix/Linux, `'\r\n'` on Windows). Each character occupies **1 byte (8 bits)** in pure ASCII and 1–4 bytes in UTF-8.
- **Binary File**: A sequence of **raw bytes** that stores data in the same internal binary representation used by the computer's memory (e.g., integers as 2/4/8-byte two's complement, floats as IEEE 754 floating-point representation). It is **not human-readable** when opened in a text editor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition to Memorize:**
> *“A text file stores data as a stream of characters terminated by an End-Of-File (EOF) marker, while a binary file stores data as a stream of bytes exactly as represented in primary memory, requiring no character translation during I/O operations.”*

The **File Handling Cycle** in Python (or R) consists of three mandatory steps:
1. **Open** the file using a built-in function (associating the file with a stream/object).
2. **Read** or **Write** data using stream methods.
3. **Close** the file to release OS-level resources (file descriptor / handle).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are working in a **library** (your computer's hard disk):

- A **Text File** is like a **notebook written in plain English**. Anyone (any program) can open it and read the sentences. If you write `"123"`, the librarian stores the **characters** `'1'`, `'2'`, `'3'` separately — three symbols. If you want to use the number `123` in a calculation, you must first **translate** the words into a number.
- A **Binary File** is like a **packed suitcase of raw goods**. The suitcase contains items (integers, floats, images) in their **physical, ready-to-use form**. A luggage scanner (the program) opens the suitcase and immediately uses the items — no translation needed. The suitcase is meaningless to a human inspector, but a machine understands it instantly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in Statistics?** When you load a 1-million-row CSV (text), the computer must parse each character into numbers. When you load a NumPy `.npy` or a Pickle file (binary), the data is already in machine format — **reading is 5×–50× faster**, which is critical for large datasets in statistical modelling.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **ASCII size per character** | **1 byte = 8 bits** | Fixed-width encoding |
| **UTF-8 size per character** | **1–4 bytes** | Variable-width Unicode |
| **Default Python text encoding** | **UTF-8** (since Python 3) | Platform-independent |
| **IEEE 754 double precision** | **8 bytes = 64 bits** | Standard for Python `float` |
| **Python `int` (arbitrary precision)** | **≥ 28 bytes** | Stored as binary in `.pkl` |
| **End-Of-File (EOF) in text mode** | **Empty string `''` on `read()`** | Sentinel value |
| **Standard text file newline (Linux)** | **`'\n'` (LF) — 1 byte** | Line Feed |
| **Standard text file newline (Windows)** | **`'\r\n'` (CRLF) — 2 bytes** | Carriage Return + Line Feed |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The File Object — Operational Lifecycle

When `open()` is invoked, the operating system performs the following sequence:

1. **Lookup**: The OS searches the **File Allocation Table (FAT)** or **inode table** (Linux) for the filename.
2. **Permission Check**: The OS verifies the **UID (User ID)** and **GID (Group ID)** against the file's permission bits (`rwx`).
3. **Buffer Allocation**: A memory buffer (typically **4 KB – 64 KB**, OS-dependent) is reserved in RAM.
4. **File Descriptor Creation**: An integer handle (a small non-negative integer) is returned to the Python interpreter.
5. **Stream Binding**: Python wraps this descriptor inside a high-level **`io.TextIOWrapper`** (for text) or **`io.BufferedReader/Writer`** (for binary) object.
6. **Reference Return**: The wrapper object is returned to the caller.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: Closing a file via `f.close()` is what calls the OS-level `close()` syscall. If you skip it, the file remains in the OS's open-file table — repeated leaks eventually cause the dreaded `OSError: [Errno 24] Too many open files` in long-running statistical simulations.

## 2.2 File Access Modes — Master Table (No Pipes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The string passed as the **second argument** of `open()` is called the **mode**. The mode string is *case-sensitive* (`'r'` ≠ `'R'` on Linux).

| Mode | Name | File Must Exist? | Pointer Position | Truncates? | Readable? | Writable? | Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `'r'` | Read (default) | **Yes** | Beginning | No | Yes | No | **Text** |
| `'w'` | Write | No | Beginning | **Yes** | No | Yes | **Text** |
| `'x'` | Exclusive Create | No (fails if exists) | Beginning | No | No | Yes | **Text** |
| `'a'` | Append | No | **End** | No | No | Yes | **Text** |
| `'r+'` | Read & Write | **Yes** | Beginning | No | Yes | Yes | **Text** |
| `'w+'` | Write & Read | No | Beginning | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | **Text** |
| `'rb'` | Read Binary | **Yes** | Beginning | No | Yes | No | **Binary** |
| `'wb'` | Write Binary | No | Beginning | **Yes** | No | Yes | **Binary** |
| `'ab'` | Append Binary | No | **End** | No | No | Yes | **Binary** |
| `'rb+'` | Read & Write Binary | **Yes** | Beginning | No | Yes | Yes | **Binary** |
| `'wb+'` | Write & Read Binary | No | Beginning | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | **Binary** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Truncation** means: if the file already contains data, opening in `'w'` or `'w+'` will **erase all existing content** instantly. This is the #1 cause of data loss in beginner programming — always double-check the mode!

## 2.3 Core Read/Write Methods — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Method | Works On | Return Type | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| `f.read(n)` | Text / Binary | `str` / `bytes` | Reads **n** characters/bytes; if n omitted, reads **entire file** |
| `f.readline()` | Text / Binary | `str` / `bytes` | Reads **one line** including the `'\n'`; returns `''` at EOF |
| `f.readlines()` | Text / Binary | `list[str]` / `list[bytes]` | Reads **all lines** into a list (memory-heavy for large files) |
| `f.write(s)` | Text / Binary | `int` | Writes string/bytes; returns **number of characters/bytes written** |
| `f.writelines(lst)` | Text / Binary | `None` | Writes a list of strings; **does NOT add newlines automatically** |
| `f.seek(offset, whence)` | Text (limited) / Binary | `int` | Moves pointer; `whence`: 0=START, 1=CURRENT, 2=END |
| `f.tell()` | Text / Binary | `int` | Returns **current pointer position** in bytes |
| `f.flush()` | Text / Binary | `None` | Forces buffer write to disk (bypasses OS cache) |
| `f.truncate(size)` | Text / Binary | `int` | Resizes file to **size** bytes at current pointer |
| `f.close()` | Text / Binary | `None` | Closes file; sets `f.closed = True` |
| `f.closed` | Text / Binary | `bool` | Attribute (not method) — `True` if file is closed |

> [!WARNING]
> **Text-mode `seek()` limitation**: In text mode, the only valid `seek()` calls are `seek(0, 0)` (rewind) and `seek(0, 2)` (jump to end). Any other offset raises `io.UnsupportedOperation` because multi-byte UTF-8 characters make byte-offset arithmetic ambiguous.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Computational Statistics**, file I/O is the backbone of every data pipeline:

- **Clinical Trials**: Reading patient CSV records (text) and writing encrypted model parameters to disk (binary).
- **Machine Learning**: Saving trained weights using `pickle.dump()` (binary) for later inference without re-training — saves hours of compute.
- **IoT Sensor Networks**: Streaming telemetry as binary for compact storage (1 million readings fit in 8 MB as `float64` vs 15 MB as text).
- **Genomics**: FASTA/FASTQ files are text-based for human inspection, while `.bam` and `.vcf` are binary for computational efficiency.
- **Banking**: Transaction logs are written in binary for tamper-evidence and speed; reports are generated in text/CSV for auditors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Context Manager Protocol — Why `with` Exists

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a statistical routine that opens a results file, writes 5 intermediate computations, and may raise an exception on the 3rd write (e.g., due to a `ZeroDivisionError`).

### 3.1.2 Naïve Approach (Unsafe)

```python
f = open("results.txt", "w")
mean = sum(data) / len(data)     # OK
f.write(f"Mean: {mean}\n")
variance = sum((x-mean)**2 for x in data) / (len(data)-1)  # OK
f.write(f"Variance: {variance}\n")
# Imagine a crash here — file never closes!
f.close()
```

### 3.1.3 The `with` Statement — Mathematical Guarantee

The **Context Manager Protocol** in Python is defined by two magic methods: `__enter__()` and `__exit__()`. The `with` statement provides the following **invariant**:

$$
\forall \, \text{code block} \, B \, \text{inside `with`}: \; \text{if exception} \, e \, \text{occurs in} \, B \implies \text{\_\_exit\_\_ is called before propagation}
$$

In simple words: **no matter what happens inside the block — success, exception, `KeyboardInterrupt` — the file is *guaranteed* to be closed.**

```python
with open("results.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Mean: 42.5\n")
    f.write("Variance: 7.8\n")
    # Even if an error occurs here, f.close() runs automatically.
# Outside the block, f.closed == True
```

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Trace

Let us trace what happens when `open("results.txt", "w")` is executed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Python calls the C-level} \; \text{open(2)} \; \text{syscall with flags} \; \text{O\_WRONLY} \mid \text{O\_CREAT} \mid \text{O\_TRUNC} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Kernel checks pathname} \rightarrow \text{allocates a new inode (file descriptor, e.g., 4)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Kernel returns fd = 4 to the Python interpreter} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Python wraps fd = 4 in a} \; \text{io.TextIOWrapper} \; \text{object} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{The wrapper's} \; \text{\_\_enter\_\_()} \; \text{method returns the wrapper itself (assigned to `f`)} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{User code executes inside the `with` block} \\
\text{Step 7a (Normal):} \quad & \text{On block exit,} \; \text{\_\_exit\_\_(None, None, None)} \; \text{calls} \; \text{f.close()} \\
\text{Step 7b (Exception):} \quad & \text{On block exit,} \; \text{\_\_exit\_\_(type, value, traceback)} \; \text{still calls} \; \text{f.close()} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Kernel releases fd = 4 back to the process's open-file table}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The `|` symbol above is a bitwise OR, **not** a Python pipe operator in this context — it represents how the OS combines the access flags. This is **not** part of a markdown table and is safe to use.

## 3.2 Binary File Size Calculation — Worked Example

### 3.2.1 Problem

You have a NumPy array `arr` of shape `(10000, 5)` containing `float64` values. Calculate the size in bytes when saved as:
1. A CSV (text) file, assuming each value prints to **8 characters** (e.g., `123.4567`).
2. A binary `.npy` file.

### 3.2.2 Solution

**Part (a) — CSV Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of values} \; N &= 10000 \times 5 = 50000 \\
\text{Characters per value} &= 8 \\
\text{Newlines per row} &= 1 \quad (\text{ends each of 10000 rows}) \\
\text{Commas per row} &= 4 \quad (\text{separates 5 columns}) \\
\text{Total characters} &= N \times 8 \;+\; (\text{commas}) \;+\; (\text{newlines}) \\
&= 50000 \times 8 \;+\; 10000 \times 4 \;+\; 10000 \times 1 \\
&= 400000 \;+\; 40000 \;+\; 10000 \\
&= 450000 \; \text{bytes} \; \approx \; \mathbf{439.45 \; KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Binary `.npy` Size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bytes per float64} &= 8 \quad \text{(IEEE 754 double precision)} \\
\text{Header overhead} &\approx 128 \; \text{bytes} \quad \text{(NumPy magic + shape + dtype metadata)} \\
\text{Total size} &= N \times 8 \;+\; 128 \\
&= 50000 \times 8 \;+\; 128 \\
&= 400000 \;+\; 128 \\
&= 400128 \; \text{bytes} \; \approx \; \mathbf{390.75 \; KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Compression Ratio

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ratio} &= \frac{\text{CSV size}}{\text{Binary size}} = \frac{450000}{400128} \approx 1.125 \\
\text{Savings} &= \frac{450000 - 400128}{450000} \times 100\% \approx 11.08\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> For purely numeric data, the saving is modest because CSV is already compact. For mixed-type data (strings, integers, floats, booleans), binary can be **2×–10× smaller** because binary avoids the per-character overhead of delimiters and quotes.

## 3.3 File Pointer Arithmetic — Reading Partial Records

Suppose a binary file `scores.bin` stores 1000 student scores, each as a 4-byte signed integer (`int32`, struct format `'i'`). You want to read **only the 500th student** without reading the previous 499.

```python
import struct

with open("scores.bin", "rb") as f:
    target_index = 499                              # 0-based
    record_size  = struct.calcsize("i")             # = 4 bytes
    f.seek(target_index * record_size, 0)           # Jump to byte 499*4 = 1996
    raw = f.read(record_size)                       # Read exactly 4 bytes
    score_500 = struct.unpack("i", raw)[0]          # Unpack into Python int
    print(f"Student #500 score: {score_500}")
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{calcsize("i")} = 4 \quad \text{(sizeof C int on most platforms)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{seek offset} = 499 \times 4 = 1996 \quad \text{bytes from start} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{read(4)} \; \text{returns the 4-byte slice} \; b'\text{...}' \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{unpack("i", raw)} \; \text{interprets 4 bytes as a signed 32-bit integer} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{[0]} \; \text{extracts the single value from the returned tuple}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Big-Endian vs Little-Endian**: On a little-endian machine (e.g., x86, ARM), `struct.pack("i", 258)` produces `b'\x02\x01\x00\x00'`. To ensure cross-platform compatibility, always use the standard size prefix: `<i` (little-endian, standard size) or `>i` (big-endian, standard size). **KTU examiner favourite question!**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 File I/O Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program calls open path mode] --> B{File Exists?}
    B -- Yes and mode is w or w+ --> C[Truncate to 0 bytes]
    B -- No and mode is w or a --> D[Create new file]
    B -- No and mode is r --> E[Raise FileNotFoundError]
    B -- Yes and mode is r or a --> F[Open existing file]
    C --> G[Allocate file descriptor in OS]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Return file object to Python]
    H --> I{Operation type}
    I -- Read --> J[read / readline / readlines]
    I -- Write --> K[write / writelines]
    J --> L[Data flows from disk to RAM buffer]
    K --> M[Data flows from RAM buffer to disk]
    L --> N[with block end or close call]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Flush buffer to physical disk]
    O --> P[Kernel releases file descriptor]
    P --> Q[File object marked closed]
    E --> R[Exception propagates to caller]
```

## 4.2 Text vs Binary File Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TEXT["TEXT FILE STREAM"]
        T1[Integer 12345] --> T2[Convert to characters]
        T2 --> T3[Characters 1 2 3 4 5]
        T3 --> T4[Encode as UTF-8 bytes]
        T4 --> T5[5 bytes on disk]
    end
    subgraph BINARY["BINARY FILE STREAM"]
        B1[Integer 12345] --> B2[Direct memory copy]
        B2 --> B3[Two complement 4 byte int]
        B3 --> B4[4 bytes on disk]
    end
    T5 --> X[Disk storage]
    B4 --> X
```

## 4.3 Context Manager Execution Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant CM as Context Manager
    participant File as File Object
    participant OS as Operating System

    User->>CM: with open data.txt w as f
    CM->>OS: syscall open path O_WRONLY O_CREAT O_TRUNC
    OS-->>CM: Return file descriptor 4
    CM->>File: Wrap fd in TextIOWrapper
    CM-->>User: __enter__ returns f
    User->>File: f.write Hello
    File->>OS: Write 5 bytes to fd 4
    User->>File: f.write World
    File->>OS: Write 5 bytes to fd 4
    Note over User,OS: Block ends or exception raised
    CM->>File: __exit__ triggered automatically
    File->>OS: flush buffer and close fd 4
    OS-->>File: fd 4 released
    File-->>User: f.closed equals True
```

## 4.4 File Pointer Positioning (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[File Start Position 0] --> B[After read 10 bytes Position 10]
    B --> C[After seek 0 0 Position 0]
    C --> D[After seek 0 2 Position End of File]
    D --> E[After seek 5 1 Position 5 bytes after current]
    E --> F[tell returns current byte offset]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between text mode and binary mode file operations in Python. In which scenario would you prefer binary mode for statistical data storage?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Text Mode (`'r'/'w'`) | Binary Mode (`'rb'/'wb'`) |
|---|---|---|
| Data Unit | **Characters (str)** | **Raw bytes (bytes)** |
| Encoding | Performs automatic **UTF-8 encode/decode** | **No encoding** — bytes pass through unchanged |
| Newline Handling | Translates `'\r\n'` ↔ `'\n'` automatically | No translation — `'\r\n'` stored as 2 raw bytes |
| Use Case | CSV, TXT, JSON, logs | Images, audio, NumPy `.npy`, `pickle`, compiled models |
| Portability | High (human-readable) | Low (machine-specific byte order) |

**Preferred Scenario**: For **large numerical datasets** in statistical computing, binary mode is preferred because (a) **no encoding overhead** during read/write, (b) **exact memory representation** is preserved (no precision loss for `float64`), and (c) **faster I/O** — typically 5–10× speedup for arrays exceeding 100 MB.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the significance of the `'with' statement` in Python file handling. What happens if you open a file but forget to close it?**

**Model Answer:**

The `with` statement implements the **Context Manager Protocol**, which guarantees that the file's `__exit__()` method — and hence `f.close()` — is **always called**, regardless of whether the code block completes successfully or raises an exception.

**Mechanism**:
1. `__enter__()` is invoked on `with` entry → returns the file object bound to the variable after `as`.
2. The code block executes.
3. `__exit__()` is invoked on block exit (normal or exceptional) → triggers `f.close()`.

**Consequences of forgetting `close()`**:
- **Resource Leak**: The OS-level file descriptor remains allocated. After 1024 opens (default Linux `ulimit -n`), the process throws `OSError: Too many open files`.
- **Data Loss**: Buffered writes may never reach the physical disk if the buffer is not flushed before process termination.
- **File Locking (Windows)**: The file remains locked, preventing other processes from modifying it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do **not** write "the `with` statement automatically closes the file" without explaining **how** (mention `__enter__` / `__exit__`). Marks are deducted for vague answers.
> - Always state the **OS-level consequence** (file descriptor leak), not just "the file stays open".

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** Write a Python program that reads a text file `data.txt` containing one integer per line, computes the **mean** and **standard deviation**, and writes the results to `report.txt` in a formatted manner. Use the `with` statement for both files. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to store a NumPy array of shape `(3, 3)` containing floating-point values into a binary file using `struct`, and then read it back. Explain the role of `struct.pack` and `struct.unpack`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

```python
import math

# ---- Read phase ----
numbers = []
with open("data.txt", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        stripped = line.strip()              # Remove \n and surrounding spaces
        if stripped:                         # Skip blank lines
            numbers.append(float(stripped))  # Convert text to number

# ---- Computation phase ----
n = len(numbers)
mean = sum(numbers) / n
variance = sum((x - mean) ** 2 for x in numbers) / (n - 1)   # Sample variance
std_dev = math.sqrt(variance)

# ---- Write phase ----
with open("report.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("Statistical Report\n")
    f.write("=" * 30 + "\n")
    f.write(f"Count        : {n}\n")
    f.write(f"Mean         : {mean:.4f}\n")
    f.write(f"Std Deviation: {std_dev:.4f}\n")
```

**Explanation of key steps:**

- `line.strip()` removes the trailing newline `'\n'` left by `readline()`-style iteration.
- We skip empty lines (`if stripped`) to handle blank lines robustly — a real-world CSV often has them.
- `with` is used **twice** — once for the source file and once for the report file — guaranteeing both are closed.
- Sample standard deviation uses denominator `n − 1` (**Bessel's correction**).

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

```python
import struct
import numpy as np

# Step 1: Create a sample NumPy array
arr = np.array([[1.5, 2.7, 3.1],
                [4.2, 5.8, 6.0],
                [7.3, 8.9, 9.4]], dtype=np.float64)

# Step 2: Write to a binary file
with open("matrix.bin", "wb") as f:
    # Pack a 3x3 grid of doubles; total 9 * 8 = 72 bytes
    fmt = "9d"   # 9 little-endian doubles
    f.write(struct.pack(fmt, *arr.flatten()))

# Step 3: Read back from the binary file
with open("matrix.bin", "rb") as f:
    raw = f.read(72)                          # Read exactly 72 bytes
    unpacked = struct.unpack("9d", raw)       # Returns a tuple of 9 floats
    recovered = np.array(unpacked).reshape(3, 3)

print("Original array:\n", arr)
print("Recovered array:\n", recovered)
print("Arrays identical?", np.array_equal(arr, recovered))
```

**Explanation of `struct.pack` and `struct.unpack`:**

- **`struct.pack(fmt, *values)`**: Converts Python values into a `bytes` object according to a **format string**. In `"9d"`, the `9` means *9 repetitions* and `d` means **C double (8 bytes, IEEE 754)**. The result is a compact 72-byte binary blob.
- **`struct.unpack(fmt, bytes_obj)`**: The inverse operation — parses a `bytes` object back into a tuple of Python values (`float` in this case).
- **Endianness**: Default is **native byte order**. For cross-platform reliability, use `"<9d"` (little-endian) or `">9d"` (big-endian).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the different file opening modes in Python with examples. What is the difference between `'w'` and `'a'` modes? Provide a real-world use case for `'r+'` mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program that demonstrates the use of `f.seek()` and `f.tell()` to **overwrite the middle line** of a 5-line text file without rewriting the entire file. Show the file pointer positions before and after each operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

| Mode | Meaning | Example | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| `'r'` | Read only | `open("in.txt", "r")` | File must exist; pointer at byte 0 |
| `'w'` | Write only | `open("out.txt", "w")` | **Erases existing content**; creates if missing |
| `'a'` | Append only | `open("log.txt", "a")` | Pointer at **EOF**; creates if missing; **never erases** |
| `'x'` | Exclusive create | `open("new.txt", "x")` | **Fails if file exists** — safe for creating fresh files |
| `'r+'` | Read & write | `open("data.txt", "r+")` | File must exist; **does not erase**; pointer at byte 0 |

**`w` vs `a`**:
- `'w'` mode **truncates** the file to zero bytes upon opening — all previous data is lost. The pointer starts at position 0.
- `'a'` mode **preserves** existing data and positions the pointer at the **end of file** (EOF). All writes are appended after existing content.

**Real-world use case for `'r+'`**: A **CSV log file** where you need to read the last entry, then update a specific field (e.g., a "status" column) without overwriting the rest. Example: updating a student's attendance record.

```python
# Read the file
with open("attendance.csv", "r+") as f:
    records = f.readlines()
    # Update the 3rd student's status (index 2)
    parts = records[2].strip().split(",")
    parts[3] = "Present"               # Update status column
    records[2] = ",".join(parts) + "\n"
    # Move pointer back to the start of the 3rd line
    offset = sum(len(line) for line in records[:2])
    f.seek(offset, 0)
    f.write(records[2])                # Overwrite that line
    f.truncate()                       # Remove any leftover bytes
```

---

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

```python
# Assume "poem.txt" already contains 5 lines:
# Line 1: "Roses are red"
# Line 2: "Violets are blue"
# Line 3: "Sugar is sweet"          <-- We will overwrite this
# Line 4: "And so are you"
# Line 5: "Python is fun"

with open("poem.txt", "r+") as f:
    # ---- Inspect pointer at start ----
    print("Initial position:", f.tell())            # Output: 0

    # ---- Read lines 1 and 2 to advance pointer to start of line 3 ----
    line1 = f.readline()
    line2 = f.readline()
    print("After reading 2 lines:", f.tell())
    # Position = len(line1) + len(line2) = 14 + 16 = 30 (varies by OS)

    # ---- Mark the start of line 3 ----
    start_of_line3 = f.tell()

    # ---- Read the existing line 3 (to know its length) ----
    old_line3 = f.readline()
    print("Old line 3:", old_line3.strip())

    # ---- Rewind to the start of line 3 ----
    f.seek(start_of_line3, 0)
    print("After rewind:", f.tell())                # Back to start_of_line3

    # ---- Write the new line ----
    new_line = "Sugar is bitter\n"
    f.write(new_line)

    # ---- Optional: truncate trailing characters if new line is shorter ----
    f.truncate()

    # ---- Final pointer position ----
    print("Final position:", f.tell())
```

**Output trace (representative):**

```
Initial position: 0
After reading 2 lines: 30
Old line 3: Sugar is sweet
After rewind: 30
Final position: 43
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `f.truncate()`** when the new line is **shorter** than the old one leaves a garbled "hybrid" line (e.g., `"Sugar is bitteet"`). Always truncate after a partial in-place write.
> 2. **Confusing `tell()` semantics**: In **text mode**, `tell()` returns an **opaque cookie** (not always the exact byte count) and `seek()` is restricted to `seek(0,0)` and `seek(0,2)` in pure text mode. For precise byte-level in-place editing, **always use binary mode**.
> 3. **Missing encoding argument**: On systems with non-UTF-8 locales (e.g., legacy Windows CP1252), text mode may raise `UnicodeDecodeError` on opening. Specify `encoding="utf-8"` explicitly in the `open()` call for cross-platform statistical scripts.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Text file** stores data as **human-readable characters** (UTF-8/ASCII); **Binary file** stores data as **raw bytes** mirroring memory layout.
- The `open(path, mode)` function returns a file object; default mode is `'r'` (read text).
- **Modes `'w'` and `'a'` create the file** if it does not exist; `'w'` **truncates** existing content, `'a'` **preserves** it.
- Reading methods: `read(n)` → n chars/bytes; `readline()` → one line; `readlines()` → list of all lines.
- Writing methods: `write(s)` returns the count of chars/bytes written; `writelines(lst)` does **not** add automatic newlines.
- File pointer methods: `seek(offset, whence)` moves the pointer; `tell()` reports its position. **Text mode restricts `seek()` to `(0,0)` and `(0,2)`.**
- The `with` statement invokes the **Context Manager Protocol** (`__enter__` / `__exit__`) — **guarantees** `close()` even on exceptions.
- Forgetting `close()` causes **file descriptor leaks** and potential **data loss** (buffered writes never flushed).
- For numerical data, **binary I/O is faster** and preserves **exact float representation** (no `str→float` precision loss).
- The `struct` module provides **C-style packed binary I/O** — format codes: `i` (4-byte int), `d` (8-byte double), `<` (little-endian), `>` (big-endian).
- For big-data statistics, prefer **NumPy `.npy` / `.npz`** or **Pandas `to_pickle`** over CSV when speed and precision matter.
- Always specify `encoding="utf-8"` explicitly in `open()` for **cross-platform portability**.
- The `try/except/finally` block is an **alternative** to `with` for explicit cleanup, but `with` is **shorter and safer**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic programming experiments on Combining and Merging Datasets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic programming experiments on Combining and Merging Datasets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Combining and Merging Datasets** refers to the process of joining two or more data structures (DataFrames in Python/pandas, or data frames in R) along a particular axis or using a common key column to produce a unified dataset for analysis. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PCCBL508 – Computational Statistics Lab*, this experiment focuses on the three primary operations:

1. **`pd.concat()`** — Stacking datasets either vertically (row-wise) or horizontally (column-wise).
2. **`pd.merge()`** — SQL-style joining of datasets using one or more common keys (columns).
3. **`DataFrame.join()`** — A convenient method for joining on the **index** rather than columns.

Formally, given two datasets $A$ and $B$, the merge operation is defined as:

$$
A \bowtie_{\text{key}} B = \{ (a, b) \;|\; a \in A,\; b \in B,\; a_{\text{key}} = b_{\text{key}} \}
$$

where $\bowtie$ represents the join predicate and `key` is the common attribute used for matching.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment forms the foundation of **Data Wrangling** in the lab manual. Students are expected to demonstrate proficiency in **all three operations** along with their parameters (`how`, `on`, `left_on`, `right_on`, `axis`, `ignore_index`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two phone books**:

- **Phone Book A** contains names and mobile numbers of employees in the *Sales* department.
- **Phone Book B** contains names and landline numbers of employees in the *Sales* department.

**Combining (Concat):** Tearing out the pages of Book A and Book B and stapling them together into one fat book — page by page. This is **stacking**.

**Merging (Merge):** Looking up each name in Book A, finding the same name in Book B, and writing both numbers side-by-side on a single new page. This is **joining on a key**.

**Joining (Index Join):** The books are already alphabetized, so you simply merge the two by matching the position of the name in each book.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world engineering application:** In any data science pipeline (e.g., IoT sensor fusion, customer analytics, financial reporting), raw data lives in multiple files/databases. Combining and merging is the **first preprocessing step** before Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) or model building.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Board-Exam Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Concat** | Concatenation — gluing datasets together along an axis |
| **Merge** | SQL-style database join on common columns |
| **Join** | Convenience method for index-based joining |
| **Key Column** | The common column used to match rows across datasets |
| **Inner Join** | Keep only rows with matching keys in **both** datasets |
| **Outer Join** | Keep **all** rows from both; fill missing values with **NaN** |
| **Left Join** | Keep **all** rows from the **left** dataset |
| **Right Join** | Keep **all** rows from the **right** dataset |
| **Cross Join** | Cartesian product — every row of A paired with every row of B |
| **NaN** | **Not a Number** — pandas marker for missing/unmatched values |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — The Three Operations

### A. `pd.concat()` — Stacking Operation

`pd.concat()` performs **concatenation** along a particular axis. Think of it as a "smart stack" of DataFrames.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Accept a **list or tuple** of DataFrames as input.
2. Determine the **axis**: `axis=0` (default) → stack rows; `axis=1` → stack columns.
3. Decide **join behavior**: `join='outer'` (default, union of columns) or `join='inner'` (intersection).
4. Handle **index alignment**: Use `ignore_index=True` to renumber the rows from 0 to $n-1$.
5. Identify the source of each piece using the `keys` parameter (creates a hierarchical index).

**Why this matters:** Concat is fast, has minimal computational overhead, and does **not require** a key column. It is the workhorse for vertically appending similar data files (e.g., monthly sales files).

### B. `pd.merge()` — SQL-Style Join Operation

`pd.merge()` performs a **relational join** between two DataFrames. It is by far the most-used operation in real data wrangling.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Identify the **join key(s)** using `on`, `left_on`, `right_on`, or `left_index`/`right_index`.
2. Choose the **join type** via the `how` parameter.
3. Handle duplicate column names using the `suffixes` parameter (default: `('_x', '_y')`).
4. Optionally **validate** the join using the `validate` argument to catch many-to-many errors.
5. Return a **new** DataFrame — original DataFrames are not modified (immutable operation).

### C. `DataFrame.join()` — Index-Based Join

This is a thin wrapper around `merge` that defaults to joining on the **index**. It is most useful when your data is already indexed meaningfully (e.g., time series indexed by date).

## 2.2 The Four Standard Join Types (Set-Theoretic View)

Let $A$ and $B$ be two sets of records keyed by a common column. The join result is:

| Join Type | Symbol | Set Notation | Records Retained |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inner** | $A \cap B$ | $\{ x \;|\; x \in A \land x \in B \}$ | Only matching keys in **both** |
| **Left** | $A$ | $\{ x \;|\; x \in A \}$ | All of $A$ + matching $B$ rows |
| **Right** | $B$ | $\{ x \;|\; x \in B \}$ | All of $B$ + matching $A$ rows |
| **Outer (Full)** | $A \cup B$ | $\{ x \;|\; x \in A \lor x \in B \}$ | All of **both**, NaN for misses |

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it is asked almost every KTU lab exam!**

| Function | Parameter | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `pd.concat()` | `objs` | (required) | List/tuple of DataFrames to concatenate |
| `pd.concat()` | `axis` | `0` | `0` = row stack, `1` = column stack |
| `pd.concat()` | `join` | `'outer'` | `'inner'` keeps common columns only |
| `pd.concat()` | `ignore_index` | `False` | Reset index from 0 to $n-1$ |
| `pd.concat()` | `keys` | `None` | Hierarchical index identifying source |
| `pd.merge()` | `left` | (required) | Left DataFrame |
| `pd.merge()` | `right` | (required) | Right DataFrame |
| `pd.merge()` | `how` | `'inner'` | One of `'left'`, `'right'`, `'outer'`, `'inner'`, `'cross'` |
| `pd.merge()` | `on` | `None` | Common column name present in **both** |
| `pd.merge()` | `left_on` / `right_on` | `None` | When key names differ between DataFrames |
| `pd.merge()` | `left_index` / `right_index` | `False` | Use index as join key |
| `pd.merge()` | `suffixes` | `('_x', '_y')` | Append to overlapping column names |
| `pd.merge()` | `validate` | `None` | `one_to_one`, `one_to_many`, `many_to_one`, `many_to_many` |
| `df.join()` | `other` | (required) | Right DataFrame to join |
| `df.join()` | `how` | `'left'` | Join type |
| `df.join()` | `on` | `None` | Column in `other` to align with `df`'s index |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Merging subscriber call-detail records (CDR) with tower-location tables using cell-ID keys.
- **IoT Systems:** Combining temperature and humidity sensor logs by timestamp key.
- **Finance:** Joining daily stock prices with company fundamental data via ticker symbol.
- **Healthcare:** Merging patient demographics with lab-test results via patient-ID.
- **Retail:** Stacking monthly transaction files (`pd.concat`) and merging product metadata (`pd.merge`).

## 3.1 Setup — Importing Libraries and Creating Sample DataFrames

---

## 3.2 Experiment 1 — `pd.concat()` Operations

### Example 1.1: Vertical Stacking (default `axis=0`)

```python
result_v = pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0, ignore_index=True)
print(result_v)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID    Name   Dept  Salary  Bonus
0   101    Anil  Sales  50000.0   NaN
1   102    Bina  Sales  55000.0   NaN
2   103  Chitra  Sales  60000.0   NaN
3   104  Deepak  Sales  52000.0   NaN
4   105    Esha  Sales  58000.0   NaN
5   101    Anil  Sales     NaN 5000.0
6   102    Bina  Sales     NaN 6000.0
7   103  Chitra  Sales     NaN 7000.0
8   106   Farah  Sales     NaN 4000.0
```

**Line-by-line explanation:**

- `axis=0` → rows are stacked vertically.
- `ignore_index=True` → index runs continuously from 0 to 8.
- `Salary` is **NaN** for the bottom four rows because `df2` has no `Salary` column.
- `Bonus` is **NaN** for the top five rows because `df1` has no `Bonus` column.
- This is the default **`outer` join** behavior on columns (union).

### Example 1.2: Horizontal Stacking (`axis=1`)

```python
# Restructure to have SAME EmpIDs for clean horizontal demo
df1_demo = df1.head(3).copy()
df2_demo = df2.head(3).copy()

result_h = pd.concat([df1_demo, df2_demo], axis=1)
print(result_h)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID    Name   Dept  Salary  EmpID    Name   Dept  Bonus
0   101    Anil  Sales   50000    101    Anil  Sales   5000
1   102    Bina  Sales   55000    102    Bina  Sales   6000
2   103  Chitra  Sales   60000    103  Chitra  Sales   7000
```

**Explanation:** `axis=1` glues columns side-by-side. The index is preserved. Notice **duplicate column names** — in real code, you would handle this with `keys` or by renaming before concat.

### Example 1.3: Inner Concat (Intersection of Columns)

```python
# df1 has Salary, df2 has Bonus → intersection is empty for these columns
result_inner = pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0, join='inner', ignore_index=True)
print(result_inner)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID    Name   Dept
0   101    Anil  Sales
1   102    Bina  Sales
2   103  Chitra  Sales
3   104  Deepak  Sales
4   105    Esha  Sales
5   101    Anil  Sales
6   102    Bina  Sales
7   103  Chitra  Sales
8   106   Farah  Sales
```

**Explanation:** Only the **common** columns (`EmpID`, `Name`, `Dept`) are retained. `Salary` and `Bonus` are dropped because they do not appear in **both** DataFrames.

### Example 1.4: Concat with `keys` for Hierarchical Index

```python
result_keys = pd.concat(
    {'2023': df1, '2024': df2},
    axis=0,
    ignore_index=False
)
print(result_keys)
```

**Output:**

```
        EmpID    Name   Dept  Salary  Bonus
2023 0   101    Anil  Sales  50000.0   NaN
     1   102    Bina  Sales  55000.0   NaN
     2   103  Chitra  Sales  60000.0   NaN
     3   104  Deepak  Sales  52000.0   NaN
     4   105    Esha  Sales  58000.0   NaN
2024 0   101    Anil  Sales     NaN 5000.0
     1   102    Bina  Sales     NaN 6000.0
     2   103  Chitra  Sales     NaN 7000.0
     3   106   Farah  Sales     NaN 4000.0
```

**Explanation:** The `keys={'2023', '2024'}` argument creates a **MultiIndex** whose top level identifies the source DataFrame. This is invaluable for tracking data lineage.

---

## 3.3 Experiment 2 — `pd.merge()` Operations

### Example 2.1: Inner Join (default)

```python
merged_inner = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='EmpID', how='inner', suffixes=('_2023', '_2024'))
print(merged_inner)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID  Name_2023 Dept_2023  Salary  Name_2024 Dept_2024  Bonus
0   101       Anil     Sales   50000       Anil     Sales   5000
1   102       Bina     Sales   55000       Bina     Sales   6000
2   103     Chitra     Sales   60000     Chitra     Sales   7000
```

**Step-by-step logic:**

- `on='EmpID'` → use the `EmpID` column as the join key in both DataFrames.
- `how='inner'` → keep only rows where `EmpID` exists in **both** (101, 102, 103).
- 104, 105 (only in `df1`) and 106 (only in `df2`) are **dropped**.
- `suffixes=('_2023', '_2024')` → resolves the duplicate `Name` and `Dept` columns.

### Example 2.2: Left Join

```python
merged_left = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='EmpID', how='left', suffixes=('_2023', '_2024'))
print(merged_left)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID  Name_2023 Dept_2023  Salary  Name_2024 Dept_2024  Bonus
0   101       Anil     Sales   50000       Anil     Sales   5000
1   102       Bina     Sales   55000       Bina     Sales   6000
2   103     Chitra     Sales   60000     Chitra     Sales   7000
3   104     Deepak     Sales   52000        NaN      NaN    NaN
4   105       Esha     Sales   58000        NaN      NaN    NaN
```

**Logic:** All five rows of `df1` are retained. Rows 104 and 105 have no match in `df2`, so the right-side columns are filled with `NaN`.

### Example 2.3: Right Join

```python
merged_right = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='EmpID', how='right', suffixes=('_2023', '_2024'))
print(merged_right)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID  Name_2023 Dept_2023   Salary  Name_2024 Dept_2024  Bonus
0   101       Anil     Sales  50000.0       Anil     Sales   5000
1   102       Bina     Sales  55000.0       Bina     Sales   6000
2   103     Chitra     Sales  60000.0     Chitra     Sales   7000
3   106        NaN       NaN      NaN      Farah     Sales   4000
```

**Logic:** All four rows of `df2` are retained. Row 106 (Farah) is present even though she has no entry in `df1` → `Salary`, `Name_2023`, `Dept_2023` become `NaN`.

### Example 2.4: Outer (Full) Join

```python
merged_outer = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='EmpID', how='outer', suffixes=('_2023', '_2024'))
print(merged_outer)
```

**Output:**

```
   EmpID  Name_2023 Dept_2023   Salary  Name_2024 Dept_2024  Bonus
0   101       Anil     Sales  50000.0       Anil     Sales   5000.0
1   102       Bina     Sales  55000.0       Bina     Sales   6000.0
2   103     Chitra     Sales  60000.0     Chitra     Sales   7000.0
3   104     Deepak     Sales  52000.0        NaN      NaN     NaN
4   105       Esha     Sales  58000.0        NaN      NaN     NaN
5   106        NaN       NaN      NaN      Farah     Sales   4000.0
```

**Logic:** The **union** of both DataFrames. Every `EmpID` from either side appears exactly once. Missing values become `NaN`. This is the **safest join** because no data is silently dropped.

### Example 2.5: Merge with Different Key Names

```python
# Create DataFrames with differently-named key columns
df_customers = pd.DataFrame({
    'CustomerID' : [1, 2, 3],
    'Name'       : ['Gita', 'Hari', 'Indira'],
    'City'       : ['Kochi', 'Trivandrum', 'Kozhikode']
})

df_orders = pd.DataFrame({
    'CustID'      : [1, 2, 4],
    'OrderAmount' : [2500, 1800, 3200]
})

# Merge using left_on and right_on
merged_diff = pd.merge(df_customers, df_orders,
                       left_on='CustomerID',
                       right_on='CustID',
                       how='outer')
print(merged_diff)
```

**Output:**

```
   CustomerID  Name       City  CustID  OrderAmount
0         1.0  Gita       Kochi     1.0       2500.0
1         2.0  Hari  Trivandrum     2.0       1800.0
2         3.0 Indira  Kozhikode     NaN          NaN
3         4.0   NaN        NaN     4.0       3200.0
```

**Logic:** When the key column has **different names** in the two DataFrames, you must explicitly tell pandas using `left_on` and `right_on`. Both key columns are retained in the output (you can drop one with `.drop()`).

### Example 2.6: Merge on Index

```python
# Set EmpID as index
df1_idx = df1.set_index('EmpID')
df2_idx = df2.set_index('EmpID')

merged_idx = pd.merge(df1_idx, df2_idx, left_index=True, right_index=True, how='outer')
print(merged_idx)
```

**Output:**

```
        Name_x   Dept_x   Salary  Name_y   Dept_y   Bonus
EmpID
101       Anil    Sales  50000.0    Anil    Sales  5000.0
102       Bina    Sales  55000.0    Bina    Sales  6000.0
103     Chitra    Sales  60000.0  Chitra    Sales  7000.0
104     Deepak    Sales  52000.0     NaN      NaN     NaN
105       Esha    Sales  58000.0     NaN      NaN     NaN
106        NaN      NaN      NaN   Farah    Sales  4000.0
```

**Logic:** When your join key is the index, use `left_index=True` and `right_index=True`. This is exactly what `DataFrame.join()` does automatically.

---

## 3.4 Experiment 3 — `DataFrame.join()` Method

```python
# join() defaults to LEFT join on the calling DataFrame's index
joined = df1_idx.join(df2_idx, how='outer', lsuffix='_L', rsuffix='_R')
print(joined)
```

**Output:**

```
        Name_L Dept_L   Salary  Name_R Dept_R   Bonus
EmpID
101       Anil  Sales  50000.0    Anil  Sales  5000.0
102       Bina  Sales  55000.0    Bina  Sales  6000.0
103     Chitra  Sales  60000.0  Chitra  Sales  7000.0
104     Deepak  Sales  52000.0     NaN    NaN     NaN
105       Esha  Sales  58000.0     NaN    NaN     NaN
106        NaN    NaN      NaN   Farah  Sales  4000.0
```

**Logic:** `join()` is syntactically shorter than `merge()` for index-based joining. The default `how` is `'left'` (different from `merge()`'s default `'inner'`).

---

## 3.5 Experiment 4 — R Equivalent (For Reference)

KTU also accepts R for statistics lab. Here is the R equivalent of the same operations.

```r
# Create two data frames
df1_r <- data.frame(
  EmpID  = c(101, 102, 103, 104, 105),
  Name   = c("Anil", "Bina", "Chitra", "Deepak", "Esha"),
  Dept   = rep("Sales", 5),
  Salary = c(50000, 55000, 60000, 52000, 58000)
)

df2_r <- data.frame(
  EmpID = c(101, 102, 103, 106),
  Name  = c("Anil", "Bina", "Chitra", "Farah"),
  Dept  = rep("Sales", 4),
  Bonus = c(5000, 6000, 7000, 4000)
)

# ---- rbind : vertical stacking (requires same columns) ----
df_combined <- rbind(df1_r, df2_r)
print(df_combined)

# ---- cbind : horizontal binding (requires same row count) ----
df_horiz <- cbind(df1_r, df2_r)

# ---- merge : SQL-style join ----
merged_r <- merge(df1_r, df2_r, by = "EmpID", all = FALSE)   # inner
merged_left_r  <- merge(df1_r, df2_r, by = "EmpID", all.x = TRUE)  # left
merged_right_r <- merge(df1_r, df2_r, by = "EmpID", all.y = TRUE)  # right
merged_outer_r <- merge(df1_r, df2_r, by = "EmpID", all = TRUE)    # outer
print(merged_r)
```

**Mapping from R to pandas:**

| R | pandas |
|---|---|
| `rbind(df1, df2)` | `pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0)` |
| `cbind(df1, df2)` | `pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1)` |
| `merge(df1, df2, by="key")` | `pd.merge(df1, df2, on="key")` |
| `all = TRUE` (R) | `how='outer'` (pandas) |
| `all.x = TRUE` (R) | `how='left'` (pandas) |
| `all.y = TRUE` (R) | `how='right'` (pandas) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart — Which Operation Should I Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Two or more datasets to combine?] --> B{Is there a common<br/>key column?}
    B -- No --> C[Use pd.concat<br/>with axis=0 or axis=1]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is the key the<br/>index or a column?}
    D -- Index --> E[Use df.join<br/>or pd.merge with<br/>left_index=True]
    D -- Column --> F{Do you need to keep<br/>non-matching rows?}
    F -- No, only matches --> G[Use pd.merge with<br/>how=inner]
    F -- Yes, all from left --> H[Use pd.merge with<br/>how=left]
    F -- Yes, all from right --> I[Use pd.merge with<br/>how=right]
    F -- Yes, all from both --> J[Use pd.merge with<br/>how=outer]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Four Join Types Visualized

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Left DataFrame df1]
        A1[EmpID 101 Anil]
        A2[EmpID 102 Bina]
        A3[EmpID 103 Chitra]
        A4[EmpID 104 Deepak]
        A5[EmpID 105 Esha]
    end

    subgraph B[Right DataFrame df2]
        B1[EmpID 101 Anil]
        B2[EmpID 102 Bina]
        B3[EmpID 103 Chitra]
        B4[EmpID 106 Farah]
    end

    A1 -.matches.-> B1
    A2 -.matches.-> B2
    A3 -.matches.-> B3

    A4 -.-> X1[EXCLUDED in inner, left<br/>Kept with NaN in outer]
    A5 -.-> X2[EXCLUDED in inner, left<br/>Kept with NaN in outer]
    B4 -.-> X3[EXCLUDED in inner, right<br/>Kept with NaN in outer]
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Concat Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Pandas
    participant DF1 as DataFrame 1
    participant DF2 as DataFrame 2
    participant Result as Final DataFrame

    User->>Pandas: pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=0)
    Pandas->>DF1: Read all rows
    Pandas->>DF2: Read all rows
    Pandas->>Pandas: Align columns outer join
    Pandas->>Pandas: Reset index if ignore_index=True
    Pandas->>Result: Build new DataFrame
    Result-->>User: Return unified DataFrame
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Parameter Logic of `pd.merge()`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([pd.merge left, right]) --> Key{Common<br/>key column?}
    Key -- Yes --> On[on parameter]
    Key -- No --> Names[Different names?]
    Names -- Yes --> LonRon[left_on<br/>and right_on]
    Names -- No --> Index[Use index?]
    Index -- Yes --> Idx[left_index<br/>and right_index]
    Index -- No --> Cross[how=cross]
    On --> How{Join type?}
    LonRon --> How
    Idx --> How
    How -- inner --> I[Only matches]
    How -- left --> L[All left + matches]
    How -- right --> R[All right + matches]
    How -- outer --> O[All from both]
    How -- cross --> C[Cartesian product]
    I --> Out([Return merged DataFrame])
    L --> Out
    R --> Out
    O --> Out
    C --> Out
```
