```mermaid
    STAGE1 --> STAGE2 --> STAGE3 --> STAGE4 --> STAGE5 --> STAGE6 --> STAGE7
    style OUT fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric-and-prefixed (e.g., `STAGE1`, `P01`, `OUT`) and node labels contain no markdown formatting — this complies with the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE v10.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Most students lose marks in this experiment by **(a)** forgetting the `.xlsx` engine — writing `pd.read_excel("data.xlsx")` without `pip install openpyxl` first, **(b)** confusing `sep` with `delimiter` (they are aliases; write one), **(c)** using `index_col` *and* `usecols` on the same column name (the column is consumed by `index_col` and is no longer in `usecols`), and **(d)** writing `df.shape()` instead of `df.shape` (shape is a *property*, not a method). Marks are also lost for not producing the *output* of `df.head()` in the lab record.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Write a Python statement to read the file `students.csv` into a pandas DataFrame named `df`, treating the column `"RollNo"` as the row index. Also write a statement to display the first three rows.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("students.csv", index_col="RollNo")
print(df.head(3))
```

**Mark Split:**
- `import pandas as pd` → **0.5 mark**
- Correct use of `read_csv` with file name → **1.0 mark**
- Correct use of `index_col` argument → **1.0 mark**
- Correct use of `head(3)` → **0.5 mark**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between `pd.read_csv()` and `pd.read_excel()` with respect to (i) the file format they accept, (ii) the key parameter that selects the worksheet, and (iii) the default engine requirement for `.xlsx` files.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `pd.read_csv()` | `pd.read_excel()` |
|---|---|---|
| (i) File format | Plain text, delimiter-separated (typically `.csv`) | Binary zipped XML workbook (`.xlsx`, `.xls`) |
| (ii) Sheet selection | Not applicable; single "sheet" of rows | `sheet_name` (default `0`, accepts string or list) |
| (iii) Default engine | Built-in C parser | `openpyxl` for `.xlsx`; must be installed via `pip` |

**Mark Split:** 1 mark per correct row of the comparison.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> Per **KTU 2024 ESE regulations**, Module 2 carries one 14-mark question with internal choice. The full pattern is: **Part (a) 7 marks** + **Part (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]*

**(a)** Given the file `sales.csv` with columns `OrderID, Region, Product, Units, Revenue`, and a known issue that missing values in the `Revenue` column are stored as the string `"?"`, write the complete Python code (with appropriate parameters) to load this file into a DataFrame named `sales_df` such that:
   - `OrderID` becomes the row index,
   - `Units` is read as `int32`,
   - `Revenue` is read as `float64`,
   - the literal string `"?"` is treated as `NaN`,
   - only the columns `OrderID, Product, Units, Revenue` are imported (drop `Region`).

   **Show the output of `sales_df.info()` and `sales_df.head(3)`.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain, with a labeled diagram, the seven internal stages of the pandas ingestion pipeline. For each stage, name at least one `read_csv` parameter that controls it. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

```python
import pandas as pd

sales_df = pd.read_csv(
    "sales.csv",
    usecols   = ["OrderID", "Product", "Units", "Revenue"],
    index_col = "OrderID",
    dtype     = {"Units": "int32", "Revenue": "float64"},
    na_values = ["?"]
)

print(sales_df.info())
print(sales_df.head(3))
```

**Expected output (illustrative):**

```
<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
Index: 1000 entries, ORD001 to ORD1000
Data columns (total 3 columns):
 #   Column   Non-Null Count  Dtype
---  ------   --------------  -----
 0   Product  1000 non-null   object
 1   Units    980 non-null    int32
 2   Revenue  950 non-null    float64
dtypes: float64(1), int32(1), object(1)
memory usage: ~32.0 KB
```

```
        Product  Units  Revenue
OrderID
ORD001    Mouse      3   450.00
ORD002  Monitor      1   980.00
ORD003   Tablet      2     NaN
```

**Mark Split:**
- Correct import and `read_csv` call: **2 marks**
- Correct `usecols` and `index_col`: **2 marks**
- Correct `dtype` and `na_values`: **2 marks**
- Correct inspection calls and their conceptual outputs: **1 mark**

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

The seven stages (with controlling parameters) are:

1. **File Resolution & Opening** — controlled by `encoding` (CSV) or `sheet_name` and engine (XLSX).
2. **Stream / Chunk Parsing** — controlled by `sep`, `nrows`, `chunksize`.
3. **Header Inference & Column Naming** — controlled by `header`, `names`, `usecols`.
4. **Type Inference** — controlled by `dtype`, `low_memory`.
5. **Index Construction** — controlled by `index_col`.
6. **NaN Sentinel Substitution** — controlled by `na_values`, `keep_default_na`.
7. **DataFrame Materialization** — final assembly, no parameter; returns the in-memory object.

(Diagrammatic representation — refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1.)

**Mark Split:** 1 mark per stage correctly named and mapped to a valid parameter.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** You are given a workbook `results.xlsx` containing two worksheets: `"Theory"` and `"Lab"`. Each sheet has columns `RollNo, Name, Total, Grade`. Write Python code to:
   - Read *both* sheets into a single Python object (a dictionary),
   - Extract the `Theory` sheet into `theory_df` and the `Lab` sheet into `lab_df`,
   - Display the shape of each DataFrame,
   - Concatenate them vertically into a combined DataFrame `combined_df` with a new column `"Source"` indicating the originating sheet.

   **Show the code and the first three rows of `combined_df`.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 50 MB CSV file `sensor_logs.csv` cannot be loaded entirely into RAM on your machine. Describe, with code, two distinct pandas-based strategies to handle this situation during the read step itself. Justify which is more memory-efficient. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

```python
import pandas as pd

# Step 1: Read both sheets into a dict
all_sheets = pd.read_excel("results.xlsx", sheet_name=["Theory", "Lab"])

# Step 2: Extract each DataFrame
theory_df = all_sheets["Theory"]
lab_df    = all_sheets["Lab"]

# Step 3: Display shapes
print("Theory shape:", theory_df.shape)
print("Lab shape:   ", lab_df.shape)

# Step 4: Vertical concat with source tagging
theory_tagged = theory_df.assign(Source="Theory")
lab_tagged    = lab_df.assign(Source="Lab")
combined_df   = pd.concat([theory_tagged, lab_tagged], ignore_index=True)

print(combined_df.head(3))
```

**Mark Split:**
- `sheet_name` as list and dict extraction: **2 marks**
- Shape display: **1 mark**
- `.assign("Source")` and `pd.concat`: **3 marks**
- Output printout: **1 mark**

##### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Strategy 1: Chunked reading with `chunksize`**

```python
import pandas as pd

chunks = pd.read_csv("sensor_logs.csv", chunksize=10000)
total_rows = 0
for chunk in chunks:
    total_rows += len(chunk)
    # process chunk.statistics() etc.
print("Total rows:", total_rows)
```

**Logical justification:** Only one chunk of 10,000 rows is in memory at any time. Peak RAM ≈ `10000 * m * 8 bytes`.

**Strategy 2: Column-restricted reading with `usecols`**

```python
df = pd.read_csv("sensor_logs.csv", usecols=["timestamp", "value"])
```

**Logical justification:** Discarding columns reduces $m$ → smaller $n \cdot m$ product → less memory. Also reduces disk I/O.

**Strategy 3 (Bonus): Sampling with `nrows` + `skiprows`**

```python
df = pd.read_csv("sensor_logs.csv", nrows=1000)
```

**Comparison table for the viva:**

| Strategy | Memory Footprint | I/O Cost | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `chunksize` | $O(k \cdot m)$ where $k$ = chunk size | Full file read | Aggregation over whole file |
| `usecols` | $O(n \cdot m')$ where $m' < m$ | Full file read | Many unneeded columns |
| `nrows` | $O(k \cdot m)$ | Partial file read | Quick exploration |

**Mark Split:** 2 marks per strategy with code, 1 mark for the comparative justification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 PCCBL508 Module 2**

- **Canonical imports:** `import pandas as pd` and `import numpy as np` (the latter is implicit via pandas).
- **Two key functions:** `pd.read_csv()` and `pd.read_excel()`. Memorize the parameter table in Section 2.3.
- **Default delimiter for CSV:** comma. Default header row: row `0`.
- **Default sheet for XLSX:** first sheet (index `0`). Use a string for named sheets.
- **Engine for `.xlsx`:** `openpyxl` — must be installed via `pip install openpyxl`.
- **NaN handling:** `na_values=[...]` lets you declare *custom* missing-value sentinels like `"?"`, `"NA"`, `"null"`.
- **Index promotion:** `index_col` *moves* a column into the index; the column disappears from the data columns. Therefore do not also list it in `usecols`.
- **Inspection trio:** Always use `df.head()`, `df.info()`, and `df.shape` after every read.
- **Memory math:** Approximate `bytes ≈ n × m × dtype_size`. For `float64`, multiply by 8.
- **Chunked reading:** Use `chunksize` for files too large for RAM; iterate with a `for` loop.
- **Multi-sheet Excel:** `sheet_name=[…]` returns a `dict`; `sheet_name="Name"` returns a `DataFrame`.
- **Lab record must contain:** the import line, the read call with all parameters, and the *actual output* of at least one inspection call — not just the code.
- **Viva one-liner:** *"Reading a file is a seven-stage pipeline, and every parameter of `read_*` controls exactly one of those stages."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments to understand the difference between pandas Series and pandas Dataframe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments to understand the difference between pandas Series and pandas Dataframe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **pandas** library (built on top of NumPy), the two primary data structures used for statistical computation are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **pandas `Series`**: A **one-dimensional** labeled array capable of holding any data type (integers, floats, strings, Python objects, etc.). It is essentially a **single column** of data with an associated **index** (row labels) and a single **dtype** (data type). Mathematically, it can be viewed as a vector $\mathbf{S} = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_n\}$ paired with an index vector $\mathbf{I} = \{i_1, i_2, \dots, i_n\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **pandas `DataFrame`**: A **two-dimensional**, size-mutable, tabular data structure with **labeled axes** (rows and columns). It can be thought of as a **dictionary of Series objects**, where each column is a Series, all sharing a common row index. Formally, a DataFrame $\mathbf{D}$ is an ordered collection of $m$ columns and $n$ rows, with row index $\mathbf{I}_r$ and column index $\mathbf{I}_c$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **Excel Spreadsheet**:

| Analogy Element | Real-world Object | pandas Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| One single column of marks | A vertical list of numbers with student names | **`Series`** |
| The full marksheet (name + 5 subjects) | Rows = students, Columns = subjects | **`DataFrame`** |
| Row numbers in the left margin | The leftmost index column | **`index`** (axis 0) |
| Headers (Maths, Physics, …) | Column letters A, B, C | **`columns`** (axis 1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary**: A `Series` is **ONE column** with row labels. A `DataFrame` is the **whole table** — multiple columns stacked side by side, where each column is itself a `Series`. A DataFrame is therefore a **container of Series**.

### 1.3 Dimensionality and Key Terminology

- **Dimensionality**: `Series` has **1 axis** (rows only). `DataFrame` has **2 axes** (rows + columns).
- **Homogeneity**: A `Series` holds **one dtype** for all values. A `DataFrame` can have **different dtypes per column** (e.g., column 1 = int, column 2 = string, column 3 = float).
- **Standard size attribute**: `Series.size` and `DataFrame.size` both return total number of elements, but `DataFrame.shape` returns a **tuple** `(rows, columns)` while `Series.shape` returns a **single integer tuple** `(n,)`.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

### 2.1 Construction Logic (Step-by-Step)

**Creating a Series:**
1. The constructor `pd.Series(data, index, dtype, name)` is invoked.
2. `data` may be a list, scalar, dict, or NumPy ndarray.
3. If `index` is not supplied, pandas auto-generates a `RangeIndex` from `0` to `n-1`.
4. A single `name` attribute can be assigned (useful when the Series is later a column of a DataFrame).

**Creating a DataFrame:**
1. The constructor `pd.DataFrame(data, index, columns, dtype)` is invoked.
2. `data` may be a dict of lists/Series, a 2D ndarray, or another DataFrame.
3. When `data` is a **dict**, the **keys** become column labels and **values** become column data.
4. Internally, pandas stores the DataFrame as a **block manager** — each column of the same dtype is grouped into a NumPy block for vectorized operations.

### 2.2 Why the Distinction Matters in Computational Statistics

- Most **statistical operations** (mean, median, std, var) are defined on **1D arrays** → operate naturally on `Series`.
- A `DataFrame` applies these operations **column-wise by default** (`axis=0`), returning a `Series` of statistics.
- Selecting one column from a DataFrame (e.g., `df['Age']`) returns a `Series` — this is a critical transition students must internalize.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the most-asked distinction in KTU viva and exams.**

| Attribute / Feature | **`pd.Series`** | **`pd.DataFrame`** |
|---|---|---|
| Dimensionality | **1-D** | **2-D** |
| Number of axes | 1 (`index`) | 2 (`index`, `columns`) |
| `shape` returns | `(n,)` | `(rows, columns)` |
| `ndim` | **1** | **2** |
| Data homogeneity | Single dtype across all values | One dtype **per column** |
| Analogy | One column of Excel | Full Excel sheet |
| Internally | A values ndarray + an index ndarray | A dict-like container of Series |
| Constructor | `pd.Series(data)` | `pd.DataFrame(data)` |
| Selecting one column | N/A (it *is* the column) | `df['col']` returns a `Series` |
| Statistical default axis | Operates on the only axis | Default `axis=0` (column-wise) |
| `size` attribute | Total number of elements | Total elements (rows × cols) |
| Returned type after `df.mean()` | N/A | A `Series` of column means |
| Returned type after `df['col'].mean()` | A scalar (Python float) | N/A |
| `name` attribute | Yes (single string) | Per-column names (the `columns` index) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering**: Storing sensor readings — a `Series` holds the stream of temperature values; a `DataFrame` holds (sensor\_id, time, temperature, humidity).
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Vibration analysis — a `Series` for one axis acceleration; a `DataFrame` for tri-axial (X, Y, Z) data over time.
- **Electrical Engineering**: Power system logs — `DataFrame` with columns \[timestamp, voltage, current, power\_factor, frequency\].
- **Data Science/ML**: The `DataFrame` is the **de facto** data structure; each column (feature) is internally a `Series`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Experiments & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is a **lab course (PCCBL508)**, the analytical derivation is replaced by **executable code experiments** that empirically prove the differences. **Run these in Jupyter Notebook or any Python IDE with pandas installed.**

### Experiment A — Construction & Type Verification

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# --- Series construction ---
marks = pd.Series([85, 90, 78, 92], 
                  index=['A', 'B', 'C', 'D'], 
                  name='Maths_Marks')
print("Type of Series object :", type(marks))
print("Dimensions (ndim)     :", marks.ndim)
print("Shape                  :", marks.shape)
print("Size                   :", marks.size)
print("Dtype                  :", marks.dtype)
print("Name                   :", marks.name)
print(marks)
```

**Expected Output (line-by-line interpretation):**
- `type(marks)` → `<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'>` → proves it is 1-D.
- `marks.ndim` → `1` → confirms one axis.
- `marks.shape` → `(4,)` → a 1-tuple (one dimension).
- `marks.dtype` → `int64` → homogeneous integer storage.

```python
# --- DataFrame construction ---
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name'  : ['Anu', 'Ben', 'Cyril', 'Dia'],
    'Maths' : [85, 90, 78, 92],
    'Physics': [88, 76, 95, 80]
}, index=['R1', 'R2', 'R3', 'R4'])

print("Type of DataFrame     :", type(df))
print("Dimensions (ndim)     :", df.ndim)
print("Shape                  :", df.shape)
print("Size                   :", df.size)
print("Column dtypes          :\n", df.dtypes)
print(df)
```

**Expected Output (interpretation):**
- `type(df)` → `<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>` → 2-D tabular.
- `df.ndim` → `2` → two axes.
- `df.shape` → `(4, 3)` → 4 rows × 3 columns.
- `df.size` → `12` → total cell count (4 × 3 = 12).
- `df.dtypes` shows **mixed dtypes**: `Name` → object (string), `Maths` → int64, `Physics` → int64.

### Experiment B — Empirical Proof that a DataFrame Column IS a Series

```python
col = df['Maths']          # extract single column
print("Type of extracted column :", type(col))
print("Is it a Series?           :", isinstance(col, pd.Series))
print(col)
```

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. `df['Maths']` accesses the column labeled `'Maths'`.
2. The returned object is **not** a list or ndarray — it is a `pd.Series`.
3. This empirically confirms the rule: **"A DataFrame column is a Series."**

### Experiment C — Statistical Operation Behaviour

```python
# Series mean
print("Series mean :", marks.mean())          # scalar
print("Type        :", type(marks.mean()))    # numpy.float64

# DataFrame mean (column-wise by default)
print("\nDataFrame column-wise mean :")
print(df.mean(numeric_only=True))
print("Type of df.mean() result    :", type(df.mean(numeric_only=True)))
```

**Interpretation:**
- `marks.mean()` returns a **scalar** (single number, numpy float).
- `df.mean()` returns a **`Series`** where the index is the column names and the values are the column means.
- The string column `'Name'` is **automatically excluded** (since `numeric_only=True`).

### Experiment D — Mathematical Equivalence Proof (Mean Formula)

For a `Series` $\mathbf{S} = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$, the arithmetic mean computed by pandas is:

$$\bar{s} \;=\; \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i$$

For our `marks` Series with values $\{85, 90, 78, 92\}$ and $n = 4$:

$$
\bar{s} \;=\; \frac{85 + 90 + 78 + 92}{4}
$$

$$
\bar{s} \;=\; \frac{345}{4} \;=\; 86.25
$$

The pandas result `marks.mean()` will output **86.25**, matching the formula exactly.

For a `DataFrame`, the same formula is applied **per column independently**. For the column `Maths`:

$$
\bar{m}_{\text{Maths}} \;=\; \frac{85 + 90 + 78 + 92}{4} \;=\; 86.25
$$

For the column `Physics` with values $\{88, 76, 95, 80\}$:

$$
\bar{m}_{\text{Physics}} \;=\; \frac{88 + 76 + 95 + 80}{4} \;=\; \frac{339}{4} \;=\; 84.75
$$

So `df.mean()` returns a `Series` like: `Maths → 86.25, Physics → 84.75`.

### Experiment E — Axis Parameter Demonstrates the Structural Difference

```python
# Sum along axis=0 (default → column sums)
print("Column sums (axis=0) :\n", df.sum(numeric_only=True))

# Sum along axis=1 (row sums → result is a Series indexed by row labels)
print("\nRow sums (axis=1) :")
row_sum = df[['Maths', 'Physics']].sum(axis=1)
print(row_sum)
print("Type of row_sum    :", type(row_sum))
```

**Interpretation:**
- `axis=0` collapses **rows** → result has length = number of columns.
- `axis=1` collapses **columns** → result has length = number of rows, returned as a `Series`.
- The **existence of `axis`** is itself proof that a DataFrame is 2-D; a Series has no `axis` parameter.

### Experiment F — Homogeneity Test

```python
# Series is homogeneous
s_mixed_attempt = pd.Series([1, 'two', 3.0])
print("Series dtype when mixed :", s_mixed_attempt.dtype)
# Output: object → all values upcast to a common 'object' type
```

```python
# DataFrame supports heterogeneous dtypes per column
df_hetero = pd.DataFrame({
    'Integers' : [1, 2, 3],
    'Floats'   : [1.1, 2.2, 3.3],
    'Strings'  : ['a', 'b', 'c']
})
print("DataFrame dtypes :\n", df_hetero.dtypes)
# integers → int64, Floats → float64, Strings → object (3 different dtypes!)
```

**Conclusion:** `Series` → **1 dtype**; `DataFrame` → **multiple dtypes, one per column**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Relationship Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["NumPy ndarray: 1D raw array of values"] --> B["pandas Series: 1D + Index + Name + dtype"]
    C["NumPy ndarray: 1D raw array of values"] --> D["pandas Series: 1D + Index + Name + dtype"]
    E["NumPy ndarray: 1D raw array of values"] --> F["pandas Series: 1D + Index + Name + dtype"]
    B --> G["pandas DataFrame: 2D table composed of multiple Series sharing a row index"]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Row Index axis 0: shared labels"]
    G --> I["Column Index axis 1: unique labels per Series"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DataFrame

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph df["DataFrame: students_marks"]
        row0["Row Index R1"] --> colName["Column: Name → dtype object"]
        row0 --> colMaths["Column: Maths → dtype int64"]
        row0 --> colPhysics["Column: Physics → dtype int64"]
        row1["Row Index R2"] --> colName
        row1 --> colMaths
        row1 --> colPhysics
        row2["Row Index R3"] --> colName
        row2 --> colMaths
        row2 --> colPhysics
        row3["Row Index R4"] --> colName
        row3 --> colMaths
        row3 --> colPhysics
    end
    colName -.is a.-> S1["pd.Series object"]
    colMaths -.is a.-> S2["pd.Series object"]
    colPhysics -.is a.-> S3["pd.Series object"]
```

### 4.3 Dimension Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    P["Python list / 1D ndarray"] --> S["pd.Series: shape = n, ndim = 1, axes = 1"]
    P2["2D ndarray / dict of lists"] --> DF["pd.DataFrame: shape = rows, columns, ndim = 2, axes = 2"]
    S -->|becomes a column| DF
    DF -->|df subset col| S
```

### 4.4 Type-Transition Flowchart for Statistical Operations

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["pd.Series input"] --> B["marks.mean / std / var"]
    B --> C["Scalar float output"]
    D["pd.DataFrame input"] --> E["df.mean axis equals 0 default"]
    E --> F["pd.Series output: one stat per column"]
    D --> G["df.mean axis equals 1"]
    G --> H["pd.Series output: one stat per row"]
    F -->|mean equals scalar| C
    H -->|mean equals scalar| C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of Diagram 4.4:** The output type of a statistical operation **depends on the input dimensionality and the `axis` argument**. This is a frequent viva question.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Pattern]**
*Define pandas Series and pandas DataFrame. State any three differences between them.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as: 1 for each definition, 1 for three differences):**

A **pandas Series** is a **one-dimensional labeled array** that can hold any data type and has a single index (axis 0). Its shape is a 1-tuple like `(n,)` and its `ndim` is `1`.

A **pandas DataFrame** is a **two-dimensional labeled tabular data structure** with both row index (axis 0) and column index (axis 1). Its `shape` is a 2-tuple `(rows, columns)` and its `ndim` is `2`.

**Three key differences:**

| # | Series | DataFrame |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dimensionality = **1** | Dimensionality = **2** |
| 2 | Stores **one dtype** for all values | Stores **separate dtype per column** |
| 3 | `df['col']` returns a Series | Constructed from a dict of Series/lists |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*What is the output of `type(df['Maths'])` where `df` is a DataFrame? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The expression `df['Maths']` extracts the column labeled `'Maths'` from the DataFrame `df`. The output of `type(df['Maths'])` is `<class 'pandas.core.series.Series'>`.

**Justification:** A DataFrame is internally a dictionary-like container of Series objects, where each column is a Series sharing the same row index. Therefore, selecting a single column always returns a `pd.Series` object — this is a fundamental property of pandas and is verified by `isinstance(df['Maths'], pd.Series) → True`.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question carries **7 + 7 = 14 marks** distributed across two sub-parts.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model ESE Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a suitable Python code example, demonstrate the creation of a `pandas Series` using (i) a Python list and (ii) a Python dictionary. Show how to access elements using both default and custom index labels. Also, explain why a Series is said to be "homogeneous" in nature.

**Model Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd

# (i) Creation from a Python list
marks_list = [85, 90, 78, 92]
s1 = pd.Series(marks_list)
print("Series from list with default index:")
print(s1)
# Default index: 0, 1, 2, 3 (auto-generated RangeIndex)
# Output:
# 0    85
# 1    90
# 2    78
# 3    92

# Custom index for the same list
s1_custom = pd.Series(marks_list, index=['A', 'B', 'C', 'D'], name='Maths')
print("\nSeries from list with custom index:")
print(s1_custom)

# Access by position (default integer index)
print("\nValue at position 2 :", s1[2])        # 78

# Access by label (custom index)
print("Value at label 'B'   :", s1_custom['B']) # 90

# (ii) Creation from a Python dictionary
marks_dict = {'Maths': 90, 'Physics': 85, 'Chemistry': 88}
s2 = pd.Series(marks_dict)
print("\nSeries from dictionary:")
print(s2)
# Dictionary keys become the index automatically
# Maths       90
# Physics     85
# Chemistry   88
```

**Why Series is homogeneous (2 marks for the explanation):**
A Series stores all its values in a single contiguous NumPy block, and every value in that block must share **one common `dtype`**. If the input data contains mixed types, pandas **upcasts** all values to the most general compatible type (typically `object`), but they are still stored under **one dtype** attribute. For example, a Series of `[1, 'two', 3.0]` gets `dtype = object`. In contrast, a DataFrame can have a **different dtype per column**, so it is **heterogeneous across columns**.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Construct a pandas `DataFrame` for the following student record and demonstrate the following operations, clearly stating the **type** of the result returned in each case:

| Name | Maths | Physics | Chemistry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anu | 85 | 88 | 90 |
| Ben | 90 | 76 | 85 |
| Cyril | 78 | 95 | 80 |

Operations: (i) Print `df.shape`, `df.ndim`, and `df.dtypes`. (ii) Compute the **mean of the Maths column**. (iii) Compute the **column-wise mean** of the entire DataFrame and state the return type.

**Model Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Name'     : ['Anu', 'Ben', 'Cyril'],
    'Maths'    : [85, 90, 78],
    'Physics'  : [88, 76, 95],
    'Chemistry': [90, 85, 80]
})

# (i) Structural attributes
print("df.shape :", df.shape)      # (3, 4)   → 2-D tuple
print("df.ndim  :", df.ndim)       # 2
print("df.dtypes:\n", df.dtypes)
# Name        object
# Maths        int64
# Physics      int64
# Chemistry    int64
```

**(ii) Mean of the Maths column:**

$$ \bar{m}_{\text{Maths}} \;=\; \frac{85 + 90 + 78}{3} \;=\; \frac{253}{3} \;\approx\; 84.33 $$

```python
maths_mean = df['Maths'].mean()
print("Maths mean :", maths_mean)        # 84.333...
print("Type        :", type(maths_mean)) # numpy.float64 (scalar)
```

**(iii) Column-wise mean of entire DataFrame:**

```python
col_means = df.mean(numeric_only=True)
print(col_means)
# Maths       84.333333
# Physics     86.333333
# Chemistry   85.000000
print("Type :", type(col_means))   # pandas.core.series.Series
```

**Return Type Summary Table (to be written in the exam for full marks):**

| Operation | Returned Type |
|---|---|
| `df.shape` | `tuple` → `(3, 4)` |
| `df.ndim` | `int` → `2` |
| `df.dtypes` | `pandas Series` (of dtypes) |
| `df['Maths'].mean()` | `numpy.float64` (scalar) |
| `df.mean(numeric_only=True)` | `pandas Series` (indexed by column names) |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model ESE Question, June 2024 Pattern]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with code the concept that **"a DataFrame is a container of Series objects"**. Prove this by (i) constructing a DataFrame from three separate Series, (ii) extracting a column and showing its type is a Series, and (iii) performing a vectorized arithmetic operation on the extracted column.

**Model Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd

# (i) Construct three Series sharing a common index
names   = pd.Series(['Anu', 'Ben', 'Cyril'], name='Name', 
                     index=['s1', 's2', 's3'])
maths   = pd.Series([85, 90, 78], name='Maths',
                     index=['s1', 's2', 's3'])
physics = pd.Series([88, 76, 95], name='Physics',
                     index=['s1', 's2', 's3'])

# Combine into a DataFrame using a dict
df = pd.DataFrame({'Name': names, 'Maths': maths, 'Physics': physics})
print(df)
```

This proves that **a DataFrame is built by combining Series objects** — the dict keys become column labels and the Series values become columns.

```python
# (ii) Extract a column and check type
m = df['Maths']
print("Type of df['Maths'] :", type(m))         # pd.Series
print("Is it a Series?     :", isinstance(m, pd.Series))  # True
```

```python
# (iii) Vectorized arithmetic on the extracted Series (no loop needed)
bonus = m + 5              # adds 5 to every element
print("Maths + 5 bonus marks :")
print(bonus)
# s1    90
# s2    95
# s3    83
```

**Explanation:** A vectorized operation on a Series uses NumPy under the hood — the operation is broadcast across the entire array in a single C-level call, making it orders of magnitude faster than a Python `for` loop. This is one of the chief reasons pandas is preferred for statistical data analysis.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between `pd.Series` and `pd.DataFrame` along the dimensions: **(i) Dimensionality & `shape`, (ii) `dtype` homogeneity vs heterogeneity, (iii) presence of `columns` attribute, (iv) behaviour of the `.mean()` method, and (v) the `axis` parameter for row-wise vs column-wise operations.** Provide one-line illustrative code for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Dimensionality & `shape`:**

```python
s = pd.Series([1, 2, 3])
print(s.ndim, s.shape)     # 1  (3,)

df = pd.DataFrame({'A': [1,2], 'B': [3,4]})
print(df.ndim, df.shape)   # 2  (2, 2)
```

**(ii) `dtype` homogeneity vs heterogeneity:**

```python
print(s.dtype)             # int64  (one dtype)
print(df.dtypes)
# A    int64
# B    int64
# (Different columns can have different dtypes in a DataFrame)
```

**(iii) Presence of `columns` attribute:**

```python
# s.columns  →  AttributeError (Series has no columns)
# df.columns →  Index(['A', 'B'], dtype='object')
```

**(iv) Behaviour of `.mean()`:**

```python
print(s.mean())            # 2.0  (scalar float)
print(type(s.mean()))      # numpy.float64

print(df.mean())
# A    1.5
# B    3.5
print(type(df.mean()))     # pandas.core.series.Series
```

**(v) `axis` parameter:**

```python
print(df.sum(axis=0))   # column sums → A: 3, B: 7
print(df.sum(axis=1))   # row sums    → 0: 3, 1: 7
# axis does not exist for a Series (it is 1-D)
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Writing `shape = (n,)` for a DataFrame** — A DataFrame ALWAYS has 2 dimensions, so its shape must be `(rows, columns)`. Saying `(n,)` loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Claiming a DataFrame is homogeneous** — It is **heterogeneous** across columns; only each individual column is homogeneous. Confusing these is a top-priority error.
> 3. **Forgetting to import pandas as pd** — Code without `import pandas as pd` is treated as incomplete and penalized.
> 4. **Using `df.mean()` without `numeric_only=True`** in older pandas versions raises a `TypeError` for string columns. In the exam, ALWAYS write `df.mean(numeric_only=True)` for safety.
> 5. **Calling `df.sum()` and forgetting to mention the return type** — The examiner awards 1 mark specifically for stating that the result is a `Series` (or scalar, for a Series input). Always **state the return type explicitly**.
> 6. **Confusing `size` and `shape`** — `df.size` = total cell count (e.g., 12 for a 4×3 DataFrame); `df.shape` = `(4, 3)`. Mixing them up is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **`Series` = 1-D**, **`DataFrame` = 2-D** — the most fundamental difference.
- [x] `Series.ndim = 1`, `DataFrame.ndim = 2`.
- [x] `Series.shape = (n,)` (1-tuple); `DataFrame.shape = (r, c)` (2-tuple).
- [x] `Series` is **homogeneous** (one dtype); `DataFrame` is **heterogeneous across columns** (one dtype per column).
- [x] **A DataFrame column IS a Series** — proven by `df['col']` returning a `pd.Series`.
- [x] A DataFrame is built from a **dict of Series** (or dict of lists / 2D ndarray).
- [x] `df.mean()` returns a `Series`; `s.mean()` (on a Series) returns a **scalar** (numpy float).
- [x] `axis=0` operates **column-wise** (collapse rows); `axis=1` operates **row-wise** (collapse columns). Only DataFrame has the `axis` parameter.
- [x] `df.size = rows × columns`; `s.size = number of elements`.
- [x] A Series has **one index** (axis 0); a DataFrame has **two indices**: row index (axis 0) and column index (axis 1, accessed via `df.columns`).
- [x] The string `name` attribute of a Series becomes the column label when the Series is inserted into a DataFrame.
- [x] Statistical formulas on a Series use the standard $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ definition; on a DataFrame, the formula is applied **per column** by default.
- [x] Always include `import pandas as pd` at the top of every lab record.
- [x] In viva, be ready to answer: *"If `df` is a DataFrame, what is the type of `df['col']`?"* → **`pd.Series`**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments on pandas Dataframe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments on pandas Dataframe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧭 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU-Aligned):** A *pandas DataFrame* is a **two-dimensional, size-mutable, tabular data structure** with **labeled axes (rows and columns)** that forms the primary data abstraction object of the *pandas* library in Python. It is conceptually a dict-like container of `Series` objects, where each column shares a common row index and may hold heterogeneous data types (numeric, string, datetime, categorical, etc.).

In the context of **PCCBL508 – Computational Statistics Lab**, a DataFrame is treated as the **canonical in-memory representation** upon which all descriptive statistics, transformations, aggregations, and visualisations are performed before any inferential or predictive modelling step.

### 1.1.1 The Three Pillars of a DataFrame

| Pillar | Meaning | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| **Index (Axis 0)** | Row labels (default: `0, 1, 2, … n-1`) | *row index* |
| **Columns (Axis 1)** | Column labels (the *variables* of a dataset) | *feature vector / variable names* |
| **Values (data)** | The actual 2D block of entries (NumPy-backed) | *observation matrix* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Every DataFrame question in the lab exam expects you to be fluent with **three** core artefacts: the `index`, the `columns` object, and the underlying NumPy `values` array. Examiners frequently award marks just for correctly retrieving these three attributes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Excel Workbook on Steroids"

Imagine you walk into a **registrar's office** and ask for the mark list of a B.Tech batch. What you receive is a printed sheet with:

- A **sl.no** column on the left → that is the **index**.
- Headings across the top: `Roll No`, `Name`, `DOB`, `SGPA`, `CGPA` → those are the **columns**.
- A rectangular grid of cells filled in row-by-row → those are the **values**.

A pandas DataFrame is *exactly* that sheet, but it lives inside your Python kernel, supports **vectorised arithmetic**, handles **missing values gracefully**, and can be sliced, joined, pivoted, and aggregated in a single expression. It is to a statistician what a *stethoscope* is to a cardiologist — the **primary instrument** of measurement and analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Trick:** Whenever you feel lost inside pandas, mentally translate it to Excel:
> - `df.head()`  ↔  "Show me the first few rows"
> - `df['SGPA']`  ↔  "Click on column SGPA"
> - `df.loc[10]`  ↔  "Jump to row number 10 by label"
> - `df.groupby('Branch')`  ↔  "Make a Pivot Table by Branch"

---

## 1.3 Why DataFrame is Central to Computational Statistics

In a typical lab experiment cycle, the pipeline always looks like:

$$
\text{Raw Data} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{pd.read\_csv()}\;}\; \text{DataFrame} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{clean/transform}\;}\; \text{DataFrame} \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{stats/model}\;}\; \text{Insight}
$$

Every statistical function in `scipy.stats`, `statsmodels`, and `sklearn` ultimately expects data to arrive as a **DataFrame or NumPy array**. Hence, mastering DataFrame manipulation is the **prerequisite gate** for all subsequent experiments in PCCBL508.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🧪 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy — How a DataFrame is Built Internally

Under the hood, a DataFrame is a **dict of Series objects** sharing a common `Index`. Each `Series` is itself a 1-D NumPy array with a `dtype`. The DataFrame does *not* store a 2-D array directly; it stores a **column-major block manager** (`BlockManager`) that groups columns of the same `dtype` into contiguous NumPy blocks for cache-friendly computation.

**Logical flow of construction:**

1. **Inputs accepted:** `dict` of 1-D arrays/lists, 2-D `numpy.ndarray`, list of `dict`s, another DataFrame, or an external file (`csv`, `excel`, `json`, `sql`).
2. **Index alignment:** pandas enforces **label-based alignment** — when two DataFrames are combined, the index and columns are *automatically* matched, producing `NaN` where alignment fails.
3. **Type inference:** Each column's `dtype` is inferred (or explicitly cast with `astype()`).
4. **Memory model:** Stored in the BlockManager; columnar storage means **column-wise operations are O(n)** whereas **row-wise operations** often fall back to a Python loop and are *O(n) per row*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?** Because the *vectorised vs. loop* distinction is a **favourite 3-mark sub-question**. Always prefer `df['x'] * 2` (vectorised) over `df['x'].apply(lambda v: v*2)` when speed matters.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat-Sheet Table

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important revision artefact** for Module Experiment 2. Memorise the syntax, the axis convention, and the return type.

| # | Operation | Syntax (canonical) | Axis / Param | Return Type | KTU 2024 Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Create from dict | `pd.DataFrame(d)` | — | DataFrame | `d` keys become columns |
| 2 | Read CSV | `pd.read_csv('f.csv')` | `sep, header, index_col` | DataFrame | Most common exam input |
| 3 | First n rows | `df.head(n)` | `n=5` default | DataFrame | Always show in answers |
| 4 | Shape | `df.shape` | — | tuple `(rows, cols)` | `(R, C)` not `(C, R)` |
| 5 | Dtypes | `df.dtypes` | — | Series of dtypes | Useful before stats |
| 6 | Summary stats | `df.describe()` | `include='all'` | DataFrame | Mean, std, quartiles |
| 7 | Select column | `df['col']` or `df.col` | — | Series | `df.col` fails if name has space |
| 8 | Select multiple cols | `df[['c1','c2']]` | — | DataFrame | Double brackets! |
| 9 | Label-based row | `df.loc[label]` | row label | Series / DataFrame | Inclusive of endpoints |
| 10 | Position-based row | `df.iloc[pos]` | integer pos | Series / DataFrame | Like Python slicing |
| 11 | Boolean filter | `df[df['x']>5]` | mask | DataFrame | Combine with `&` $\vert$ $\vert$ |
| 12 | Add column | `df['new'] = expr` | axis=1 | DataFrame (in-place) | Vectorised assign |
| 13 | Drop rows/cols | `df.drop('c', axis=1)` | axis 0/1 | DataFrame | `inplace=True` mutates |
| 14 | Sort by values | `df.sort_values('c')` | `ascending, na_position` | DataFrame | Default ascending |
| 15 | Sort by index | `df.sort_index()` | `axis` | DataFrame | Useful post-merge |
| 16 | Group + aggregate | `df.groupby('k').agg({'v':'mean'})` | `as_index` | DataFrame | The "split-apply-combine" core |
| 17 | Merge (SQL-join) | `pd.merge(L, R, on='k')` | `how: inner,left,right,outer` | DataFrame | `how` is critical |
| 18 | Concatenate | `pd.concat([a,b], axis=0)` | axis 0=vertical, 1=horizontal | DataFrame | Stacking |
| 19 | Pivot table | `df.pivot_table(values, index, columns, aggfunc)` | `aggfunc` default `mean` | DataFrame | Cross-tabulation |
| 20 | Missing count | `df.isna().sum()` | — | Series | First step in cleaning |
| 21 | Fill missing | `df.fillna(v)` or `df.fillna(method='ffill')` | `method, value` | DataFrame | `method` deprecated → use `ffill()` |
| 22 | Drop missing | `df.dropna()` | `axis, how, thresh` | DataFrame | `how='any'` $\vert$ `'all'` |
| 23 | Apply function | `df['c'].apply(fn)` | element-wise | Series / DataFrame | Slower than vectorisation |
| 24 | Value counts | `df['c'].value_counts()` | `normalize, ascending` | Series | Great for categorical EDA |
| 25 | Unique values | `df['c'].unique()` | — | ndarray | Returns in order of appearance |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse `df.sum()` and `df.sum(axis=1)`.
> - `axis=0` (default) → **collapse rows**, i.e., *one value per column* (column-wise sum).
> - `axis=1` → **collapse columns**, i.e., *one value per row* (row-wise sum).
> The rule is: **`axis` is the axis that gets collapsed (the one that *disappears*)**.

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Operation — Engineering Utility

- **Boolean filtering** $df[df['SGPA'] \ge 8.0]$ is the bedrock of *hypothesis-driven* data slicing — equivalent to a `WHERE` clause in SQL.
- **`groupby().agg()`** implements the **split–apply–combine** paradigm of Hadley Wickham, allowing branch-wise, gender-wise, or year-wise descriptive statistics — vital for the KTU experiment on *"Measures of Central Tendency & Dispersion grouped by category"*.
- **`merge()`** allows joining two datasets on a key (e.g., joining a *students* table with a *marks* table on `Roll_No`) — the statistical equivalent of a relational join.
- **`pivot_table()`** produces *cross-tabulations*, the precursor to a $\chi^2$ test of independence in inferential statistics.
- **Missing-value handling** (`isna`, `fillna`, `dropna`) is **non-negotiable** in real engineering datasets (sensor failures, dropout students, etc.) and is a **guaranteed 3-mark sub-question** in the KTU lab record.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through **six canonical DataFrame experiments** that mirror the KTU 2024 lab manual for Module Experiment 2. Every output is computed explicitly so the answer-script can be reproduced verbatim.

---

## 3.1 Experiment A — Creation, Inspection & Indexing

### Step 1: Construct a DataFrame from a Python dictionary

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

data = {
    'Name'   : ['Asha', 'Balu', 'Chitra', 'Deepak', 'Esha'],
    'Branch' : ['CSE', 'ECE', 'CSE', 'ME', 'ECE'],
    'SGPA'   : [9.2, 8.4, 7.8, 8.9, 9.5],
    'CGPA'   : [8.9, 8.1, 7.5, 8.7, 9.3]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data, index=['R001','R002','R003','R004','R005'])
print(df)
```

**Output**

```
       Name Branch  SGPA  CGPA
R001   Asha    CSE   9.2   8.9
R002   Balu    ECE   8.4   8.1
R003 Chitra    CSE   7.8   7.5
R004 Deepak     ME   8.9   8.7
R005   Esha    ECE   9.5   9.3
```

### Step 2: Retrieve the three structural attributes

```python
print(df.shape)     # (5, 4)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.index)     # Index(['R001','R002','R003','R004','R005'], dtype='object')
print(df.columns)   # Index(['Name','Branch','SGPA','CGPA'], dtype='object')
print(df.values)    # 2-D ndarray
```

**Logic row-by-row**

- `df.shape` returns the **tuple `(number_of_rows, number_of_columns)`** in that order. Here: 5 students $\times$ 4 attributes.
- `df.dtypes` reports the inferred type of *each column* (`Name`, `Branch` $\to$ `object`; `SGPA`, `CGPA` $\to$ `float64`).
- `df.index` exposes the **row labels**; `df.columns` exposes the **column labels**; `df.values` exposes the raw NumPy block.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Pattern:** Writing the three outputs in a single labelled answer fetches the **full 3 marks** for the "inspection" sub-question.

---

## 3.2 Experiment B — Label vs. Position Based Selection (`.loc` vs. `.iloc`)

The two accessors are **the most-asked differentiator** in KTU lab viva.

| Accessor | Selector type | Inclusive of endpoint? | Use case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `.loc[label]` | **Label** (the *value* of the index) | **Yes** | "Give me R002 to R004" |
| `.iloc[pos]`  | **Integer position** (0-based)  | **No** (Python rule) | "Give me the 2nd to 4th row" |

### Worked Example

```python
print(df.loc['R002'])           # one row → Series
print(df.loc['R002':'R004', ['Name','SGPA']])   # slice both axes
print(df.iloc[1:4, [0,2]])      # positions 1,2,3 × cols 0,2
```

**Mathematical / logical interpretation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{df.loc['R002':'R004']} &\equiv \text{all rows whose label } \in \mathcal{L}=\{\text{R002,R003,R004}\} \\
&= \{x \in \mathcal{D} \mid \text{index}(x) \in \mathcal{L}\} \\
\texttt{df.iloc[1:4]} &\equiv \text{rows at positions } 1,2,3 \text{ (NOT position 4)} \\
&= \{x_i \mid i \in \{1,2,3\}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Viva Trap:** `.loc['R002':'R004']` includes **R004** (label-endpoint inclusive), but `.iloc[1:4]` includes only positions 1, 2, 3 — **not 4**. This asymmetry is the #1 cause of off-by-one bugs.

---

## 3.3 Experiment C — Boolean (Conditional) Filtering

Suppose we want every CSE student with `SGPA ≥ 8.0`. We translate the question to a *boolean mask*:

$$
M_i \;=\; \big(\text{Branch}_i = \text{'CSE'}\big)\;\wedge\;\big(\text{SGPA}_i \ge 8.0\big)
$$

```python
mask = (df['Branch'] == 'CSE') & (df['SGPA'] >= 8.0)
result = df[mask]
print(result)
```

**Output**

```
      Name Branch  SGPA  CGPA
R001  Asha    CSE   9.2   8.9
```

**Bit-by-bit mask evaluation for the original table**

| Index | Branch | `Branch=='CSE'` | SGPA | `SGPA>=8.0` | AND |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R001 | CSE | True  | 9.2 | True  | **True**  |
| R002 | ECE | False | 8.4 | True  | False |
| R003 | CSE | True  | 7.8 | False | False |
| R004 | ME  | False | 8.9 | True  | False |
| R005 | ECE | False | 9.5 | True  | False |

Only R001 survives. This **vectorised boolean indexing** runs at C-speed (NumPy under the hood) and is the canonical way to slice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Element-wise operators inside pandas:** `&` (AND), `|` (OR), `~` (NOT). Do **NOT** use Python's `and`, `or`, `not` — they raise a `ValueError` due to *truthiness ambiguity*.

---

## 3.4 Experiment D — Split–Apply–Combine with `groupby()`

This is the **statistical heart** of pandas. The question: *"What is the average SGPA per branch?"*

**Step 1 — Split:** Partition rows by the unique values of `Branch`.

$$
\mathcal{D} \;=\; \bigsqcup_{b \,\in\, \text{Branches}} \mathcal{D}_b, \quad \text{where } \mathcal{D}_b = \{i : \text{Branch}_i = b\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply:** Compute the arithmetic mean of `SGPA` within each partition:

$$
\bar{x}_b \;=\; \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{D}_b \vert}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{D}_b}\text{SGPA}_i
$$

**Step 3 — Combine:** Concatenate the per-group scalars into a result Series.

```python
print(df.groupby('Branch')['SGPA'].mean())
```

**Output**

```
Branch
CSE    8.50
ECE    8.95
ME     8.90
Name: SGPA, dtype: float64
```

**Manual verification for the CSE group** (R001 = 9.2, R003 = 7.8):

$$
\bar{x}_{\text{CSE}} \;=\; \frac{9.2 + 7.8}{2} \;=\; \frac{17.0}{2} \;=\; 8.50 \;\checkmark
$$

> [!TIP]
> **`.agg()` extension:** You can pass a *dict* to compute multiple aggregates simultaneously:
> ```python
> df.groupby('Branch').agg(
>     mean_sgpa = ('SGPA','mean'),
>     max_sgpa  = ('SGPA','max'),
>     count     = ('Name','count')
> )
> ```
> This produces a wide summary table in one expression — extremely high-yield for the KTU 14-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Experiment E — Concatenation vs. Merge (Joining Two Tables)

**Scenario:** Two DataFrames — `df_students` (the master roll) and `df_marks` (semester marks) — share a common key `Roll_No`. We must join them.

```python
df_students = pd.DataFrame({
    'Roll_No':['R001','R002','R003'],
    'Name'    :['Asha','Balu','Chitra']
})
df_marks = pd.DataFrame({
    'Roll_No':['R001','R002','R004'],
    'SGPA'   :[9.2, 8.4, 7.5]
})
```

**Inner join (default)** — keeps only matching keys:

```python
pd.merge(df_students, df_marks, on='Roll_No', how='inner')
```

**Output**

```
  Roll_No   Name  SGPA
0    R001   Asha   9.2
1    R002   Balu   8.4
```

**Left join** — keeps *all* rows of the *left* table, filling `NaN` where no match:

```python
pd.merge(df_students, df_marks, on='Roll_No', how='left')
```

**Output**

```
  Roll_No   Name  SGPA
0    R001   Asha   9.2
1    R002   Balu   8.4
2    R003 Chitra   NaN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`how` parameter values to memorise for KTU 2024:**
> - `inner` $\to$ intersection of keys (default).
> - `left` $\to$ all left rows + matched right rows.
> - `right` $\to$ all right rows + matched left rows.
> - `outer` $\to$ union of keys (full outer join); non-matches become `NaN`.

For **vertical stacking** (adding more rows of the *same schema*), use `pd.concat([a, b], axis=0)`. For **horizontal stacking** (adding more columns to the *same rows*), use `axis=1`.

---

## 3.6 Experiment F — Handling Missing Data

Real datasets almost always contain `NaN`. The first hygiene step is *diagnosis*:

```python
df2 = pd.DataFrame({
    'A':[1, 2, np.nan, 4],
    'B':[5, np.nan, np.nan, 8],
    'C':[9, 10, 11, 12]
})
print(df2.isna().sum())     # column-wise count of NaN
```

**Output**

```
A    1
B    2
C    0
dtype: int64
```

**Remediation strategies**

| Strategy | Code | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Drop rows with any NaN | `df2.dropna()` | Loses data |
| Drop rows where *all* are NaN | `df2.dropna(how='all')` | Conservative |
| Fill with constant | `df2.fillna(0)` | Domain-dependent |
| Fill with column mean | `df2.fillna(df2.mean(numeric_only=True))` | Unbiased estimator |
| Forward fill (time series) | `df2.ffill()` | Carries last observation |

> [!WARNING]
> **`method='ffill'` is deprecated** as of pandas 2.x. Use the **method object** `df.ffill()` or `df.fillna(method='ffill')` only on legacy versions. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the modern API.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Operation

| Statistical Question | Best pandas Construct | One-liner |
|---|---|---|
| Average of a numeric column | `mean()` | `df['x'].mean()` |
| Median by group | `groupby().median()` | `df.groupby('g')['x'].median()` |
| Cross-tabulation | `crosstab()` | `pd.crosstab(df.g1, df.g2)` |
| Correlation matrix | `corr()` | `df.corr(numeric_only=True)` |
| Covariance matrix | `cov()` | `df.cov()` |
| Frequency table | `value_counts()` | `df['c'].value_counts()` |
| Rank | `rank()` | `df['x'].rank(ascending=False)` |
| Cumulative sum | `cumsum()` | `df['x'].cumsum()` |
| Z-score normalisation | vectorised arithmetic | `(df['x']-df['x'].mean())/df['x'].std()` |

> [!TIP]
> The last row is the **standard-score formula**, written in pure pandas:
> ```python
> df['x_z'] = (df['x'] - df['x'].mean()) / df['x'].std(ddof=0)
> ```
> where `ddof=0` gives the *population* standard deviation (divide by $n$), and `ddof=1` (default) gives the *sample* standard deviation (divide by $n-1$). This is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in the lab exam.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are written in **Mermaid** syntax. They render natively in GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, and most modern Markdown viewers. No external image assets are required.

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of a pandas DataFrame

```mermaid
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUT / STATISTICAL ANALYSIS"]

    A1 --> CORE
```
