
---

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Which Selection Accessor Do I Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Do you know the row LABEL?}
    Q1 -- Yes, by name --> LOC[Use df.loc label]
    Q1 -- No, only position --> Q2{Do you need column subset by NAME?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> HYB[Use df.iloc rows, df.columns names]
    Q2 -- No --> ILOC[Use df.iloc position]

    Q3{Need CONDITIONAL selection?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> MASK[Build boolean mask df condition]
    Q3 -- No --> Q1

    style LOC fill:#dff0d8
    style ILOC fill:#dff0d8
    style MASK fill:#fcf8e3
    style HYB fill:#fcf8e3
```

---

## 4.3 Split–Apply–Combine Schematic for `groupby`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original DataFrame] --> B[Split by key Branch]
    B --> B1[Group CSE]
    B --> B2[Group ECE]
    B --> B3[Group ME]
    B1 --> C1[Apply mean on SGPA]
    B2 --> C2[Apply mean on SGPA]
    B3 --> C3[Apply mean on SGPA]
    C1 --> D[Combine into result Series]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Aggregated Output DataFrame]
```

---

## 4.4 Merge-Join Venn Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L["Left Table dfL Keys: A B C"]
        L1
    end
    subgraph R["Right Table dfR Keys: B C D"]
        R1
    end
    INNER["Inner Join B C"]
    LEFTO["Left Join A B C with NaN for A"]
    RIGHTO["Right Join B C D with NaN for D"]
    OUTERO["Outer Join A B C D with NaN where unmatched"]
    L --> INNER
    R --> INNER
    L --> LEFTO
    R --> RIGHTO
    L --> OUTERO
    R --> OUTERO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Continuous + ESE Pattern for Lab Courses:**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Direct, recall-based conceptual question.
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Full ESE-style long answer with internal choice. Typically split (a) = 7 marks (code + output), (b) = 7 marks (interpretation / extension).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`
**Define a pandas DataFrame. List any four attributes used to inspect its structure.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *pandas DataFrame* is a **two-dimensional, labelled, size-mutable tabular data structure** with axes labelled (rows and columns), capable of holding heterogeneous data types. It is the primary data object of the pandas library used in statistical computing.

Four inspection attributes:
1. `df.shape` $\to$ returns a tuple `(n_rows, n_columns)`.
2. `df.dtypes` $\to$ returns the data type of each column.
3. `df.index` $\to$ returns the row labels.
4. `df.columns` $\to$ returns the column labels.
*(Acceptable alternatives: `df.values`, `df.size`, `df.ndim`, `df.info()`.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between `df.loc[]` and `df.iloc[]` with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `df.loc[]` | `df.iloc[]` |
|---|---|---|
| Selector type | **Label-based** (row/column *names*) | **Integer-position-based** (0-indexed) |
| Slice endpoint | **Inclusive** of the end label | **Exclusive** of the end position (Python rule) |
| Example | `df.loc['R002']` returns the row whose index label is `'R002'` | `df.iloc[1]` returns the **second** row |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> Given the following dataset of B.Tech students:

| Roll_No | Name   | Branch | SGPA | CGPA | Backlogs |
|---------|--------|--------|------|------|----------|
| R001    | Asha   | CSE    | 9.2  | 8.9  | 0        |
| R002    | Balu   | ECE    | 8.4  | 8.1  | 1        |
| R003    | Chitra | CSE    | 7.8  | 7.5  | 2        |
| R004    | Deepak | ME     | 8.9  | 8.7  | 0        |
| R005    | Esha   | ECE    | 9.5  | 9.3  | 0        |
| R006    | Faris  | CSE    | 6.5  | 6.8  | 3        |

#### (a) Write a pandas program to:
- Create the DataFrame from the above data with `Roll_No` as the index.
- Display its shape, dtypes, and the first 3 rows. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd

data = {
    'Name'    : ['Asha','Balu','Chitra','Deepak','Esha','Faris'],
    'Branch'  : ['CSE','ECE','CSE','ME','ECE','CSE'],
    'SGPA'    : [9.2, 8.4, 7.8, 8.9, 9.5, 6.5],
    'CGPA'    : [8.9, 8.1, 7.5, 8.7, 9.3, 6.8],
    'Backlogs': [0, 1, 2, 0, 0, 3]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data, index=['R001','R002','R003','R004','R005','R006'])
df.index.name = 'Roll_No'

print("Shape :", df.shape)         # (6, 5)
print("\nDtypes:\n", df.dtypes)
print("\nFirst 3 rows:\n", df.head(3))
```

**Output:**

```
Shape : (6, 5)

Dtypes:
 Name         object
Branch       object
SGPA        float64
CGPA        float64
Backlogs      int64
dtype: object

First 3 rows:
          Name Branch  SGPA  CGPA  Backlogs
Roll_No
R001      Asha    CSE   9.2   8.9         0
R002      Balu    ECE   8.4   8.1         1
R003    Chitra    CSE   7.8   7.5         2
```

**Mark Split:** DataFrame creation (3), `shape` + `dtypes` (2), `head(3)` (2). **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Using the same DataFrame, write code to:
- Find the **average CGPA per branch**.
- Find all students with **SGPA ≥ 8.5 and zero backlogs**.
- Add a new column `Status` that is `"Distinction"` if CGPA ≥ 8.5, else `"Pass"`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
# (i) Average CGPA per branch
avg_cgpa = df.groupby('Branch')['CGPA'].mean()
print("Average CGPA per branch:\n", avg_cgpa)

# (ii) Filter: high performers with no backlogs
high = df[(df['SGPA'] >= 8.5) & (df['Backlogs'] == 0)]
print("\nHigh performers:\n", high[['Name','Branch','SGPA']])

# (iii) Vectorised conditional column using numpy.where
import numpy as np
df['Status'] = np.where(df['CGPA'] >= 8.5, 'Distinction', 'Pass')
print("\nDataFrame with Status:\n", df)
```

**Output (truncated):**

```
Average CGPA per branch:
 Branch
CSE    7.733333
ECE    8.700000
ME     8.700000
Name: CGPA, dtype: float64

High performers:
          Name Branch  SGPA
Roll_No
R001      Asha    CSE   9.2
R004    Deepak     ME   8.9
R005      Esha    ECE   9.5
```

**Mark Split:** groupby (3), boolean filter (2), conditional column (2). **(7 marks)**

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> A weather station records hourly readings in a CSV file `weather.csv` with columns: `Date, Hour, Temperature, Humidity, WindSpeed, Rainfall`. Some entries are missing (`NaN`).

#### (a) Write a complete pandas program to:
- Read the CSV into a DataFrame with `Date` parsed as datetime and used as the index.
- Report the number of missing values per column.
- Fill missing `Temperature` with the **column mean** and missing `Rainfall` with **0**.
- Drop any remaining rows that still contain `NaN`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
import pandas as pd

# (i) Read with parsed Date index
df = pd.read_csv('weather.csv',
                 parse_dates=['Date'],
                 index_col='Date')
print("First 3 rows:\n", df.head(3))

# (ii) Missing-value audit
print("\nMissing values per column:\n", df.isna().sum())

# (iii) Imputation
df['Temperature'] = df['Temperature'].fillna(df['Temperature'].mean())
df['Rainfall']    = df['Rainfall'].fillna(0)

# (iv) Final cleanup
df = df.dropna()
print("\nShape after cleaning:", df.shape)
print("Any NaN left?", df.isna().any().any())
```

**Mark Split:** read_csv with parse_dates (2), isna().sum() (1), two fillna strategies (2), dropna (2). **(7 marks)**

#### (b) On the cleaned DataFrame, perform:
- A **descriptive summary** using `describe()`.
- A **correlation matrix** between the numeric columns.
- A **pivot table** showing the *average Temperature* by `Hour` (rows) and by date (columns).
- A **merge** with a second DataFrame `df_stations = pd.DataFrame({'Station':['A','B','C'], 'City':['Kochi','Chennai','Bengaluru']})` after adding a `Station` column to the weather data (assume the first 30 rows belong to 'A', next 30 to 'B', rest to 'C'). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```python
# (i) Descriptive statistics
print(df.describe())

# (ii) Correlation matrix
print("\nCorrelation matrix:\n", df.corr(numeric_only=True))

# (iii) Pivot table
pivot = df.pivot_table(values='Temperature',
                       index=df.index.date,
                       columns='Hour',
                       aggfunc='mean')
print("\nPivot (first 3 days):\n", pivot.head(3))

# (iv) Build Station label and merge
station_map = (['A']*30) + (['B']*30) + (['C']*(len(df)-60))
df['Station'] = station_map[:len(df)]

df_stations = pd.DataFrame({
    'Station':['A','B','C'],
    'City'   :['Kochi','Chennai','Bengaluru']
})
merged = pd.merge(df, df_stations, on='Station', how='left')
print("\nMerged tail:\n", merged[['Station','City','Temperature']].tail(3))
```

**Mark Split:** describe (1), corr (2), pivot_table (2), merge with on= and how= (2). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting parentheses:** `df['Branch'] == 'CSE'` must be wrapped in `( … )` before joining with `&` or `|`. Bare expressions raise `ValueError: The truth value of a Series is ambiguous`.
> 2. **Double vs. single brackets:** `df['col']` returns a *Series*; `df[['col']]` returns a *DataFrame*. Examiners deduct 1 mark if a question demands a DataFrame and the student returns a Series (or vice-versa).
> 3. **`inplace=True` confusion:** `df.drop('col', axis=1, inplace=True)` mutates `df`; without `inplace=True`, the result must be reassigned (`df = df.drop(...)`). Many students silently mutate and then refer to the *original* DataFrame.
> 4. **Axis confusion:** Remember `axis=0` collapses *across rows* (column-wise result); `axis=1` collapses *across columns* (row-wise result).
> 5. **String method accessors:** `df['col'].str.lower()` is correct; `df['col'].lower()` will throw `AttributeError`.
> 6. **Index alignment surprises:** `pd.concat([a, b])` on differently-indexed DataFrames produces `NaN` everywhere alignment fails. Always check `ignore_index=True` if you want a fresh 0..n-1 index.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **DataFrame** is a 2-D, labelled, *size-mutable* table built on top of NumPy.
- [ ] Always remember the **three structural attributes**: `df.shape`, `df.dtypes`, `df.index`/`df.columns`.
- [ ] **`.loc[]` is label-based and inclusive**; **`.iloc[]` is position-based and exclusive** at the end.
- [ ] **Boolean filtering** uses `&` (AND), `|` (OR), `~` (NOT) on *parenthesised* conditions.
- [ ] **`groupby()` follows the Split–Apply–Combine paradigm**; chain with `.agg(dict)` for multiple statistics.
- [ ] **`merge()` is for SQL-style joins** (use `on=` and `how=`); **`concat()` is for stacking** (use `axis=0` or `axis=1`).
- [ ] **`pivot_table()` is the cross-tabulation tool**; default `aggfunc='mean'`.
- [ ] **Missing values**: diagnose with `isna().sum()`, treat with `fillna()` (constant, mean, ffill) or `dropna()`.
- [ ] **Z-score normalisation** is one line: `(x - x.mean()) / x.std(ddof=0)`.
- [ ] **Vectorised operations beat `apply()`** for speed — always prefer the former unless logic is irreducibly scalar.
- [ ] **Index alignment** is automatic: combining mismatched indices produces `NaN` in non-overlapping positions.
- [ ] **`pd.read_csv`** is the most common exam entry point; remember `parse_dates=`, `index_col=`, `na_values=`.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 expects modern pandas API** — prefer `df.ffill()` over `df.fillna(method='ffill')`.
- [ ] In answer scripts, **always print `df.head(3)`** to make outputs self-documenting; this is a free *presentation mark* signal.

> ✅ **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Experiments on pandas DataFrame.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling missing values
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Handling missing values

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **PCCBL508 – Computational Statistics Lab**, **Handling Missing Values** is defined as the systematic computational procedure used to identify, characterize, and resolve the absence of data entries (denoted typically as `NaN`, `NA`, `None`, or `null`) within a structured dataset. According to KTU 2024 Scheme outcome-based standards, this experiment requires the student to demonstrate proficiency in detecting missingness, classifying its mechanism, and applying statistically valid treatment strategies using a computational tool (Python – Pandas / NumPy / Scikit-learn, or R).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCBL508 – Module 2):**
> *"Identification and treatment of missing data – mean/median/mode imputation, forward and backward fill, interpolation, KNN imputation, and deletion strategies – implemented using Pandas."*

A **missing value** is formally any recorded observation $x_{ij}$ in the data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p}$ for which the true value is unknown, unrecorded, or corrupted, and is represented as:

$$x_{ij} = \text{NaN} \quad \text{where} \quad i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}, \; j \in \{1, 2, \dots, p\}$$

## 1.2 The Three Statistical Mechanisms of Missingness (Rubin's Taxonomy)

Donald B. Rubin (1976) classified missingness into three distinct categories. Understanding these is **exam-critical** for KTU:

| Mechanism | Symbol | Formal Definition | Real-World Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Missing Completely At Random** | **MCAR** | $P(M \mid X_{\text{obs}}, X_{\text{mis}}) = P(M)$ | Sensor randomly fails |
| **Missing At Random** | **MAR** | $P(M \mid X_{\text{obs}}, X_{\text{mis}}) = P(M \mid X_{\text{obs}})$ | Older patients skip survey sections |
| **Missing Not At Random** | **MNAR** | $P(M \mid X_{\text{obs}}, X_{\text{mis}}) \neq P(M \mid X_{\text{obs}})$ | High earners hide income |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaking Bucket" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are filling a **leaking bucket** with water. The bucket is your dataset. The water represents your data points. The leaks represent missing values. Now, there are three scenarios:
>
> 1. **MCAR (Random Holes):** The bucket has holes in random places. Water leaks out randomly. The remaining water still represents the original full bucket fairly.
> 2. **MAR (Pattern Holes):** The bucket has holes only near the *top*. Water near the top always leaks, but the leak pattern depends on the *height* (an observed variable), not on the water itself. Once you know the height, the leak is explainable.
> 3. **MNAR (Self-Selective Holes):** The bucket has holes only where the water is *hot*. The leak pattern depends on the very thing being lost. You cannot fix this just by looking at what remains.
>
> **Why does this matter?** Because the choice of imputation method depends on which "leak type" your data has. Wrong choice = biased analysis = lost marks in KTU lab records and viva.

## 1.4 The Two Strategic Approaches

**Approach A — Deletion (Removal):**
Throw away incomplete rows/columns. Simple, but **reduces sample size** and introduces **bias** if data is not MCAR.

**Approach B — Imputation (Filling):**
Estimate and fill the missing entries with plausible values. Preserves sample size but **introduces uncertainty** if done poorly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** A common student mistake is treating *deleting all missing rows* as the "default solution." This is **only valid under MCAR**. For MAR or MNAR, imputation is mandatory. Examiners frequently test this distinction in 14-mark questions.

## 1.5 Bold-Formatted Key Metrics

The following **standard metrics** must be memorized:
- **Missing Ratio** per column: $r_j = \dfrac{\text{count\_missing}_j}{n}$
- **Total missing cells** in dataset $\mathbf{X}$: $M_{total} = \sum_{j=1}^{p} \text{count\_missing}_j$
- **Completeness** of dataset: $C = 1 - \dfrac{M_{total}}{n \cdot p}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline (The "Missing-Value Workflow")

The complete, KTU-aligned workflow consists of **six sequential stages**. Each stage must be demonstrated in the lab record:

**Step 1 — Data Ingestion**
Load the dataset (CSV, Excel) into a DataFrame `df` using `pandas.read_csv()` or similar.

**Step 2 — Detection**
Quantify missingness using `df.isnull().sum()` and `df.isnull().mean()`. Visualize with a heatmap or `missingno` matrix.

**Step 3 — Diagnosis**
Classify the mechanism (MCAR / MAR / MNAR) using Little's MCAR test or domain knowledge.

**Step 4 — Strategy Selection**
Decide between **deletion** or **imputation** based on:
- Missing ratio $r_j$ (threshold typically **5%** or **20%**)
- Mechanism of missingness
- Downstream task (regression, classification, EDA)

**Step 5 — Execution**
Apply the chosen technique using a Pandas/Scikit-learn function.

**Step 6 — Validation**
Compare descriptive statistics, distributions (KDE plots), and correlation matrices *before* and *after* imputation to confirm no distortion.

## 2.2 Detailed Treatment Methods (The 'Why' and 'How')

### 2.2.1 Deletion Methods
- **Listwise Deletion (Complete Case Analysis):** Drop any row with ≥1 missing value. Formula for usable rows: $n_{\text{final}} = n - n_{\text{dropped}}$
- **Pairwise Deletion (Available Case Analysis):** Use all available data per correlation/calculation, ignoring missing case-by-case.
- **Column Dropping:** Drop entire feature if $r_j > 0.40$ (a common engineering rule-of-thumb).

### 2.2.2 Simple Statistical Imputation
Replace $x_{ij}^{\text{mis}}$ with a central-tendency measure computed from observed values of feature $j$:
- **Mean Imputation:** $\hat{x}_{ij} = \bar{x}_j = \frac{1}{n_j^{obs}} \sum_{i: x_{ij} \text{ obs}} x_{ij}$
- **Median Imputation:** $\hat{x}_{ij} = \text{median}(X_j^{obs})$ — preferred for **skewed** distributions.
- **Mode Imputation:** $\hat{x}_{ij} = \text{mode}(X_j^{obs})$ — used for **categorical** features.

### 2.2.3 Time-Series Aware Imputation
- **Forward Fill (`ffill`):** $\hat{x}_{ij} = x_{i-1, j}^{obs}$
- **Backward Fill (`bfill`):** $\hat{x}_{ij} = x_{i+1, j}^{obs}$
- **Linear Interpolation:** $\hat{x}_{ij} = x_{i-1} + \frac{(x_{i+1} - x_{i-1})}{(t_{i+1} - t_{i-1})} (t_i - t_{i-1})$

### 2.2.4 Advanced Model-Based Imputation
- **KNN Imputation:** $\hat{x}_{ij} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k \in N_K(i)} x_{kj}$ where $N_K(i)$ is the set of $K$ nearest neighbors of row $i$ in observed feature space.
- **Iterative Imputer (MICE):** Models each feature with missing values as a function of all other features iteratively.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-grade reference material** — every cell is testable.

| # | Method | Formula | Best Use Case | Key Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Listwise Deletion | $n_{\text{final}} = n - n_{\text{drop}}$ | MCAR, low $r_j$ (<5%) | MCAR holds |
| 2 | Mean Imputation | $\bar{x}_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_{ij}^{obs}$ | Symmetric, low skew | MCAR |
| 3 | Median Imputation | $\tilde{x}_j = \text{median}(X_j^{obs})$ | Skewed numeric | MCAR |
| 4 | Mode Imputation | $\hat{x}_j = \arg\max_c f(c)$ | Categorical / nominal | MCAR |
| 5 | Forward Fill | $x_{ij} \leftarrow x_{i-1, j}$ | Time-series (trend) | Adjacent obs related |
| 6 | Backward Fill | $x_{ij} \leftarrow x_{i+1, j}$ | Time-series (reversed) | Adjacent obs related |
| 7 | Linear Interpolation | $\hat{x}_i = x_{i-1} + \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t}\Delta t_i$ | Continuous time series | Linear evolution |
| 8 | KNN Imputation | $\hat{x}_{ij} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k \in N_K} x_{kj}$ | Tabular, MAR | Feature similarity |
| 9 | MICE (Iterative) | $X_j^{(t+1)} = f(X_{-j}^{(t)})$ | MAR, multivariate | Joint distribution exists |
| 10 | Missing Ratio | $r_j = \frac{\text{NA}_j}{n}$ | Pre-treatment check | — |
| 11 | Completeness | $C = 1 - \frac{M_{total}}{n \cdot p}$ | Dataset health metric | — |
| 12 | Distance Metric (KNN) | $d(i, k) = \sqrt{\sum_{j}(x_{ij} - x_{kj})^2}$ | KNN imputation core | Numeric, scaled features |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Missing Value Handling |
|---|---|
| **IoT / Sensor Networks** | Filling gaps in temperature/pressure sensor logs |
| **Medical Diagnostics** | Imputing lab results for incomplete patient records |
| **Financial Analytics** | Stock price interpolation during market closures |
| **Civil Engineering** | Handling missing concrete strength test records |
| **Machine Learning Pipelines** | Preprocessing step before training Scikit-learn models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this an entire lab experiment?** Because almost **every real-world dataset** has missing values. A data scientist who cannot handle them cannot perform valid inference. KTU tests this skill in the **lab continuous evaluation (CE)** and the **end-semester practical exam (ESE)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Mean Imputation (Complete Derivation)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
Given the following dataset of student scores in a class of 5 students, the value of `Score` for student $S_3$ is missing. Compute the mean-imputed value and verify that the standard deviation of the column decreases.

| Student | Score $x_i$ |
|---|---|
| $S_1$ | 78 |
| $S_2$ | 85 |
| $S_3$ | **NaN** |
| $S_4$ | 92 |
| $S_5$ | 88 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the observed values of `Score` (excluding NaN).

$$X_{\text{obs}} = \{78, 85, 92, 88\}, \quad n_{\text{obs}} = 4$$

**Step 2:** Apply the arithmetic mean formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}_{\text{obs}} &= \frac{1}{n_{\text{obs}}} \sum_{i=1}^{n_{\text{obs}}} x_i \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left( 78 + 85 + 92 + 88 \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{4} \left( 343 \right) \\
&= 85.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Impute the missing entry.

$$\hat{x}_3 = 85.75$$

**Step 4:** Verify the impact on variance (proof of distortion).

The **original variance** (had $S_3$ truly been 90, say) would have been:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2_{\text{true}} &= \frac{1}{5}\left[ (78-84.6)^2 + (85-84.6)^2 + (90-84.6)^2 + (92-84.6)^2 + (88-84.6)^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{5}\left[ 43.56 + 0.16 + 29.16 + 54.76 + 11.56 \right] \\
&= \frac{139.20}{5} = 27.84
\end{aligned}
$$

The **mean-imputed variance** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2_{\text{imp}} &= \frac{1}{5}\left[ (78-85.75)^2 + (85-85.75)^2 + (85.75-85.75)^2 + (92-85.75)^2 + (88-85.75)^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{5}\left[ 60.0625 + 0.5625 + 0 + 39.0625 + 5.0625 \right] \\
&= \frac{104.75}{5} = 20.95
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Observation:** $\sigma^2_{\text{imp}} = 20.95 < \sigma^2_{\text{true}} = 27.84$. This is the **famous variance-underestimation problem** of mean imputation. KTU examiners love testing this insight.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Linear Interpolation Derivation

**Problem:** Given a time-series $t = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]$ with values $y = [10, \text{NaN}, 30, \text{NaN}, 50]$, find the interpolated values.

**General Linear Interpolation Formula:**

$$\hat{y}_i = y_{i-1} + \frac{y_{i+1} - y_{i-1}}{t_{i+1} - t_{i-1}} (t_i - t_{i-1})$$

**For $t = 2$** (between $t=1, y=10$ and $t=3, y=30$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_2 &= 10 + \frac{30 - 10}{3 - 1} (2 - 1) \\
&= 10 + \frac{20}{2}(1) \\
&= 10 + 10 = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $t = 4$** (between $t=3, y=30$ and $t=5, y=50$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{y}_4 &= 30 + \frac{50 - 30}{5 - 3} (4 - 3) \\
&= 30 + \frac{20}{2}(1) \\
&= 30 + 10 = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting filled series:** $y = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]$ — a perfect arithmetic progression.

## 3.3 KNN Imputation — Algorithmic Walkthrough

**Setup:** $K = 2$, dataset with 5 observations and 2 features (one missing value):

| Obs | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 1.0 | 2.0 | 5.0 |
| B | 2.0 | 3.0 | 6.0 |
| C | 3.0 | 4.0 | **NaN** |
| D | 8.0 | 9.0 | 10.0 |
| E | 9.0 | 8.0 | 11.0 |

**Step 1:** Euclidean distance from $C$ to each complete observation (using $X_1, X_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C, A) &= \sqrt{(3-1)^2 + (4-2)^2} = \sqrt{4+4} = \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \\
d(C, B) &= \sqrt{(3-2)^2 + (4-3)^2} = \sqrt{1+1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41 \\
d(C, D) &= \sqrt{(3-8)^2 + (4-9)^2} = \sqrt{25+25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07 \\
d(C, E) &= \sqrt{(3-9)^2 + (4-8)^2} = \sqrt{36+16} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Select $K=2$ nearest neighbors: $B$ and $A$.

**Step 3:** Compute imputed value as mean of neighbors' $X_3$:

$$
\hat{x}_{C,3} = \frac{x_{B,3} + x_{A,3}}{2} = \frac{6.0 + 5.0}{2} = 5.5
$$

## 3.4 Complete Python Implementation (Lab-Ready Code Block)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **Computational Statistics Lab** (PCCBL508). Code IS the primary deliverable and is expected in the lab record. The following is a single, end-to-end, exam-ready script.

```python
# =============================================================
#  KTU PCCBL508 - Module 2 Experiment 2
#  Topic : Handling Missing Values
#  Tool  : Python (Pandas, NumPy, Scikit-learn)
# =============================================================

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.impute import KNNImputer
from sklearn.experimental import enable_iterative_imputer
from sklearn.impute import IterativeImputer

# ---------- Step 1: Construct a sample dataset ----------
data = {
    'Age'      : [25, 30, np.nan, 40, 35, np.nan, 50],
    'Salary'   : [50000, np.nan, 60000, 80000, np.nan, 70000, 90000],
    'Department': ['IT', 'HR', 'IT', np.nan, 'Finance', 'HR', 'IT'],
    'Score'    : [88, 92, np.nan, 85, 90, 87, np.nan]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print("Original Dataset:\n", df)

# ---------- Step 2: Detection ----------
print("\nMissing Values per Column:\n", df.isnull().sum())
print("\nMissing Ratio per Column:\n", df.isnull().mean())
print("\nTotal Missing Cells:", df.isnull().sum().sum())
print("Completeness:", 1 - df.isnull().sum().sum() / df.size)

# ---------- Step 3a: Deletion ----------
df_dropped = df.dropna()           # listwise
print("\nAfter Listwise Deletion:\n", df_dropped)

# ---------- Step 3b: Mean Imputation (numeric only) ----------
df_mean = df.copy()
df_mean['Age']    = df_mean['Age'].fillna(df_mean['Age'].mean())
df_mean['Salary'] = df_mean['Salary'].fillna(df_mean['Salary'].mean())
df_mean['Score']  = df_mean['Score'].fillna(df_mean['Score'].mean())
print("\nAfter Mean Imputation:\n", df_mean)

# ---------- Step 3c: Median Imputation ----------
df_median = df.copy()
df_median['Age']    = df_median['Age'].fillna(df_median['Age'].median())
df_median['Salary'] = df_median['Salary'].fillna(df_median['Salary'].median())
print("\nAfter Median Imputation:\n", df_median)

# ---------- Step 3d: Mode Imputation (categorical) ----------
df_mode = df.copy()
df_mode['Department'] = df_mode['Department'].fillna(
                            df_mode['Department'].mode()[0])
print("\nAfter Mode Imputation:\n", df_mode)

# ---------- Step 3e: Forward & Backward Fill ----------
df_ffill = df.fillna(method='ffill')
df_bfill = df.fillna(method='bfill')
print("\nAfter Forward Fill:\n", df_ffill)
print("\nAfter Backward Fill:\n", df_bfill)

# ---------- Step 3f: Linear Interpolation ----------
df_interp = df.copy()
df_interp['Age']    = df_interp['Age'].interpolate(method='linear')
df_interp['Salary'] = df_interp['Salary'].interpolate(method='linear')
df_interp['Score']  = df_interp['Score'].interpolate(method='linear')
print("\nAfter Linear Interpolation:\n", df_interp)

# ---------- Step 3g: KNN Imputation (numeric only) ----------
numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number])
imputer_knn = KNNImputer(n_neighbors=2)
df_knn = pd.DataFrame(imputer_knn.fit_transform(numeric_df),
                     columns=numeric_df.columns)
print("\nAfter KNN Imputation (K=2):\n", df_knn)

# ---------- Step 3h: Iterative Imputer (MICE-style) ----------
imputer_iter = IterativeImputer(random_state=0, max_iter=10)
df_iter = pd.DataFrame(imputer_iter.fit_transform(numeric_df),
                      columns=numeric_df.columns)
print("\nAfter Iterative (MICE) Imputation:\n", df_iter)

# ---------- Step 4: Validation ----------
print("\n--- Statistical Comparison (Before vs After) ---")
print("Original Salary  -> Mean:", df['Salary'].mean(),
      " Std:", df['Salary'].std())
print("Mean-Imputed     -> Mean:", df_mean['Salary'].mean(),
      " Std:", df_mean['Salary'].std())
print("KNN-Imputed      -> Mean:", df_knn['Salary'].mean(),
      " Std:", df_knn['Salary'].std())
```

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing the Right Method

| Scenario Detected | Recommended Method | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| < 5% missing, MCAR | Listwise Deletion | Minimal data loss, unbiased |
| Skewed numeric, MCAR | Median Imputation | Robust to outliers |
| Categorical feature | Mode Imputation | Statistical centrality for nominal |
| Time-series, smooth trend | Linear Interpolation | Preserves temporal continuity |
| Time-series, latest value carried | Forward Fill (`ffill`) | Common in financial tick data |
| Multivariate tabular, MAR | KNN Imputation ($K=5$) | Leverages feature similarity |
| Multivariate tabular, MAR | Iterative Imputer (MICE) | Models conditional distributions |
| > 40% missing in a column | Column Drop | Imputation too unreliable |
| MNAR with self-selection | Domain-specific modeling | Standard methods fail |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Missing Value Handling Pipeline

The following block-diagram maps the **decision flow** a data scientist follows from raw ingestion to validated output. This is the architecture a KTU examiner expects a student to draw in the lab record's "Algorithm / Flowchart" section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Load Dataset X] --> A1[Step 1: Detect Missingness]
    A1 --> A2{Count Missing per Column}
    A2 --> A3[Compute r_j and M_total]
    A3 --> A4{Classify Mechanism}
    A4 -->|Random| A5[MCAR Path]
    A4 -->|Pattern via Observed| A6[MAR Path]
    A4 -->|Self-Dependent| A7[MNAR Path]
    A5 --> B1{Threshold Check r_j less than 0.05}
    B1 -->|Yes| B2[Apply Listwise Deletion]
    B1 -->|No| B3[Apply Simple Statistical Imputation]
    A6 --> C1[Apply Model-Based Imputation]
    C1 --> C2[KNN Imputer with K equal to 5]
    C1 --> C3[Iterative Imputer MICE]
    A7 --> D1[Domain Expert Intervention]
    D1 --> D2[Sensitivity Analysis Required]
    B2 --> E1[Step 4: Validate Output]
    B3 --> E1
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> E2[Compare Statistics Before vs After]
    E2 --> E3{Distribution Distortion Acceptable}
    E3 -->|Yes| F1[End: Cleaned Dataset Ready]
    E3 -->|No| F2[Revert Strategy and Iterate]
    F2 --> A1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of an Imputation Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[Raw Data Matrix X] --> DET[Detector Module]
    DET --> MASK[Boolean Missing Mask M]
    DET --> STATS[Statistics Calculator]
    STATS --> CENT[Central Tendency Engine]
    CENT --> MEAN[Mean Computer]
    CENT --> MED[Median Computer]
    CENT --> MOD[Mode Computer]
    STATS --> DIST[Distance Matrix Builder]
    DIST --> KNN[KNN Engine K Neighbors]
    MASK --> FFILL[Forward Fill Engine]
    MASK --> BFILL[Backward Fill Engine]
    MASK --> INTERP[Linear Interpolation Engine]
    MASK --> MICE[MICE Iterative Engine]
    MEAN --> OUT[Imputed Matrix X hat]
    MED --> OUT
    MOD --> OUT
    KNN --> OUT
    FFILL --> OUT
    BFILL --> OUT
    INTERP --> OUT
    MICE --> OUT
    OUT --> VAL[Validation Module]
    VAL --> FIN[Validated Clean Dataset]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Relationship Between Missing-Value Concepts

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT[Missing Value Universe] --> TYP[Three Rubin Mechanisms]
    TYP --> MCAR[MCAR Random]
    TYP --> MAR[MAR Pattern Based]
    TYP --> MNAR[MNAR Self Dependent]
    ROOT --> DET[Detection Tools]
    DET --> ISNULL[isnull Function]
    DET --> HEAT[Heatmap Visualization]

    FIX --> IMP[Imputation Family]
    DEL --> LIST[Listwise Dropna]
    DEL --> PAIR[Pairwise Available Case]

    IMP --> TIME[Time Series Aware]
    IMP --> ADV[Advanced Model Based]
    SIMP --> MN[Mean]
    SIMP --> MD[Median]
    SIMP --> MO[Mode]

    TIME --> BF[Backward Fill]
    TIME --> LI[Linear Interpolation]
```

> 2. The **Functional Architecture (4.2)** is for viva — be ready to explain any block, e.g., *"What does the Distance Matrix Builder do?"*

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **MCAR**, **MAR**, and **MNAR** mechanisms of missing data. Give one real-world example of each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **MCAR (Missing Completely At Random):** Missingness is independent of both observed and unobserved data. The probability of a value being missing is the same for all cases. *Example:* A laboratory dropper randomly breaks a few blood samples — the breakage has no relation to the patient's health or any measurement.
> - **MAR (Missing At Random):** Missingness depends only on **observed** data. *Example:* In a survey, women are less likely to disclose weight, but since gender is observed, the missingness is MAR-conditional on gender.
> - **MNAR (Missing Not At Random):** Missingness depends on the **missing value itself**. *Example:* High-income individuals deliberately hide their salary, so the probability of salary being missing increases with the actual (unobserved) salary level.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
> **Q2.** Explain why **mean imputation** underestimates the variance of a dataset. Illustrate with a formula.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Mean imputation replaces every missing value with the same constant $\bar{x}_{\text{obs}}$. This artificially **reduces the spread** of the dataset because:
> 1. The squared deviation of imputed values from the mean is always **zero** (since they equal the mean).
> 2. The squared deviations of true observations remain unchanged, but their *weight* in the variance denominator changes.
>
> Formally, if $n_1$ observed and $n_2$ imputed values exist, the biased variance becomes:
>
> $$\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{biased}} = \frac{1}{n_1 + n_2} \left[ \sum_{i \in \text{obs}} (x_i - \bar{x})^2 + \sum_{j \in \text{imp}} \underbrace{(\bar{x} - \bar{x})^2}_{= \, 0} \right]$$
>
> This systematically underestimates the true $\sigma^2$, leading to **overconfident statistical inference**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Deletion + Imputation

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PCCBL508, Model Paper Pattern]**
> **Q(A). (a)** With a suitable example, explain the **three mechanisms of missing data** as defined by Rubin. Describe how the choice of mechanism influences the imputation strategy. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(A). (b)** Given the following dataset of employee records with missing entries, perform the following tasks using **Python (Pandas)**: **(7 marks)**
>
> | EmpID | Age | Salary | Department | Experience |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 25 | 50000 | IT | 2 |
> | 2 | 30 | NaN | HR | 5 |
> | 3 | NaN | 60000 | IT | 3 |
> | 4 | 40 | 80000 | NaN | 10 |
> | 5 | 35 | 70000 | Finance | 7 |
> | 6 | NaN | NaN | HR | 4 |
> | 7 | 50 | 90000 | IT | NaN |
>
> Tasks:
> 1. Detect and quantify missing values (missing ratio per column).
> 2. Apply **mean imputation** for `Age` and `Salary`, **mode imputation** for `Department`, and **median imputation** for `Experience`.
> 3. Compare the **mean and standard deviation** of `Salary` before and after imputation. Comment on the change.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three Mechanisms (7 marks):**
For complete content, see Section 1.2 of these notes. Key points to write:
- Define Rubin's framework formally with $P(M \mid X_{\text{obs}}, X_{\text{mis}})$.
- List all three mechanisms with definitions.
- Provide examples (sensor failure for MCAR, gender-conditioned non-response for MAR, salary concealment for MNAR).
- Connect each mechanism to its valid treatment (MCAR → deletion OK; MAR → model-based imputation; MNAR → domain-specific).

**(b) Computational Tasks (7 marks):**

**Task 1 — Detection (2 marks):**
- `df.isnull().sum()` returns: Age=2, Salary=2, Department=1, Experience=1
- Total = 6 missing cells out of 35 → completeness = 1 - 6/35 ≈ **82.86%**
- Missing ratios: Age = 0.286, Salary = 0.286, Department = 0.143, Experience = 0.143

**Task 2 — Imputation Code (3 marks):**
```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

data = {'Age':[25,30,np.nan,40,35,np.nan,50],
        'Salary':[50000,np.nan,60000,80000,70000,np.nan,90000],
        'Department':['IT','HR','IT',np.nan,'Finance','HR','IT'],
        'Experience':[2,5,3,10,7,4,np.nan]}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

df['Age']        = df['Age'].fillna(df['Age'].mean())
df['Salary']     = df['Salary'].fillna(df['Salary'].mean())
df['Department'] = df['Department'].fillna(df['Department'].mode()[0])
df['Experience'] = df['Experience'].fillna(df['Experience'].median())
print(df)
```

**Task 3 — Statistical Comparison (2 marks):**
- **Before** Salary: mean = 70000 (excluding NaN), std ≈ 15811
- **After** Salary: mean = 70000, std ≈ 14142
- **Comment:** The mean is preserved (as expected with mean imputation), but the **standard deviation decreases by ~10.5%** — confirming the variance-underestimation phenomenon discussed in Section 3.1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Advanced Imputation Techniques

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PCCBL508, Model Paper Pattern]**
> **Q(B). (a)** Describe the **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) imputation** algorithm in detail. Explain how the choice of $K$ and the distance metric affect the imputed values. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q(B). (b)** Given the following time-series data of hourly temperature readings, the values at hours 3 and 6 are missing. Apply **forward fill**, **backward fill**, and **linear interpolation**. Compare the results. **(7 marks)**
>
> | Hour | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | Temp (°C) | 20 | 22 | NaN | 26 | 28 | NaN | 32 | 34 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) KNN Imputation Explanation (7 marks):**
- **Algorithm definition:** KNN imputation estimates missing values by averaging (or weighted-averaging) the corresponding feature values from the $K$ most similar complete observations in the dataset.
- **Step-by-step procedure:**
  1. For row $i$ with missing $x_{ij}$, compute Euclidean distance $d(i, k) = \sqrt{\sum_{l \neq j}(x_{il} - x_{kl})^2}$ to all complete rows $k$.
  2. Select the $K$ rows with smallest distances → $N_K(i)$.
  3. Impute $\hat{x}_{ij} = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k \in N_K(i)} x_{kj}$.
- **Effect of $K$:** Small $K$ (e.g., 1) is sensitive to noise (overfitting). Large $K$ over-smooths (underfitting). A common choice is $K = 5$.
- **Effect of distance metric:** Euclidean assumes equal feature scaling — must standardize features. Manhattan distance is more robust to outliers. Mahalanobis accounts for feature correlation.
- **When to use:** Multivariate tabular data with MAR mechanism.

**(b) Time-Series Imputation (7 marks):**

**Forward Fill Result:** $T = [20, 22, 22, 26, 28, 28, 32, 34]$
- Carries the last observed value forward. $\hat{T}_3 = T_2 = 22$, $\hat{T}_6 = T_5 = 28$.

**Backward Fill Result:** $T = [20, 22, 26, 26, 28, 32, 32, 34]$
- Carries the next observed value backward. $\hat{T}_3 = T_4 = 26$, $\hat{T}_6 = T_7 = 32$.

**Linear Interpolation Result:** $T = [20, 22, 24, 26, 28, 30, 32, 34]$
- For hour 3: $\hat{T}_3 = 22 + \frac{26-22}{4-2}(3-2) = 22 + 2 = 24$
- For hour 6: $\hat{T}_6 = 28 + \frac{32-28}{7-5}(6-5) = 28 + 2 = 30$

**Comparison Table (write this in the answer):**

| Method | $\hat{T}_3$ | $\hat{T}_6$ | Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Fill | 22 | 28 | Replicates last value |
| Backward Fill | 26 | 32 | Replicates next value |
| Linear Interpolation | **24** | **30** | **Best for smooth trends** |

**Conclusion:** Linear interpolation provides the **most accurate estimate** for this linearly increasing series because it leverages information from *both* sides of the gap, whereas ffill/bfill only use one side.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PCCBL508 Lab Exam:**
>
> 1. **Trap 1 — "Just use `dropna()` for everything":** Examiners deduct up to **4 marks** if you apply listwise deletion without justifying that MCAR holds. Always state the mechanism first.
> 2. **Trap 2 — "Mean imputation is always fine":** Mean imputation on **skewed** data (e.g., income) is wrong. Use **median** for skewed numeric features. Lose 2 marks if you don't justify.
> 3. **Trap 3 — "Code without comments":** In the lab record, a 14-mark code answer without explanatory comments gets capped at **8 marks**. Always annotate.
> 4. **Trap 4 — "No before/after validation":** The validation step (Section 3.4) is a **mandatory deliverable**. Skipping it costs **2 marks**.
> 5. **Trap 5 — "Confusing `isnull()` with `isna()`":** They are aliases in Pandas, but in viva if you cannot explain the difference between `isnull()`, `notnull()`, `dropna()`, and `fillna()`, you lose **viva marks**.
> 6. **Trap 6 — "Applying KNN without scaling":** KNN imputation uses Euclidean distance — without `StandardScaler`, features with larger scales dominate. **-2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**
>
> - [ ] **MCAR vs MAR vs MNAR** — three mechanisms by Rubin, mechanism dictates method.
> - [ ] **Detection:** Always use `df.isnull().sum()` and `df.isnull().mean()` first.
> - [ ] **Missing Ratio threshold:** Drop column if $r_j > 0.40$.
> - [ ] **Mean Imputation** — for symmetric numeric data, MCAR; *underestimates variance*.
> - [ ] **Median Imputation** — for skewed numeric data, MCAR; **robust to outliers**.
> - [ ] **Mode Imputation** — for categorical/nominal data only.
> - [ ] **Forward Fill (`ffill`)** — for time-series with stable values.
> - [ ] **Backward Fill (`bfill`)** — for time-series with future-known values.
> - [ ] **Linear Interpolation** — for smooth, continuous time-series (best for trends).
> - [ ] **KNN Imputation** — for tabular MAR data; remember to **scale features** first.
> - [ ] **Iterative Imputer (MICE)** — for multivariate MAR; uses conditional distributions.
> - [ ] **Variance bias:** Mean imputation reduces $\sigma^2$ — flag this in your validation.
> - [ ] **Completeness metric:** $C = 1 - \frac{M_{total}}{n \cdot p}$ — must appear in your report.
> - [ ] **Pandas functions to memorize:** `isnull`, `notnull`, `dropna`, `fillna`, `interpolate`, `replace`.
> - [ ] **Sklearn imputers:** `SimpleImputer`, `KNNImputer`, `IterativeImputer`.
> - [ ] **Always validate:** Compare `.describe()` output and correlation matrices before vs after.
> - [ ] **Avoid applying mean imputation to categorical columns** — it's mathematically invalid.
> - [ ] **Viva question to expect:** *"What happens if you impute before train-test split?"* — Answer: **Data leakage**. Always split first, fit imputer on train, transform test.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiments on Group By Mechanics
topics.content:
# 📘 GROUP BY MECHANICS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Experiments on Group By Mechanics --- ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Computational Statistics**, **GROUP BY mechanics** refers to a family of declarative operations (originally formalized in **Structured Query Language — SQL — ANSI/ISO Standard SQL:2003 and later**) used to partition a tabular dataset into subsets (groups) sharing identical values in one or more categorical attributes, and then apply **aggregate functions** to each subset independently. The result is a reduced dataset where each row represents a summary statistic for one group. From a statistical viewpoint, GROUP BY implements **conditional aggregation** — a discrete mapping: $$f: \mathcal{D} \longrightarrow \mathcal{G} \times \mathbb{R}^k$$ where $\mathcal{D}$ is the input dataset, $\mathcal{G}$ is the set of distinct group keys, and $\mathbb{R}^k$ is the $k$-dimensional vector of aggregate values (mean, count, variance, etc.) computed conditionally on $g \in \mathcal{G}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** GROUP BY mechanics is the *backbone* of descriptive statistical computing. Every experiment involving conditional means, frequency tables, contingency tables, and stratified sampling uses this operation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Classroom Grouping" Intuition Imagine a **school assembly of 1000 students**. The Principal wants to know the **average height per class** and the **total number of students per house**. | Step | Real-World Action | SQL Equivalent | |------|------------------|----------------| | 1 | Principal says: *"Group all students by their class"* | `GROUP BY class` | | 2 | For each class, count the students | `COUNT(*)` | | 3 | For each class, compute average height | `AVG(height)` | | 4 | Filter out groups with fewer than 30 students | `HAVING COUNT(*) > 30` | **The Principal does NOT mix classes.** Each class is treated as a **separate mini-dataset**, and the calculation happens **inside** that mini-dataset. This is exactly how GROUP BY works in SQL/Pandas/R — it creates "logical buckets" and computes one summary per bucket. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** GROUP BY = "**Split → Apply → Combine**" (the famous **Split-Apply-Combine** paradigm formalized by Hadley Wickham in 2011 for the R `dplyr` ecosystem, also native to Pandas `groupby()`). --- ## 1.3 Core Components of GROUP BY Mechanics The five pillars of any GROUP BY operation are: 1. **Grouping Key(s)** — the categorical column(s) used to partition rows. 2. **Aggregate Function(s)** — `COUNT`, `SUM`, `AVG`, `MIN`, `MAX`, `STDDEV`, `VARIANCE`. 3. **Filter Clause (`HAVING`)** — filters *groups* (post-aggregation), unlike `WHERE` which filters *rows* (pre-aggregation). 4. **Group Modifiers** — `ROLLUP`, `CUBE`, `GROUPING SETS` (multi-level summarization). 5. **Result Projection** — the `SELECT` clause determines which grouped and aggregated columns appear in the output. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** *"**W**here filters **W**edges (rows), **H**aving filters **H**eaps (groups)."* — `WHERE` runs **before** GROUP BY, `HAVING` runs **after** GROUP BY. # 📗 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Split → Apply → Combine Paradigm ### 🔹 Step 1 — SPLIT (Logical Partitioning) The engine scans the input relation $R$ and builds a hash table (or sorts the data) keyed on the grouping columns. For each unique combination of grouping-key values, a *bucket* $B_g$ is created: $$\mathcal{P}(R) = \{B_{g_1}, B_{g_2}, \ldots, B_{g_m}\}$$ where $g_i$ denotes the $i$-th unique group key, $m$ is the number of distinct groups, and: $$\bigcup_{i=1}^{m} B_{g_i} = R \quad \text{and} \quad B_{g_i} \cap B_{g_j} = \emptyset \;\; \forall i \neq j$$ ### 🔹 Step 2 — APPLY (Aggregate Computation) For each bucket $B_g$, compute one or more aggregate functions $f_k$: $$a_k(g) = f_k(B_g) = \text{aggregate}_k(\{x \in B_g : x.\text{col}_k\})$$ ### 🔹 Step 3 — COMBINE (Result Assembly) The output relation $R'$ contains exactly $m$ rows, one per group, with the grouping keys plus the computed aggregates as columns. --- ## 2.2 The Execution Order (CRITICAL for KTU) SQL logical processing order (per ANSI standard) — **memorize this sequence**: ```text 1. FROM → Identify source tables 2. WHERE → Filter rows (pre-aggregation) 3. GROUP BY → Partition rows into buckets 4. HAVING → Filter groups (post-aggregation) 5. SELECT → Project columns + compute aggregates 6. ORDER BY → Sort the final result 7. LIMIT → Trim output rows ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Using a *column alias* defined in `SELECT` inside the `HAVING` clause. In MySQL this is permitted; in standard SQL (PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQLite) it is **NOT allowed** because `HAVING` is logically evaluated *before* `SELECT`. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Aggregate Functions & Statistical Identities The following formulas are what the engine computes internally for each group $B_g$ of size $n_g$: | Aggregate Function | Formula | Output Type | NULL Handling | |--------------------|---------|-------------|---------------| | `COUNT(*)` | $n_g = \vert B_g \vert$ | Integer | Counts NULLs | | `COUNT(col)` | $\sum_{i=1}^{n_g} \mathbb{1}[x_i \neq \text{NULL}]$ | Integer | Skips NULLs | | `SUM(col)` | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n_g} x_i$ | Numeric | Skips NULLs | | `AVG(col)` | $\dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n_g} x_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n_g} \mathbb{1}[x_i \neq \text{NULL}]}$ | Numeric (float) | Skips NULLs | | `MIN(col)` | $\min\{x_i : x_i \in B_g\}$ | Same as column | Skips NULLs | | `MAX(col)` | $\max\{x_i : x_i \in B_g\}$ | Same as column | Skips NULLs | | `STDDEV_POP(col)` | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_g}\sum_{i=1}^{n_g}(x_i - \bar{x}_g)^2}$ | Float | Skips NULLs | | `STDDEV_SAMP(col)` | $\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n_g - 1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_g}(x_i - \bar{x}_g)^2}$ | Float | Skips NULLs | | `VAR_POP(col)` | $\dfrac{1}{n_g}\sum_{i=1}^{n_g}(x_i - \bar{x}_g)^2$ | Float | Skips NULLs | | `VAR_SAMP(col)` | $\dfrac{1}{n_g - 1}\sum_{i=1}^{n_g}(x_i - \bar{x}_g)^2$ | Float | Skips NULLs | > [!NOTE] > The **difference between POPULATION and SAMPLE** statistics is the denominator: $n$ vs $n-1$ (Bessel's correction). For **lab exam answers**, always state which one your engine uses (MySQL = sample by default for `STDDEV`). --- ## 2.4 Advanced Grouping Constructs (ROLLUP, CUBE, GROUPING SETS) ### 🔹 ROLLUP Computes hierarchical subtotals along a specified dimension list. For grouping keys $(A, B, C)$: $$\text{ROLLUP}(A,B,C) = \{\text{group by } (A,B,C),\;(A,B),\;(A),\;()\}$$ This produces $k+1$ levels of aggregation for $k$ columns. ### 🔹 CUBE Computes **all** $2^k$ possible subtotal combinations: $$\text{CUBE}(A,B,C) = \{\text{group by } (A,B,C),\;(A,B),\;(A,C),\;(B,C),\;(A),\;(B),\;(C),\;()\}$$ ### 🔹 GROUPING SETS Explicit specification of which groupings you want — full control: $$\text{GROUPING SETS}((A,B),(A),(B),())$$ --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Domain | GROUP BY Application | |--------|---------------------| | **Telecom** | Average call drop rate per cell tower per hour | | **Banking** | Total transaction volume per branch per day | | **IoT / Smart City** | Mean temperature per sensor per district | | **Bio-statistics** | Patient cohort comparison (mean BP by treatment group) | | **Web Analytics** | Session count per user per browser | | **Manufacturing QC** | Defect rate per machine per shift | # 📙 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — Deriving Per-Group Statistics Manually **Dataset:** `sales(region, product, amount, quantity)` | region | product | amount | quantity | |--------|---------|--------|----------| | South | Pen | 100 | 5 | | South | Pen | 200 | 10 | | South | Book | 300 | 3 | | North | Pen | 150 | 7 | | North | Book | 250 | 5 | | East | Pen | 120 | 6 | ### 🔹 Query > *"For each region, compute the total revenue, average sale amount, and number of distinct products."* ```sql SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue, AVG(amount) AS avg_sale, COUNT(DISTINCT product) AS product_variety FROM sales GROUP BY region; ``` ### 🔹 Manual Derivation for `region = 'South'` The bucket $B_{\text{South}}$ contains exactly 3 rows: $$B_{\text{South}} = \{(100,5), (200,10), (300,3)\}$$ **Total Revenue:** $$\text{SUM} = 100 + 200 + 300 = 500$$ **Average Sale:** $$\text{AVG} = \frac{100 + 200 + 300}{3} = \frac{600}{3} = 200$$ **Distinct Products:** $$\text{COUNT DISTINCT} = \vert\{\text{Pen}, \text{Book}\}\vert = 2$$ ### 🔹 Full Result Table | region | total_revenue | avg_sale | product_variety | |--------|---------------|----------|-----------------| | South | 600 | 200.00 | 2 | | North | 400 | 200.00 | 2 | | East | 120 | 120.00 | 1 | ### 🔹 SQL Equivalent Using Pandas (Lab-Ready Code) ```python import pandas as pd df = pd.DataFrame({ 'region': ['South','South','South','North','North','East'], 'product': ['Pen','Pen','Book','Pen','Book','Pen'], 'amount': [100, 200, 300, 150, 250, 120], 'quantity': [5, 10, 3, 7, 5, 6] }) result = ( df.groupby('region', as_index=False) .agg( total_revenue = ('amount', 'sum'), avg_sale = ('amount', 'mean'), product_variety = ('product', 'nunique') ) ) print(result) ``` --- ## 3.2 Derivative Work — HAVING vs WHERE (Classic KTU Trap) **Task:** *"Show only regions where average sale exceeds ₹150 **and** total quantity is greater than 10."* ```sql SELECT region, AVG(amount) AS avg_sale, SUM(quantity) AS total_qty FROM sales WHERE amount > 50 -- pre-aggregation: row filter GROUP BY region HAVING AVG(amount) > 150 -- post-aggregation: group filter AND SUM(quantity) > 10; ``` ### 🔹 Step-by-Step Logical Flow **Step 1 — Apply WHERE `amount > 50`:** The row `(East, Pen, 120, 6)` survives (since 120 > 50). All 6 rows pass this filter in this small example. **Step 2 — Group by `region`:** $$B_{\text{South}} = \{(100,5),(200,10),(300,3)\}$$ $$B_{\text{North}} = \{(150,7),(250,5)\}$$ $$B_{\text{East}} = \{(120,6)\}$$ **Step 3 — Compute aggregates per bucket:** | region | avg_sale | total_qty | |--------|----------|-----------| | South | 200.00 | 18 | | North | 200.00 | 12 | | East | 120.00 | 6 | **Step 4 — Apply HAVING (avg_sale > 150 AND total_qty > 10):** - South: 200 > 150 ✓ and 18 > 10 ✓ → **kept** - North: 200 > 150 ✓ and 12 > 10 ✓ → **kept** - East: 120 > 150 ✗ → **dropped** **Final Output:** South, North. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — GROUP BY Variants at a Glance | Variant | Number of Output Rows (for $k$ group cols, $n$ distinct values) | NULL Treated As | Subtotals? | |---------|------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------|------------| | `GROUP BY` | $\prod_{i=1}^{k} n_i$ (Cartesian of distinct values) | A valid group | No | | `ROLLUP` | $k+1$ levels of subtotals | A valid group | Yes (hierarchical) | | `CUBE` | $2^k$ combinations | A valid group | Yes (all combinations) | | `GROUPING SETS` | User-defined | A valid group | User-defined | ### 🔹 ROLLUP Worked Example For `(region, product)`: ```sql SELECT region, product, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales GROUP BY ROLLUP(region, product); ``` This produces subtotal rows where `region = NULL` (product subtotal) and `region = NULL, product = NULL` (grand total). ### 🔹 GROUPING() Function To distinguish NULLs that come from the data vs. NULLs from ROLLUP/CUBE subtotals: $$\text{GROUPING}(col) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } col \text{ is NULL due to aggregation} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always use `COALESCE(col, 'ALL')` to label subtotal rows meaningfully. Examiners love seeing this in viva. --- ## 3.4 Derivation — Why GROUP BY Cannot Reference Non-Aggregated, Non-Grouped Columns Consider the following **invalid** query: ```sql SELECT region, product, AVG(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY region; -- product is NOT in GROUP BY and NOT aggregated! ``` **Reasoning:** For each region group, there may be multiple distinct `product` values. The engine cannot return one product per region since multiple exist — this violates **single-valued logic per row**. Hence SQL strictly forbids it (except under `ANY_VALUE()` in MySQL or functional dependency inference in some engines). The formal constraint enforced is: $$\text{Every column in SELECT} \in \text{GROUP BY columns} \cup \text{Aggregate functions}$$ # 📕 Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Split → Apply → Combine Flow (Core Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INPUT DATASET
sales table
6 rows"] --> B["STEP 1 SPLIT
Partition by region key"] B --> C1["BUCKET South
3 rows"] B --> C2["BUCKET North
2 rows"] B --> C3["BUCKET East
1 row"] C1 --> D1["STEP 2 APPLY
SUM AVG COUNT DISTINCT"] C2 --> D2["STEP 2 APPLY
SUM AVG COUNT DISTINCT"] C3 --> D3["STEP 2 APPLY
SUM AVG COUNT DISTINCT"] D1 --> E1["COMBINE row 1
South 600 200 2"] D2 --> E2["COMBINE row 2
North 400 200 2"] D3 --> E3["COMBINE row 3
East 120 120 1"] E1 --> F["OUTPUT RESULT SET
3 summary rows"] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G["OPTIONAL HAVING
Filter groups"] G --> H["OPTIONAL ORDER BY
Sort final result"] ``` --- ## 4.2 SQL Logical Processing Order (Sequential Phases) ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["1 FROM"] --> P2["2 JOIN"] P2 --> P3["3 WHERE"] P3 --> P4["4 GROUP BY"] P4 --> P5["5 HAVING"] P5 --> P6["6 SELECT"] P6 --> P7["7 DISTINCT"] P7 --> P8["8 ORDER BY"] P8 --> P9["9 LIMIT OFFSET"] style P4 fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P5 fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style P6 fill:#118ab2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.3 ROLLUP vs CUBE — Subtotal Hierarchy Visualization ```mermaid graph TD Root["GRAND TOTAL
SUM over all rows"] --> L1A["Subtotal by region"] Root --> L1B["Subtotal by product"] Root --> L1C["Subtotal by region + product"] L1A --> L2A1["region South only"] L1A --> L2A2["region North only"] L1A --> L2A3["region East only"] L1B --> L2B1["product Pen only"] L1B --> L2B2["product Book only"] L1C --> L3A["South Pen subtotal"] L1C --> L3B["South Book subtotal"] L1C --> L3C["North Pen subtotal"] L1C --> L3D["North Book subtotal"] L1C --> L3E["East Pen subtotal"] style Root fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,color:#fff style L1A fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L1B fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,color:#000 style L1C fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,color:#000 ``` > The above graph represents a **CUBE** expansion. A **ROLLUP** would omit the standalone `product` branch and the cross combinations, keeping only the hierarchical path: `(region, product) → (region) → ()`. --- ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Lab System) ```mermaid flowchart TD SRC["RAW DATA SOURCE
CSV or RDBMS Table"] --> ING["DATA INGESTION
Pandas read_sql or read_csv"] ING --> CLEAN["DATA CLEANING
Drop NA Convert Dtypes"] CLEAN --> GB["GROUPBY ENGINE
Pandas groupby
or SQL GROUP BY"] GB --> AGG["AGGREGATION LAYER
sum mean count std"] AGG --> HAV["HAVING EQUIVALENT
Filter on aggregates"] HAV --> SORT["ORDER BY SORT"] SORT --> OUT["FINAL DATAFRAME
or RESULT TABLE"] OUT --> VIZ["OPTIONAL VISUALIZATION
Matplotlib Bar Chart"] ``` # 📓 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between the `WHERE` and `HAVING` clauses in SQL with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | `WHERE` | `HAVING` | |--------|---------|----------| | **When executed** | **Before** GROUP BY | **After** GROUP BY | | **Operates on** | Individual **rows** | **Groups** (aggregated rows) | | **Can use aggregate functions?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | **Purpose** | Filter raw records | Filter summarized groups | **Example:** ```sql -- WHERE filters ROWS before grouping -- HAVING filters GROUPS after aggregation SELECT department, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal FROM employees WHERE hire_year >= 2020 -- filter rows GROUP BY department HAVING AVG(salary) > 50000; -- filter groups ``` The `WHERE` clause removes employees hired before 2020 first; then grouping happens; then `HAVING` keeps only those departments whose average exceeds ₹50,000. --- ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]` **Explain the Split-Apply-Combine paradigm in the context of GROUP BY operations.** **Model Answer:** The Split-Apply-Combine paradigm, popularized by Hadley Wickham, describes the logical three-phase operation performed by any GROUP BY mechanism: 1. **Split:** Divide the dataset into groups based on one or more key columns. Each unique combination of key values forms a bucket. 2. **Apply:** Independently apply one or more aggregate functions (SUM, AVG, COUNT, MIN, MAX, STDDEV) to each bucket. 3. **Combine:** Assemble the per-group summary results back into a single output dataset, where each row corresponds to one input group plus its aggregates. This paradigm is implemented natively in R (`dplyr`), Python (`pandas.groupby`), and SQL (`GROUP BY` clause). --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`** #### (a) **Consider the following table `orders(order_id, customer_id, product_category, order_date, amount)`. Write SQL queries to solve the following sub-problems. (7 marks)** **Dataset snapshot:** | order_id | customer_id | product_category | order_date | amount | |----------|-------------|------------------|-------------|--------| | 1 | C1 | Electronics | 2024-01-15 | 1500 | | 2 | C2 | Clothing | 2024-02-10 | 800 | | 3 | C1 | Electronics | 2024-02-12 | 2200 | | 4 | C3 | Books | 2024-03-05 | 400 | | 5 | C2 | Clothing | 2024-03-18 | 1200 | | 6 | C1 | Books | 2024-04-02 | 600 | | 7 | C4 | Electronics | 2024-04-22 | 3000 | | 8 | C3 | Clothing | 2024-05-09 | 950 | **Sub-problems:** **(i)** Find the total order amount for each product category. **(ii)** Find the number of unique customers in each product category. **(iii)** Find product categories where the total amount exceeds ₹3000. **Model Solution:** **(i)** Total order amount per category: ```sql SELECT product_category, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM orders GROUP BY product_category; ``` **Manual verification:** $$B_{\text{Electronics}} = \{1500, 2200, 3000\} \Rightarrow \text{SUM} = 6700$$ $$B_{\text{Clothing}} = \{800, 1200, 950\} \Rightarrow \text{SUM} = 2950$$ $$B_{\text{Books}} = \{400, 600\} \Rightarrow \text{SUM} = 1000$$ | product_category | total_amount | |------------------|--------------| | Electronics | 6700 | | Clothing | 2950 | | Books | 1000 | **(ii)** Number of unique customers per category: ```sql SELECT product_category, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS unique_customers FROM orders GROUP BY product_category; ``` | product_category | unique_customers | |------------------|------------------| | Electronics | 3 | | Clothing | 2 | | Books | 2 | **(iii)** Categories where total exceeds ₹3000: ```sql SELECT product_category, SUM(amount) AS total_amount FROM orders GROUP BY product_category HAVING SUM(amount) > 3000; ``` **Output:** Only `Electronics` (₹6700) qualifies. #### (b) **Demonstrate the use of `ROLLUP` on the `orders` table to generate category-wise and grand-total summaries. Use the `GROUPING()` function to label subtotal rows. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```sql SELECT CASE WHEN GROUPING(product_category) = 1 THEN 'GRAND TOTAL' ELSE product_category END AS category_label, SUM(amount) AS total_amount, COUNT(*) AS order_count FROM orders GROUP BY ROLLUP(product_category); ``` **Logical derivation:** ROLLUP on a single column produces two levels: - Level 1: `GROUP BY product_category` → 3 rows (Electronics, Clothing, Books) - Level 2: `()` → 1 row (Grand Total) **Expected output:** | category_label | total_amount | order_count | |----------------|--------------|-------------| | Books | 1000 | 2 | | Clothing | 2950 | 3 | | Electronics | 6700 | 3 | | GRAND TOTAL | 10650 | 8 | **Verification of grand total:** $$\text{SUM}_{\text{grand}} = 6700 + 2950 + 1000 = 10650$$ $$\text{COUNT}_{\text{grand}} = 3 + 3 + 2 = 8 \;\checkmark$$ --- ### **Question B — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` (ALTERNATIVE)** #### (a) **For the `orders` table above, write SQL queries using GROUP BY to find: (7 marks)** **(i)** The average order amount per customer. **(ii)** The top 2 customers by total spending. **(iii)** Customers whose average order value is greater than ₹1000. **Model Solution:** **(i)** Average order amount per customer: ```sql SELECT customer_id, AVG(amount) AS avg_order_value FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id; ``` **Manual derivation for C1:** $$B_{C1} = \{1500, 2200, 600\} \Rightarrow \text{AVG} = \frac{4300}{3} \approx 1433.33$$ | customer_id | avg_order_value | |-------------|-----------------| | C1 | 1433.33 | | C2 | 1000.00 | | C3 | 675.00 | | C4 | 3000.00 | **(ii)** Top 2 customers by total spending: ```sql SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount) AS total_spent FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY total_spent DESC LIMIT 2; ``` **Manual sums:** - C1: 1500 + 2200 + 600 = 4300 - C2: 800 + 1200 = 2000 - C3: 400 + 950 = 1350 - C4: 3000 | customer_id | total_spent | |-------------|-------------| | C1 | 4300 | | C4 | 3000 | **(iii)** Customers whose average order value > ₹1000: ```sql SELECT customer_id, AVG(amount) AS avg_val FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id HAVING AVG(amount) > 1000; ``` **Output:** C1 (1433.33), C4 (3000). #### (b) **Using Pandas (Python), implement the same aggregations as Question A part (a) on a DataFrame version of the `orders` table. Show the code, output, and explain any functional differences from SQL. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** ```python import pandas as pd orders = pd.DataFrame({ 'order_id': [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8], 'customer_id': ['C1','C2','C1','C3','C2','C1','C4','C3'], 'product_category': ['Electronics','Clothing','Electronics', 'Books','Clothing','Books', 'Electronics','Clothing'], 'order_date': pd.to_datetime([ '2024-01-15','2024-02-10','2024-02-12','2024-03-05', '2024-03-18','2024-04-02','2024-04-22','2024-05-09' ]), 'amount': [1500, 800, 2200, 400, 1200, 600, 3000, 950] }) # (i) Total per category print("=== Total per category ===") print(orders.groupby('product_category')['amount'].sum()) # (ii) Unique customers per category print("\n=== Unique customers per category ===") print(orders.groupby('product_category')['customer_id'].nunique()) # (iii) Categories with total > 3000 print("\n=== Categories with total > 3000 ===") result = (orders.groupby('product_category')['amount'] .sum() .reset_index() .query('amount > 3000')) print(result) ``` **Expected Output:** ``` === Total per category === product_category Books 1000 Clothing 2950 Electronics 6700 Name: amount, dtype: int64 === Unique customers per category === product_category Books 2 Clothing 2 Electronics 3 Name: customer_id, dtype: int64 === Categories with total > 3000 === product_category amount 0 Electronics 6700 ``` **Functional differences from SQL:** | Aspect | SQL | Pandas | |--------|-----|--------| | `HAVING` equivalent | `HAVING` clause | `.query()` or boolean masking post-groupby | | NULL handling | Strict (ANSI) | `NaN` excluded by default; use `dropna=False` | | Multi-level grouping | `ROLLUP`, `CUBE` | `groupby([...]).agg()` with multiple keys; manual subtotals | | Result type | Result set (rows) | DataFrame or Series | --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE marks in GROUP BY questions:** > > 1. **Confusing WHERE and HAVING (2–3 mark deduction):** A student who puts `AVG(salary) > 50000` inside `WHERE` will receive ZERO because aggregates cannot be used there. > 2. **Forgetting to GROUP BY a non-aggregated SELECT column:** Results in a syntax error or worse, an unpredictable behavior — instant 1–2 mark cut. > 3. **Using `ORDER BY` column alias inside `HAVING`:** Allowed in MySQL but NOT in PostgreSQL/Oracle — students must know the dialect. > 4. **NULL treatment in `COUNT` vs `COUNT(*)`:** `COUNT(col)` skips NULLs; `COUNT(*)` counts them. Examiners LOVE this trick question. > 5. **Not using `COALESCE` or `GROUPING()` to label subtotal rows** in ROLLUP/CUBE outputs — costs the "presentation" marks. > 6. **In Pandas:** Forgetting `.reset_index()` after `groupby()` results in a Series with grouped index, which often surprises students. Always reset for clean tabular output. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔑 **GROUP BY** partitions rows into buckets sharing the same key value(s); one summary row per bucket is produced. - 🔑 **Execution order:** `FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY → LIMIT`. - 🔑 **`WHERE` filters rows; `HAVING` filters groups.** This is the single most tested distinction. - 🔑 Every non-aggregated column in `SELECT` **must** appear in `GROUP BY`. - 🔑 **Aggregate functions:** `COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX, STDDEV, VARIANCE` — know their NULL behavior. - 🔑 **ROLLUP** = hierarchical subtotals; **CUBE** = all $2^k$ subtotal combinations; **GROUPING SETS** = user-specified. - 🔑 **`GROUPING(col)`** returns 1 for NULLs from aggregation, 0 for data NULLs — use it to label subtotal rows clearly. - 🔑 **Sample vs Population** standard deviation differs by $n$ vs $n-1$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction). - 🔑 In **Pandas**, `groupby()` returns a `DataFrameGroupBy` object — chain `.agg()`, `.sum()`, `.mean()`, or `.nunique()` to get results; use `.reset_index()` for clean output. - 🔑 Always use **`COALESCE(col, 'LABEL')`** in SQL to make ROLLUP/CUBE subtotal rows human-readable. - 🔑 **Split → Apply → Combine** is the conceptual backbone — describe your solution using these three words in viva to score bonus points.

topics.title: Data Aggregation, Groupwise Operations and Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Data Aggregation, Groupwise Operations and Transformations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Aggregation** is the computational process of transforming a granular, row-level dataset into a summarized, higher-level form by applying summary statistical functions (such as `sum`, `mean`, `median`, `count`, `std`, `var`, `min`, `max`, `first`, `last`) along one or more grouping axes.

**Groupwise Operations** refer to the *Split–Apply–Combine* paradigm formalized by Hadley Wickham (2011), wherein a dataset is:
1. **Split** into chunks based on the values of one or more *key* columns,
2. **Apply** an aggregation, transformation, or filtration function independently to each chunk, and
3. **Combine** the per-chunk results back into a unified tabular structure.

**Transformations** are a specialized form of groupwise operation that broadcasts a function back to the **original row indices**, producing an output of the *same shape* as the input. This is in contrast to aggregation, which collapses rows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Python, all three operations are implemented primarily through the pandas library (`pandas.DataFrame.groupby()`), which is the de-facto industry and academic tool for tabular data manipulation. The KTU lab rubric for **Experiment 2** explicitly tests the ability to use `groupby()`, `agg()`, `transform()`, `apply()`, `pivot_table()`, and `crosstab()`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam Paper Sorter"

Imagine you are a professor with **1,000 answer sheets** for a class of 200 students, each student having answered **5 subjects** (English, Maths, Physics, Chemistry, Computer Science). You want to find the **average mark per subject per student**.

| Step | Real-World Action | pandas Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Split | Sort the answer sheets into **student-wise piles**, then within each pile, into **subject-wise sub-piles** | `df.groupby(['Student', 'Subject'])` |
| Apply | For each sub-pile, calculate the **mean of the marks** | `.mean()` or `.agg('mean')` |
| Combine | Paste the results into a neat **marks register table** | pandas returns a new DataFrame |

This is *exactly* what `groupby()` does — but in milliseconds instead of hours.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory KTU Vocabulary to Memorize:**
> - **GroupBy object** — A lazy, intermediate pandas object; computation is deferred until an aggregate/transform/apply method is called.
> - **Aggregation (`agg`)** — Produces a result with **fewer rows** than the input (e.g., 1000 rows → 5 rows for 5 subjects).
> - **Transformation (`transform`)** — Produces a result with the **same number of rows** as the input.
> - **Filtration (`filter`)** — Drops entire groups based on a group-level boolean condition.
> - **Pivot Table** — A 2D generalization of groupby: groups by both row-keys *and* column-keys.
> - **Cross-Tabulation** — Computes the frequency of co-occurrence between two or more categorical variables.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Retail Analytics:** Computing total revenue per store, per product category, per quarter.
- **IoT/Sensor Networks:** Averaging temperature readings per sensor per hour.
- **Bioinformatics:** Aggregating gene expression levels per tissue type.
- **Finance:** Computing Value-at-Risk (VaR) per asset class.
- **Web Analytics:** Counting unique users (`nunique`) per page, per country, per device.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Split–Apply–Combine Paradigm (Wickham, 2011)

The paradigm is implemented through three logical phases. In pandas, all three are usually written in a single chained expression:

```python
result = (df
    .groupby(key_columns)      # 1. SPLIT
    .agg_function()             # 2. APPLY
)                               # 3. COMBINE happens automatically
```

**Phase 1 — SPLIT:** pandas internally constructs a dictionary-like mapping `{group_key: index_array}`. For a DataFrame with `N` rows and `k` unique group keys, the time complexity is **O(N)** for hashing, and the space complexity is **O(N + k)**.

**Phase 2 — APPLY:** For each group, the chosen function is applied. For built-in aggregations, pandas uses optimized Cython paths; for user-defined functions, it falls back to a Python-level loop.

**Phase 3 — COMBINE:** Results are stitched together. The output index is the unique group keys. Multi-level (hierarchical) indices arise when grouping by multiple columns.

### 2.2 Aggregation vs. Transformation vs. Filtration — The Core Distinction

| Property | `agg()` | `transform()` | `filter()` |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Output shape** | Smaller (collapsed) | Same as input | Smaller (groups dropped) |
| **Output index** | Group keys | Original row index | Original row index (subset) |
| **Return value** | Scalar per group | Array of input length per group | Boolean (group-level) |
| **Common use** | Reports, summaries | Feature engineering (e.g., z-scores) | Removing noisy groups |
| **Broadcasting** | No | Yes | N/A |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Aggregation & Transformation Mathematics

The following table summarizes the canonical statistical functions that KTU examiners expect students to recognize, write, and apply. **All formulas use sample statistics** (denominator `n` or `n-1` for sample std/var).

| Function Name | Mathematical Definition | pandas String | Output Type | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Count | $\vert G \vert$ | `'count'` | int | Number of non-null observations per group |
| Sum | $\sum_{i \in G} x_i$ | `'sum'` | numeric | Totals (e.g., revenue) |
| Mean | $\bar{x}_G = \dfrac{1}{\vert G \vert}\sum_{i \in G} x_i$ | `'mean'` | float | Average |
| Median | $\text{median}(G)$ | `'median'` | numeric | Robust central tendency |
| Std Dev (sample) | $s_G = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\vert G \vert - 1}\sum_{i \in G}(x_i - \bar{x}_G)^2}$ | `'std'` | float | Spread (Bessel-corrected) |
| Variance (sample) | $s_G^2 = \dfrac{1}{\vert G \vert - 1}\sum_{i \in G}(x_i - \bar{x}_G)^2$ | `'var'` | float | Spread squared |
| Min / Max | $\min(G)$, $\max(G)$ | `'min'`, `'max'` | numeric | Range bounds |
| First / Last | $x_{(1)}$, $x_{(\vert G \vert)}$ | `'first'`, `'last'` | any | Picking representative values |
| nunique | $\vert \{x : x \in G\} \vert$ | `'nunique'` | int | Distinct count |
| Group Z-score (transform) | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \bar{x}_G}{s_G}$ | `lambda x: (x - x.mean()) / x.std()` | float | Outlier detection per group |
| Group Rank (transform) | $\text{rank}(x_i \text{ within } G)$ | `lambda x: x.rank()` | float | Within-group ordinal position |
| Group Normalize (transform) | $\tilde{x}_i = \dfrac{x_i - \min(G)}{\max(G) - \min(G)}$ | `lambda x: (x - x.min()) / (x.max() - x.min())` | float $\in [0,1]$ | Min-max scaling per group |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** When asked to "compute groupwise statistics", always state the formula **AND** the pandas implementation. Marks are awarded for both the mathematical correctness and the coding syntax. Use the `\vert` notation for cardinality (e.g., $\vert G \vert$) to comply with KTU's LaTeX-rendering rubric.

### 2.4 Pivot Tables and Cross-Tabulation — The 2D Generalization

A **pivot table** generalizes `groupby().agg()` to **two grouping axes**: rows (index) and columns. The mathematical formulation is:

$$
T_{ij} = \text{agg}\big(\{x_k : \text{row}_k = i \;\land\; \text{col}_k = j\}\big)
$$

A **cross-tabulation** (contingency table) is a special pivot where the aggregation is always `count` (or `sum` of an indicator):

$$
C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}[\text{row}_k = i \;\land\; \text{col}_k = j]
$$

where $\mathbb{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function ($\mathbb{1}=1$ if the condition is true, else $0$).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Supply Chain:** Pivot table of `demand(region, product, month)` is the backbone of any logistics dashboard.
- **Quality Control:** Cross-tabulation of `defect_type` × `shift` reveals which shift has the highest defect rate.
- **Telecommunications:** Groupby `cell_tower` then transform with z-score to detect anomalous signal strength per tower.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Setup — The Running Example Dataset
