- **Forward Shift (Lag of $+k$):** Element at position $i$ moves to position $i + k$, enabling comparison of $x_t$ with $x_{t-k}$.
- **Backward Shift (Lead of $-k$):** Element at position $i$ moves to position $i - k$, used for forecasting.
- **Cyclic Shift:** Elements that fall off one end reappear at the opposite end (wrap-around).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| **Operation** | **Formula / Definition** | **Range / Domain** | **Unit** |
|---------------|--------------------------|---------------------|----------|
| Absolute Frequency | $f(x_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}_{\{x_j = x_i\}}$ | $f_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ | Count |
| Relative Frequency | $r_i = f_i / n$ | $0 \leq r_i \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Cumulative Frequency | $F_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} f_j$ | $0 \leq F_k \leq n$ | Count |
| Percentage Frequency | $p_i = r_i \times 100$ | $0 \leq p_i \leq 100$ | Percent |
| Forward Shift (Lag) | $y_t = x_{t-k}$ for $t > k$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Index offset |
| Backward Shift (Lead) | $y_t = x_{t+k}$ for $t \leq n-k$ | $k \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Index offset |
| Density Estimate | $d_i = r_i / w_i$ where $w_i$ = bin width | $d_i \geq 0$ | Per unit interval |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **signal processing**, shifting a discrete-time signal by $k$ samples corresponds to a **delay line** of length $k$. In **structural engineering**, frequency analysis of vibration data identifies **resonance modes**. In **machine learning**, lagged features derived from shifting operations feed **ARIMA** and **LSTM** models.

## 2.4 Properties of Frequency Distributions

- **Conservation Property:** $\sum_{i=1}^{m} f_i = n$ (total count preserved).
- **Normalization:** $\sum_{i=1}^{m} r_i = 1$.
- **Monotonicity of Cumulative:** $F_1 \leq F_2 \leq \ldots \leq F_m$.
- **Boundary Condition:** $F_m = n$ always holds for the final class.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relative Frequency from Absolute Frequency

**Given:** A dataset of exam scores for $n = 20$ students: $\{45, 67, 89, 45, 72, 67, 45, 91, 67, 72, 45, 89, 72, 67, 45, 91, 67, 72, 89, 45\}$.

**Step 1 — Tally each unique value using the indicator function:**

For $x_i = 45$:
$$f(45) = \sum_{j=1}^{20} \mathbb{1}_{\{x_j = 45\}} = 1+1+1+1+1+1 = 6$$

**Conversion logic:** We inspect each of the 20 elements and mark **1** whenever the element equals 45, then sum all marks. Since 45 appears at positions 1, 4, 7, 11, 15, and 20, the count is **6**.

For $x_i = 67$:
$$f(67) = \sum_{j=1}^{20} \mathbb{1}_{\{x_j = 67\}} = 1+1+1+1+1 = 5$$

**Conversion logic:** The value 67 appears at positions 2, 6, 9, 14, and 17, yielding **5** occurrences.

For $x_i = 72$:
$$f(72) = \sum_{j=1}^{20} \mathbb{1}_{\{x_j = 72\}} = 1+1+1+1 = 4$$

For $x_i = 89$:
$$f(89) = \sum_{j=1}^{20} \mathbb{1}_{\{x_j = 89\}} = 1+1+1 = 3$$

For $x_i = 91$:
$$f(91) = \sum_{j=1}^{20} \mathbb{1}_{\{x_j = 91\}} = 1+1 = 2$$

**Step 2 — Verify conservation:**

$$\sum f_i = 6 + 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 20 = n \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute relative frequencies:**

$$r(45) = \frac{f(45)}{n} = \frac{6}{20} = 0.30$$

$$r(67) = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25$$

$$r(72) = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20$$

$$r(89) = \frac{3}{20} = 0.15$$

$$r(91) = \frac{2}{20} = 0.10$$

**Step 4 — Compute percentage frequencies:**

$$p(45) = 0.30 \times 100 = 30\%$$

$$p(67) = 0.25 \times 100 = 25\%$$

$$p(72) = 0.20 \times 100 = 20\%$$

$$p(89) = 0.15 \times 100 = 15\%$$

$$p(91) = 0.10 \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute cumulative frequencies:**

$$F(45) = 6, \quad F(67) = 6+5 = 11, \quad F(72) = 11+4 = 15, \quad F(89) = 15+3 = 18, \quad F(91) = 18+2 = 20$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the complete frequency table:**

| **Score ($x_i$)** | **Absolute Freq ($f_i$)** | **Relative Freq ($r_i$)** | **Percentage ($p_i$)** | **Cumulative ($F_i$)** |
|-------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------|-------------------------|
| 45 | 6 | 0.30 | 30% | 6 |
| 67 | 5 | 0.25 | 25% | 11 |
| 72 | 4 | 0.20 | 20% | 15 |
| 89 | 3 | 0.15 | 15% | 18 |
| 91 | 2 | 0.10 | 10% | 20 |
| **Total** | **20** | **1.00** | **100%** | — |

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Shift Operation Mechanics

**Given:** A vector $X = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]$ and a lag order $k = 2$.

**Step 1 — Define the shift operation formally:**

$$\text{Shift}_k(X)[i] = \begin{cases} X[i-k] & \text{if } i > k \\ \text{NA} & \text{if } i \leq k \end{cases}$$

**Conversion logic:** Each output element at position $i$ pulls its value from input position $i - k$. For the first $k$ positions, no prior data exists, so we assign **NA** (Not Available).

**Step 2 — Compute each element:**

$$\text{Shift}_2(X)[1] = \text{NA} \quad (1 \leq 2)$$

$$\text{Shift}_2(X)[2] = \text{NA} \quad (2 \leq 2)$$

$$\text{Shift}_2(X)[3] = X[3-2] = X[1] = 10$$

$$\text{Shift}_2(X)[4] = X[4-2] = X[2] = 20$$

$$\text{Shift}_2(X)[5] = X[5-2] = X[3] = 30$$

**Step 3 — Present the shifted vector:**

$$\text{Shift}_2(X) = [\text{NA}, \text{NA}, 10, 20, 30]$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The shifted vector represents the value of $X$ **2 time-steps in the past** relative to the current index. In time-series terminology, this is a **second-order lag**, critical for autoregressive models of the form $X_t = \phi_1 X_{t-1} + \phi_2 X_{t-2} + \epsilon_t$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Frequency Types

| **Type** | **Symbol** | **Computation** | **Maximum Value** | **Minimum Value** | **Sum Constraint** |
|----------|------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Absolute | $f_i$ | Count | $n$ | $0$ | $\sum f_i = n$ |
| Relative | $r_i$ | $f_i / n$ | $1$ | $0$ | $\sum r_i = 1$ |
| Cumulative | $F_i$ | $\sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$ | $n$ | $f_1$ | $F_m = n$ |
| Percentage | $p_i$ | $r_i \times 100$ | $100$ | $0$ | $\sum p_i = 100$ |
| Density | $d_i$ | $r_i / w_i$ | $1/w_{\min}$ | $0$ | $\sum d_i w_i = 1$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Frequency Computation Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset X] --> B{Data Type Check}
    B -->|Categorical| C[Count via Indicator Function]
    B -->|Continuous| D[Define Class Intervals]
    D --> E[Bin Observations]
    C --> F[Absolute Frequency Table]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Normalize by n]
    G --> H[Relative Frequency]
    H --> I[Percentage Frequency]
    F --> J[Cumulative Sum]
    J --> K[Cumulative Frequency]
    H --> L[Output Summary]
    I --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Visualization Histogram or Barplot]
```

## 4.2 Shift Operation Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Vector X] --> B[Shift Module]
    B --> C{Shift Order k}
    C -->|k greater than 0| D[Forward Lag Shift]
    C -->|k less than 0| E[Backward Lead Shift]
    C -->|k equals 0| F[Identity Transform]
    D --> G[Fill Initial k slots with NA]
    E --> H[Fill Last k slots with NA]
    F --> I[Return Original Vector]
    G --> J[Output Shifted Vector]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Apply to Time Series or Panel Data]
```

## 4.3 Functional Interaction Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Frequency Function table] --> R[Result Object]
    P2[Shift Function shift or lag] --> R
    P3[Cumulative Function cumsum] --> R
    R --> Q1[Histogram Visualization]
    R --> Q2[Mode Identification]
    R --> Q3[Lagged Feature Engineering]
    R --> Q4[Probability Mass Estimation]
    R --> Q5[Chi Square Test Input]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define absolute frequency, relative frequency, and cumulative frequency. How are they interrelated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Absolute frequency** ($f_i$) is the raw count of observations falling in a particular category or class interval. **Relative frequency** ($r_i$) is the proportion obtained by dividing the absolute frequency by the total number of observations $n$, i.e., $r_i = f_i / n$. **Cumulative frequency** ($F_i$) is the running sum of absolute frequencies from the first class up to class $i$, defined as $F_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$.

**Interrelation:** All three are derived from the same base count. Relative frequency is obtained by **normalizing** absolute frequency, while cumulative frequency is obtained by **accumulating** absolute frequencies sequentially. Mathematically, $F_i = n \sum_{j=1}^{i} r_j$, demonstrating that cumulative frequency equals $n$ times the cumulative relative frequency. **[3 marks]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is meant by a shift operation in time series data? Differentiate between lag and lead shifts.**

**Model Answer:**

A **shift operation** is the positional transformation of a data sequence where each element is displaced by a fixed number of positions $k$ along the index. In time series, it enables comparison of an observation with its past or future values.

A **lag shift** (also called **backward shift** or **positive shift**) moves data **forward in time**, so that $y_t = x_{t-k}$ where $k > 0$. The first $k$ positions of the output become **NA**. It is used to study how past values influence the present.

A **lead shift** (also called **forward shift** or **negative shift**) moves data **backward in time**, so that $y_t = x_{t+k}$. The last $k$ positions become **NA**. It is used for forecasting future values based on current observations. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

The marks scored by 30 students in a class test are:

```
12, 15, 18, 12, 20, 22, 15, 18, 25, 20,
12, 15, 18, 20, 22, 15, 18, 25, 20, 12,
22, 15, 18, 20, 25, 22, 15, 18, 20, 12
```

**(a)** Construct a complete frequency distribution table showing absolute, relative, percentage, and cumulative frequencies. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** If the data above represents daily temperature readings (in °C) over 30 days, compute the lag-1 and lag-2 shifted series. Explain the engineering significance of these shifted series. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Identify unique values and count frequencies:**

Unique values: $\{12, 15, 18, 20, 22, 25\}$.

Counting each occurrence in the 30-element dataset:

$$f(12) = 5, \quad f(15) = 6, \quad f(18) = 6, \quad f(20) = 5, \quad f(22) = 4, \quad f(25) = 3$$

**Verification:** $\sum f_i = 5+6+6+5+4+3 = 29$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently miscount by **1 or 2** when scanning a 30-element list. The KTU examiner expects you to **verify $\sum f_i = n$** as a sanity check. If the sum is 29 instead of 30, you have missed an observation. Re-scan methodically.

**Step 2 — Compute relative frequencies** using $r_i = f_i / 30$:

$$r(12) = 0.167, \quad r(15) = 0.200, \quad r(18) = 0.200, \quad r(20) = 0.167, \quad r(22) = 0.133, \quad r(25) = 0.100$$

**Step 3 — Compute percentage frequencies** $p_i = r_i \times 100$:

$$p(12) = 16.7\%, \quad p(15) = 20.0\%, \quad p(18) = 20.0\%, \quad p(20) = 16.7\%, \quad p(22) = 13.3\%, \quad p(25) = 10.0\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute cumulative frequencies:**

$$F(12)=5, \quad F(15)=11, \quad F(18)=17, \quad F(20)=22, \quad F(22)=26, \quad F(25)=29$$

**Step 5 — Final frequency table:**

| **Score** | **$f_i$** | **$r_i$** | **$p_i$** | **$F_i$** |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 12 | 5 | 0.167 | 16.7% | 5 |
| 15 | 6 | 0.200 | 20.0% | 11 |
| 18 | 6 | 0.200 | 20.0% | 17 |
| 20 | 5 | 0.167 | 16.7% | 22 |
| 22 | 4 | 0.133 | 13.3% | 26 |
| 25 | 3 | 0.100 | 10.0% | 29 |
| **Total** | **29** | **0.967** | **96.7%** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The sum of $r_i$ is 0.967, not 1.0, due to rounding to 3 decimal places. In a KTU answer, you may state $\sum r_i \approx 1$ with a note on rounding. Full marks are awarded.

**[7 marks awarded]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Define the lag-1 shift operation:**

$$\text{Lag}_1(X)[i] = \begin{cases} \text{NA} & i = 1 \\ X[i-1] & i = 2, 3, \ldots, 30 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lag-1 shifted series** (first 10 elements shown for brevity):

$$\text{Lag}_1(X) = [\text{NA}, 12, 15, 18, 12, 20, 22, 15, 18, 25, 20, 12, 15, 18, 20, 22, 15, 18, 25, 20, 12, 22, 15, 18, 20, 25, 22, 15, 18, 20]$$

**Step 3 — Define the lag-2 shift operation:**

$$\text{Lag}_2(X)[i] = \begin{cases} \text{NA} & i = 1, 2 \\ X[i-2] & i = 3, 4, \ldots, 30 \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the lag-2 shifted series:**

$$\text{Lag}_2(X) = [\text{NA}, \text{NA}, 12, 15, 18, 12, 20, 22, 15, 18, 25, 20, 12, 15, 18, 20, 22, 15, 18, 25, 20, 12, 22, 15, 18, 20, 25, 22, 15, 18]$$

**Step 5 — Engineering significance:**

In **environmental monitoring** and **climate engineering**, lag-1 and lag-2 shifted temperature series enable computation of the **autocorrelation function (ACF)**, which measures how today's temperature correlates with yesterday's and day-before-yesterday's readings. A high lag-1 autocorrelation indicates **thermal inertia** — useful in designing **HVAC systems** and **building insulation**. In **ARIMA modeling**, these lagged series serve as **regressors** for short-term weather forecasting. **[7 marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A quality control engineer records the number of defective products per shift over 15 consecutive shifts:

```
3, 5, 2, 7, 3, 5, 2, 4, 3, 6, 5, 2, 3, 4, 5
```

**(a)** Construct a frequency table and identify the **mode** and the **modal class** of the distribution. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Generate a **cumulative frequency polygon** (ogive) description and compute the **first-order difference** (equivalent to a lag-1 shift with subtraction) of the series. Interpret the result. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Count frequencies:**

Unique values: $\{2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$.

$$f(2)=3, \quad f(3)=4, \quad f(4)=2, \quad f(5)=3, \quad f(6)=1, \quad f(7)=1$$

**Verification:** $\sum f_i = 3+4+2+3+1+1 = 14$.

**Step 2 — Frequency table:**

| **Defects ($x_i$)** | **$f_i$** | **$r_i$** | **$F_i$** |
|---------------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| 2 | 3 | 0.214 | 3 |
| 3 | 4 | 0.286 | 7 |
| 4 | 2 | 0.143 | 9 |
| 5 | 3 | 0.214 | 12 |
| 6 | 1 | 0.071 | 13 |
| 7 | 1 | 0.071 | 14 |

**Step 3 — Identify the mode:**

The **mode** is the value with the **highest absolute frequency**. Here, $f(3) = 4$ is the maximum, so the mode is $\boxed{3 \text{ defects per shift}}$. The **modal class** is the category corresponding to this mode, i.e., the class "3 defects". **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Construct the cumulative frequency polygon (ogive) description:**

An **ogive** is a line plot of cumulative frequency $F_i$ versus the upper class boundary. Plotting points: $(2,3), (3,7), (4,9), (5,12), (6,13), (7,14)$. The resulting curve is **monotonically non-decreasing** and reaches a maximum of 14 at the final class. The ogive is used in **Six Sigma** and **SPC** (Statistical Process Control) to determine the **median** — the $x$-value where the ogive crosses $F = n/2 = 7$.

**Step 2 — Compute the first-order difference (lag-1 differencing):**

$$\Delta X[i] = X[i] - X[i-1] \quad \text{for } i = 2, 3, \ldots, 15$$

Computing each element:

$$\Delta X[2] = 5 - 3 = 2$$

$$\Delta X[3] = 2 - 5 = -3$$

$$\Delta X[4] = 7 - 2 = 5$$

$$\Delta X[5] = 3 - 7 = -4$$

$$\Delta X[6] = 5 - 3 = 2$$

$$\Delta X[7] = 2 - 5 = -3$$

$$\Delta X[8] = 4 - 2 = 2$$

$$\Delta X[9] = 3 - 4 = -1$$

$$\Delta X[10] = 6 - 3 = 3$$

$$\Delta X[11] = 5 - 6 = -1$$

$$\Delta X[12] = 2 - 5 = -3$$

$$\Delta X[13] = 3 - 2 = 1$$

$$\Delta X[14] = 4 - 3 = 1$$

$$\Delta X[15] = 5 - 4 = 1$$

**Step 3 — Present the differenced series:**

$$\Delta X = [\text{NA}, 2, -3, 5, -4, 2, -3, 2, -1, 3, -1, -3, 1, 1, 1]$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

First-order differencing **removes trend** and transforms the series into **incremental changes** between consecutive shifts. In quality control, $\Delta X[i] > 0$ indicates **deterioration** (more defects than the previous shift), while $\Delta X[i] < 0$ indicates **improvement**. The mean of $\Delta X$ is approximately **0.077**, very close to **0**, suggesting the process is in **statistical control** with no systematic drift. This differencing technique is the **first step in ARIMA (p, d, q) modeling** with $d=1$. **[7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When computing differenced or shifted series, students **commonly forget to place NA at position 1** for lag-1, or **fail to state the length** of the output vector. Both omissions cost **1 mark each**. Always explicitly state the output length (here, 15 elements with the first being NA) and verify the conservation property.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Absolute frequency** $f_i$ is a **raw integer count**; always verify $\sum f_i = n$.
- **Relative frequency** $r_i = f_i / n$ lies in $[0, 1]$ and $\sum r_i = 1$ (exactly, not approximately, before rounding).
- **Cumulative frequency** $F_i$ is **monotonically non-decreasing**; the last entry always equals $n$.
- **Percentage frequency** $p_i = r_i \times 100$; $\sum p_i = 100$ (before rounding).
- **Shift of order $k$:** $k > 0$ means **lag** (look into the past); $k < 0$ means **lead** (look into the future); $k = 0$ means **identity** (no change).
- The **first $k$ entries** of a lag-$k$ shifted series are always **NA**.
- The **last $k$ entries** of a lead-$k$ shifted series are always **NA**.
- **Differencing** ($\Delta X = X_t - X_{t-1}$) is a **combination of lag-1 shift and subtraction** — a cornerstone of ARIMA modeling.
- The **mode** is the value with the **highest absolute frequency**; for grouped data, the **modal class** is the class with the highest frequency density.
- **Conservation check:** Always verify $\sum f_i = n$ and $F_m = n$ as a mandatory final step.
- In KTU lab examinations, always pair theoretical calculations with the **corresponding R or Python code** for full marks.
- **Ogive** (cumulative frequency polygon) crosses $F = n/2$ at the **median** of the distribution.
- **Density** $d_i = r_i / w_i$ is used when class widths are **unequal** to ensure area-under-histogram equals 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization on Matplotlib packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization on Matplotlib packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Matplotlib** is a comprehensive, cross-platform, object-oriented plotting library for the Python programming language and its numerical mathematical extension **NumPy**. As per the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for *PCCBL508 – Computational Statistics Lab*, Matplotlib is the canonical 2D (and limited 3D) visualization toolkit used to transform raw statistical arrays into publication-quality static, animated, and interactive visualizations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Definition (PCCBL508):**
> "Matplotlib is a low-level graph plotting library in Python that serves as a visualization utility. It is built on the foundation of NumPy arrays and is designed to integrate seamlessly with the broader SciPy scientific computing stack."

The library is structured around three primary layers (or interfaces):

| Layer | Interface | Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Backend Layer** | `FigureCanvas`, `Renderer` | Talks to the hardware (PNG, PDF, SVG output) |
| **Artist Layer** | `Figure`, `Axes`, `Line2D` | The "middle-man" — full control over every pixel |
| **Scripting Layer** | `pyplot` | The quick, MATLAB-style state-based interface (most commonly used by beginners) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Matplotlib as a "Digital Canvas + Smart Painter".**
>
> Imagine you walk into a giant art studio. The **Figure** is your empty canvas mounted on the wall. The **Axes** are the rectangular drawing regions drawn on that canvas where your actual chart lives (with its own X-axis, Y-axis, gridlines, and labels). The **`pyplot` module** is the friendly studio assistant who hands you brushes, asks *"What color?"*, *"Line or dots?"*, and remembers your previous choices so you don't have to repeat yourself. You, the data scientist, just hand over the **NumPy arrays** (your raw statistical data), and the assistant draws the chart for you.

### The "Figure → Axes" Mental Model

Every Matplotlib plot obeys a strict hierarchy. Forgetting this hierarchy is the **#1 reason students get zero marks** for figure outputs in their lab records.

1. **Figure** (`fig`) — The outermost container. Think of it as a *full A4 page*.
2. **Axes** (`ax`) — The actual coordinate system where data is plotted. A single Figure can hold **multiple Axes** (e.g., a 2×2 grid of subplots).
3. **Axis** (note: singular, lowercase 'a') — The number-line objects (X-axis, Y-axis) belonging to an Axes.

## 1.3 Standard Imports (Mandatory Boilerplate)

Before any plotting, these two lines are mandatory in every experiment. KTU evaluators will deduct marks if they are missing.

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
```

> [!TIP]
> The alias `plt` is a globally accepted convention (PEP 8 compliant). Using `matplotlib.pyplot` directly in code is considered unprofessional and may be flagged during internal lab evaluations.

---

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Metrics Used in Matplotlib

Although Matplotlib is a software library, it has standardized units:

- **Default Figure Size:** $\mathbf{6.4 \times 4.8}$ **inches** at $\mathbf{100}$ **DPI** (resulting in $640 \times 480$ pixels by default).
- **Default Resolution:** $\mathbf{72}$ **DPI** (screen), can be increased to $\mathbf{300}$ **DPI** for print.
- **Color Models Supported:** Named colors (`'red'`), Hex codes (`'#FF5733'`), RGB tuples (`(0.1, 0.2, 0.5)`), and the **`tab:`** colormap palette (`'tab:blue'`).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Matplotlib Architecture: A Layered Approach

### 2.1.1 The `pyplot` State Machine

When you call `plt.plot(x, y)`, pyplot performs the following behind-the-scenes:

1. It checks whether a **current Figure** exists. If not, it creates a new one.
2. It checks whether a **current Axes** exists in that Figure. If not, it creates a new Axes filling the entire Figure.
3. It draws the `Line2D` artist onto that Axes.
4. It returns a list of `Line2D` objects for further customization.

### 2.1.2 The Object-Oriented Interface (OOI) — *Recommended for KTU Records*

The state machine becomes confusing in multi-plot scenarios. KTU examiners expect you to use the **OOI** for any non-trivial experiment. The pattern is:

```python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 5), dpi=100)
ax.plot(x, y, label='Data')
ax.set_xlabel('X Label')
ax.set_ylabel('Y Label')
ax.set_title('Title')
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always prefer `fig, ax = plt.subplots(...)` over `plt.plot(...)` in your lab record.** It is the industry-standard (used in the official Matplotlib documentation) and earns full marks in KTU evaluations.

## 2.2 The Five Core Plot Types (MUST KNOW)

| Plot Type | Function Call | Statistical Use-Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Line Plot** | `ax.plot(x, y)` | Time series, trend analysis |
| **Scatter Plot** | `ax.scatter(x, y)` | Correlation, clustering, bivariate distributions |
| **Bar Chart** | `ax.bar(categories, values)` | Categorical comparisons, frequency counts |
| **Histogram** | `ax.hist(data, bins=k)` | Univariate distribution, density estimation |
| **Pie Chart** | `ax.pie(values, labels=...)` | Proportional / percentage composition |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes every key Matplotlib formula/parameter that KTU expects you to know for the **ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** viva and written exam.

| Function / Method | Syntax | Formula / Mathematical Equivalent | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Figure Size** | `plt.subplots(figsize=(w, h))` | $A = w \times h$ | **inches** |
| **Pixel Resolution** | `plt.savefig('f.png', dpi=d)` | $P = d \times \text{size}_{\text{inch}}$ | **pixels** |
| **Bin Count (Sturges)** | `bins = int(np.ceil(np.log2(n))) + 1` | $k = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil + 1$ | unitless |
| **Bin Count (Freedman-Diaconis)** | `bins = (max - min) / (2 \cdot IQR / n^{1/3})` | $h = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{IQR}}{n^{1/3}}$ | unitless |
| **Linspace Generator** | `np.linspace(start, stop, n)` | $x_i = a + i \cdot \frac{b-a}{n-1}$ | same as data |
| **Arange Generator** | `np.arange(start, stop, step)` | $x_i = a + i \cdot \Delta$ | same as data |
| **Normal PDF (Reference Line)** | $\frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | $f(x \mid \mu, \sigma)$ | probability density |
| **Subplot Grid Indexing** | `ax = fig.add_subplot(rows, cols, idx)` | $\text{idx} = r \cdot \text{cols} + c + 1$ | unitless |
| **Logarithmic Scale** | `ax.set_yscale('log')` | $\log_{10}(y)$ | log units |
| **Gridline Spacing** | `ax.set_xticks(np.arange(min, max, step))` | $x_k = a + k \cdot \Delta$ | data units |
| **Bar Width (default)** | `ax.bar(...)` | $w_{\text{default}} = 0.8$ | data units |
| **Pie Sum Constraint** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ | Must equal $100\%$ for autopct | percent |

## 2.4 Why Matplotlib? — Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Visualization Use |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Signal Processing** | Plotting ECG, EEG, or vibration signals over time |
| **Structural Engineering** | Stress-strain curves ($\sigma$-$\varepsilon$) |
| **Machine Learning** | Loss vs. epoch curves, confusion matrix heatmaps |
| **Statistics (this course)** | Histograms, box plots, Q-Q plots, regression lines |
| **Control Systems** | Step response, Bode plots (with `bode` from `control` lib) |

## 2.5 The Three Styling Pillars

Every plot you create in your lab record must address all three:

1. **Labels**: `ax.set_xlabel()`, `ax.set_ylabel()`, `ax.set_title()`
2. **Legend**: `ax.legend(loc='best')` — `loc` can be `'upper right'`, `'lower left'`, `'center'`, etc.
3. **Gridlines** (optional but recommended): `ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)`

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Plotting a Sine Wave with Full Customization

This is a **classic KTU 2024 Scheme lab question** — *"Plot the sine function for $x \in [0, 2\pi]$ with appropriate labels, title, and grid."* We derive the complete solution:

### Step 1 — Import Libraries
We need `numpy` for mathematical functions and array generation, and `matplotlib.pyplot` for the plotting interface.

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
```

### Step 2 — Generate the Independent Variable
We use `np.linspace` to create $n = 100$ evenly spaced points between $0$ and $2\pi$. The mathematical formula being applied is:

$$x_i = a + i \cdot \frac{b - a}{n - 1}, \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 99$$

Substituting $a = 0$, $b = 2\pi$, $n = 100$:

$$x_0 = 0 + 0 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{99} = 0$$
$$x_{99} = 0 + 99 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{99} = 2\pi$$

```python
x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)
```

### Step 3 — Compute the Dependent Variable
Apply the sine function element-wise. The result is a NumPy array of $100$ values in the range $[-1, 1]$.

$$y_i = \sin(x_i) \quad \forall i \in [0, 99]$$

```python
y = np.sin(x)
```

### Step 4 — Create the Figure and Axes (OOI Pattern)
We set the figure size to $\mathbf{8 \times 4}$ **inches** and resolution to $\mathbf{100}$ **DPI**.

```python
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4), dpi=100)
```

### Step 5 — Plot the Data with Customization
Here we apply the **three styling pillars**: line color (`color='navy'`), line style (`linestyle='-'`), line width (`linewidth=2`), and a data marker (`marker='o'`).

```python
ax.plot(x, y, color='navy', linestyle='-', linewidth=2, marker='o',
        markersize=4, label='sin(x)', alpha=0.8)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Marker codes quick reference**: `'o'` = circle, `'s'` = square, `'^'` = triangle up, `'*'` = star, `'D'` = diamond, `'x'` = cross.

### Step 6 — Add Labels, Title, and Legend
Mandatory for KTU lab records.

```python
ax.set_xlabel('Angle (radians)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Amplitude', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Plot of sin(x) from 0 to 2π', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=11)
```

### Step 7 — Add Gridlines
Dashed lines with reduced opacity for a clean, publication-quality look.

```python
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)
```

### Step 8 — Set X-axis Ticks to $\pi$ Multiples
We manually set the ticks at $0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2, 2\pi$ for readability.

```python
ax.set_xticks([0, np.pi/2, np.pi, 3*np.pi/2, 2*np.pi])
ax.set_xticklabels(['0', 'π/2', 'π', '3π/2', '2π'])
```

### Step 9 — Display or Save the Figure
The `plt.show()` command renders the plot in a window. To save it for the lab record:

```python
plt.savefig('sine_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
```

The `bbox_inches='tight'` argument removes excess whitespace — **KTU evaluators love this detail**.

### Complete Program (Copy-Paste Ready)

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Step 2: Generate independent variable
x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 100)

# Step 3: Compute dependent variable
y = np.sin(x)

# Step 4: Create Figure and Axes
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4), dpi=100)

# Step 5: Plot the data
ax.plot(x, y, color='navy', linestyle='-', linewidth=2, marker='o',
        markersize=4, label='sin(x)', alpha=0.8)

# Step 6: Labels, title, legend
ax.set_xlabel('Angle (radians)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Amplitude', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Plot of sin(x) from 0 to 2π', fontsize=14, fontweight='bold')
ax.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=11)

# Step 7: Gridlines
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)

# Step 8: Custom X-ticks
ax.set_xticks([0, np.pi/2, np.pi, 3*np.pi/2, 2*np.pi])
ax.set_xticklabels(['0', 'π/2', 'π', '3π/2', '2π'])

# Step 9: Save and show
plt.savefig('sine_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()
```

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Plot Type

The following decision matrix helps you select the correct plot type based on the **data type** and **analytical goal** — a frequent KTU viva question.

| Data Type | Variables | Goal | Recommended Plot | Matplotlib Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Continuous | 1 (univariate) | Show distribution | Histogram | `ax.hist(data, bins=k)` |
| Continuous | 2 (bivariate) | Show relationship | Scatter plot | `ax.scatter(x, y)` |
| Continuous | 1 vs Time | Show trend | Line plot | `ax.plot(t, y)` |
| Categorical | Categorical + Value | Compare groups | Bar chart | `ax.bar(labels, values)` |
| Categorical | Parts of whole | Show composition | Pie chart | `ax.pie(values, labels=...)` |
| Continuous | 1 + 1 | Show correlation | Scatter + Regression line | `ax.scatter` + `ax.plot` |
| Continuous | 1 (univariate) | Show spread/outliers | Box plot | `ax.boxplot(data)` |
| Continuous | Multiple series | Compare distributions | Box plot (multi) | `ax.boxplot([d1, d2, d3])` |

---

## 3.3 Subplots: Deriving the Index Formula

When creating a grid of subplots via `plt.subplots(nrows, ncols)`, the returned `Axes` is a 2D NumPy array. To access the subplot at row $r$ and column $c$ (0-indexed):

$$\text{axes}_{rc} = \text{axes}[r, \, c]$$

For a $2 \times 3$ grid (6 subplots total), the index mapping is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Position }(1,1) &\rightarrow \text{axes}[0][0] \\
\text{Position }(1,2) &\rightarrow \text{axes}[0][1] \\
\text{Position }(2,3) &\rightarrow \text{axes}[1][2]
\end{aligned}$$

**Example — Plotting sin, cos, and tan together:**

```python
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 4))

x = np.linspace(-2 * np.pi, 2 * np.pi, 200)

axes[0].plot(x, np.sin(x), color='blue')
axes[0].set_title('sin(x)')

axes[1].plot(x, np.cos(x), color='red')
axes[1].set_title('cos(x)')

axes[2].plot(x, np.tan(x), color='green')
axes[2].set_ylim(-5, 5)   # Important: tan(x) is unbounded!
axes[2].set_title('tan(x)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> `plt.tight_layout()` is **mandatory** for multi-subplot figures. It automatically adjusts subplot padding so labels don't overlap. Forgetting this loses $\mathbf{2}$ **marks** in KTU evaluations.

---

## 3.4 Histogram Derivation: Optimal Bin Count

The most theoretically rich KTU question is *"Choose the optimal number of bins for a histogram."* Matplotlib's default is `'auto'`, but KTU expects you to know two classical formulas:

### Sturges' Rule (for $n \le 200$)

$$k = \lceil \log_2(n) \rceil + 1$$

For $n = 100$ data points:

$$k = \lceil \log_2(100) \rceil + 1 = \lceil 6.64 \rceil + 1 = 7 + 1 = 8 \text{ bins}$$

### Freedman-Diaconis Rule (for $n > 200$, robust to outliers)

$$h = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{IQR}(x)}{n^{1/3}}$$

where $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ (the interquartile range).

For $n = 1000$ points with $\text{IQR} = 4.5$:

$$h = 2 \cdot \frac{4.5}{1000^{1/3}} = 2 \cdot \frac{4.5}{10} = 0.9$$

Then the number of bins is:

$$k = \left\lceil \frac{\max(x) - \min(x)}{h} \right\rceil$$

---

## 3.5 Scatter Plot with Color Mapping — Advanced Pattern

A common advanced KTU question is to plot a scatter plot with a third dimension encoded as color:

```python
np.random.seed(42)
x = np.random.randn(200)
y = np.random.randn(200)
colors = np.random.rand(200)        # Third dimension
sizes = 100 * np.random.rand(200)   # Fourth dimension

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 6))
scatter = ax.scatter(x, y, c=colors, s=sizes, cmap='viridis', alpha=0.7)
plt.colorbar(scatter, ax=ax, label='Color Value')
ax.set_title('Scatter Plot with Color and Size Encoding')
ax.set_xlabel('X')
ax.set_ylabel('Y')
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.4)
plt.show()
```

The colormap `'viridis'` is the **default and recommended** for KTU submissions as it is colorblind-friendly.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define Matplotlib. List any four types of plots that can be generated using `matplotlib.pyplot`.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Matplotlib is a comprehensive Python library used for creating static, animated, and interactive 2D visualizations. It is built on top of NumPy arrays and is part of the SciPy ecosystem. **(1 mark)**

The four types of plots are: **(2 marks — ½ mark each)**
1. **Line plot** — `plt.plot(x, y)` for time series / trend
2. **Scatter plot** — `plt.scatter(x, y)` for correlation
3. **Histogram** — `plt.hist(data, bins=k)` for distribution
4. **Bar chart** — `plt.bar(categories, values)` for categorical comparison

*(Acceptable alternatives: Pie chart, Box plot, Stem plot, Fill_between plot)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between `plt.plot()` and `plt.scatter()` in Matplotlib. When would you prefer one over the other?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `plt.plot()` | `plt.scatter()` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Default Use** | Connecting data points with lines | Showing individual data points |
| **Performance** | Faster for large datasets ($n > 10^4$) | Slower; one artist per point |
| **Point Customization** | Same style for all points | Per-point color, size, and shape |
| **Statistical Use** | Time series, function plots | Correlation, clustering, regression |

**When to prefer:**
- Use `plt.plot()` for **continuous functions** like $y = \sin(x)$ or time-series data. **(1 mark)**
- Use `plt.scatter()` when individual points have **distinct attributes** (different colors/sizes representing a third/fourth variable). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram of the Matplotlib architecture, explain the **Figure–Axes–Axis** hierarchy. Why is the object-oriented interface preferred over the `pyplot` state machine for complex plots? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Python program to:
1. Generate $500$ samples from a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$.
2. Plot the histogram with $30$ bins.
3. Overlay the theoretical Probability Density Function (PDF) curve on the histogram.
4. Add appropriate labels, title, legend, and gridlines. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Matplotlib Architecture (7 marks breakdown):**

The Matplotlib library is organized into **three layers** that separate concerns between the user, the internal data model, and the hardware output device.

1. **Backend Layer** (1 mark) — Composed of `FigureCanvas` (the drawing surface), `Renderer` (the object that knows how to draw on the canvas), and `Event` (handles user input like mouse clicks). This layer is the only one that "talks" to the operating system to produce PNG, PDF, or on-screen output.

2. **Artist Layer** (2 marks) — The middle layer containing all the drawable objects:
   - **Figure**: The top-level container (the entire image/window).
   - **Axes**: A rectangular region where data is plotted. A Figure can contain *multiple* Axes.
   - **Axis objects**: The number-line objects ($x$-axis, $y$-axis) that handle ticks, tick labels, and limits.
   - **Line2D, Text, Patch, Polygon**: Concrete drawable objects.

3. **Scripting Layer** (2 marks) — The topmost layer is `matplotlib.pyplot`, which provides a state-based, MATLAB-like interface. It automatically manages the "current Figure" and "current Axes" behind the scenes.

**Why OOI is preferred** (2 marks):
- **Explicit references**: With `fig, ax = plt.subplots()`, you always know exactly which Axes you are modifying — critical when a figure has multiple subplots.
- **Function reusability**: You can write a function `plot_data(ax, x, y)` that works on *any* Axes, promoting modular code.
- **No state leakage**: The `pyplot` state machine can cause subtle bugs when you mix `plt.title()` calls in a multi-plot figure.

---

#### Solution to Q.A(b):

**Complete Python Program (7 marks):**

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# (1) Generate 500 samples from N(0, 1)
np.random.seed(0)                         # For reproducibility  (1 mark)
samples = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=500)

# (2) Plot histogram with 30 bins
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5), dpi=100)
ax.hist(samples, bins=30, density=True, color='skyblue',
        edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7, label='Sample Histogram')  # (2 marks)

# (3) Overlay the theoretical PDF
x_theory = np.linspace(-4, 4, 500)
pdf = (1 / np.sqrt(2 * np.pi)) * np.exp(-0.5 * x_theory**2)   # (2 marks)
ax.plot(x_theory, pdf, color='red', linewidth=2, label='Theoretical PDF')

# (4) Labels, title, legend, grid
ax.set_xlabel('Value', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Probability Density', fontsize=12)
ax.set_title('Histogram of N(0,1) with Theoretical PDF Overlay', fontsize=13)
ax.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=11)
ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()                                                          # (2 marks)
```

**Mathematical Note (for full marks):**
The theoretical PDF of $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is:

$$f(x \mid \mu, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

For $\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{x^2}{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to set `density=True` in `ax.hist()`. Without it, the histogram shows raw *counts* and the PDF curve (which integrates to 1) will be on a completely different Y-scale, producing a **wrong-looking plot** and resulting in **loss of 2 marks**.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the different ways to create subplots in Matplotlib. Compare `plt.subplot()` and `plt.subplots()` with suitable code snippets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to create a $2 \times 2$ grid of subplots showing:
1. A line plot of $\sin(x)$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi]$.
2. A scatter plot of $100$ random points.
3. A bar chart of 5 categories.
4. A pie chart of 4 segments.

Use the object-oriented interface. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**Two methods of creating subplots:**

**Method 1: `plt.subplot(nrows, ncols, index)`** (3 marks)
This is a state-machine call that switches the "current Axes" to the specified subplot. The `index` is a **1-based** integer that scans the grid left-to-right, top-to-bottom.

```python
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))

plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)   # First subplot (left)
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [1, 4, 9])
plt.title('Subplot 1')

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)   # Second subplot (right)
plt.plot([1, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3])
plt.title('Subplot 2')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Method 2: `plt.subplots(nrows, ncols)` — Recommended** (3 marks)
This is a *single* function call that returns a Figure and a NumPy array of Axes. It is preferred because:
- The Axes array can be iterated over for uniform styling.
- It does not rely on hidden "current Axes" state.
- It is far more readable in lab records.

```python
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
axes[0].plot([1, 2, 3], [1, 4, 9])
axes[0].set_title('Subplot 1')
axes[1].plot([1, 2, 3], [1, 2, 3])
axes[1].set_title('Subplot 2')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Feature | `plt.subplot()` | `plt.subplots()` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Return | None (state-based) | `(fig, axes)` tuple |
| Indexing | 1-based integer | 0-based `[r, c]` |
| Loop-friendly | No | Yes (iterate over `axes.flat`) |
| OOI | No | Yes |

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

**Complete $2 \times 2$ Subplot Grid Program (7 marks):**

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)

# Create 2x2 grid: shared (5, 5) inches
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(8, 6), dpi=100)   # (1 mark)

# --- Subplot 1 (top-left): sin(x) line plot ---
x = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 200)
axes[0, 0].plot(x, np.sin(x), color='blue', linewidth=2)
axes[0, 0].set_title('sin(x)')
axes[0, 0].set_xlabel('x')
axes[0, 0].set_ylabel('sin(x)')                          # (1.5 marks)

# --- Subplot 2 (top-right): scatter plot ---
x_rand = np.random.rand(100)
y_rand = np.random.rand(100)
axes[0, 1].scatter(x_rand, y_rand, c='green', alpha=0.6, edgecolor='black')
axes[0, 1].set_title('Random Scatter')
axes[0, 1].set_xlabel('X')
axes[0, 1].set_ylabel('Y')                               # (1.5 marks)

# --- Subplot 3 (bottom-left): bar chart ---
categories = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']
values = [23, 45, 56, 78, 33]
axes[1, 0].bar(categories, values, color='orange', edgecolor='black')
axes[1, 0].set_title('Bar Chart')
axes[1, 0].set_xlabel('Category')
axes[1, 0].set_ylabel('Value')                           # (1.5 marks)

# --- Subplot 4 (bottom-right): pie chart ---
labels = ['Apple', 'Banana', 'Cherry', 'Date']
sizes = [30, 25, 25, 20]
axes[1, 1].pie(sizes, labels=labels, autopct='%1.1f%%',
               startangle=90, colors=['red', 'yellow', 'pink', 'brown'])
axes[1, 1].set_title('Pie Chart')                        # (1.5 marks)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse the indexing. For a $2 \times 2$ grid, the indices are `axes[0,0]`, `axes[0,1]`, `axes[1,0]`, `axes[1,1]`. Writing `axes[1]` or `axes[2]` is a **common error** that causes `IndexError` and costs **3 marks**.
>
> **Pitfall #2:** Forgetting `autopct='%1.1f%%'` in `pie()` results in a pie chart without percentage labels — losing **1 mark** for "incomplete output".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick-Reference for KTU 2024 ESE & Lab Viva:**

- ✅ **Always start** every program with `import numpy as np` and `import matplotlib.pyplot as plt`.
- ✅ **Prefer the OOI pattern**: `fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(w,h), dpi=d)` over `plt.plot()`.
- ✅ **Remember the hierarchy**: `Figure` (canvas) → `Axes` (plot region) → `Axis` (number lines).
- ✅ **For subplots**, use `plt.subplots(nrows, ncols)` and access via `axes[r, c]` (0-indexed).
- ✅ **For histograms** showing a density, **always** pass `density=True`.
- ✅ **Save with `plt.savefig('f.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')`** for high-quality outputs.
- ✅ **Use `plt.tight_layout()`** for multi-subplot figures to avoid overlapping labels.
- ✅ **Add `ax.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.6)`** for a professional, publication-style look.
- ✅ **Default figure size** is $\mathbf{6.4 \times 4.8}$ **inches**; always specify `figsize` explicitly.
- ✅ **`np.linspace(start, stop, n)`** generates $n$ evenly spaced points (inclusive of both endpoints).
- ✅ **`np.arange(start, stop, step)`** generates points with a fixed step (exclusive of `stop`).
- ✅ **Line styles**: `'-'` (solid), `'--'` (dashed), `'-.''` (dash-dot), `':'` (dotted).
- ✅ **Markers**: `'o'`, `'s'`, `'^'`, `'*'`, `'D'`, `'x'`, `'+'`, `'.'`.
- ✅ **Common colormaps**: `'viridis'` (default), `'plasma'`, `'jet'`, `'coolwarm'`, `'gray'`.
- ✅ **`plt.show()`** must be the **last** plotting command; `plt.savefig()` must be called **before** `plt.show()`.
- ✅ **For reproducibility**, always use `np.random.seed(some_integer)` at the start of any random-data experiment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic programming experiments on Plotting Graphs, Controlling Graph, Adding Text
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Basic programming experiments on Plotting Graphs, Controlling Graph, Adding Text # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Graphical Visualization in Computational Statistics** refers to the systematic creation, manipulation, and annotation of 2D/3D visual representations of statistical data using programming constructs. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCBL508), this encompasses three primary sub-operations: > 1. **Plotting Graphs** — the procedural rendering of data points onto a Cartesian coordinate system using functions like `plot()`, `scatter()`, `bar()`, and `hist()`. > 2. **Controlling Graph** — the parameterized regulation of visual attributes such as figure size, axes limits, ticks, line styles, markers, colors, grid lines, and subplot layout. > 3. **Adding Text** — the dynamic insertion of titles, axis labels, legends, annotations, and mathematical expressions using TeX/LaTeX-rendering functions. The standard library of choice in KTU-endorsed labs is **Python's `matplotlib.pyplot`** module (with NumPy for numerical data) and **R's base graphics/ggplot2**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Master Painter" Analogy:** > Imagine you have hired a master painter (your plotting function) to create a portrait of your data. > - **Plotting Graphs** = Asking the painter to *draw the outline* (the curves, points, and bars that represent the raw data). > - **Controlling Graph** = Giving the painter specific instructions: *"Use a 10×6 inch canvas, draw the x-axis from 0 to 10, use a red dashed line, place blue circular markers."* You are **dictating the look**. > - **Adding Text** = Asking the painter to *sign the painting* with a title, label the x-axis as "Time (seconds)", label the y-axis as "Velocity (m/s)", and add a small note pointing at the peak: *"Maximum value reached here"*. Without these three steps, your statistical output is just numbers in a file — invisible to decision-makers. ## 1.3 Why This Matters in Statistics Statistical inference (mean, variance, regression) gives numbers, but **visualization turns numbers into insight**. Tukey's Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) philosophy states: *"The greatest value of a picture is when it forces us to notice what we never expected to see."* Plotting, controlling, and annotating are the three pillars of this EDA workflow. ## 1.4 Standard Library Stack | Library / Tool | Role in KTU Lab | |---|---| | **NumPy** | Numerical array generation (`np.linspace`, `np.arange`) | | **Matplotlib (pyplot)** | Core 2D plotting engine | | **Seaborn** | High-level statistical aesthetics (built on Matplotlib) | | **Pandas `.plot()`** | Convenience plotting from DataFrames | | **R `plot()`, `ggplot2`** | Alternative language for KTU cross-platform evaluations | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three-Phase Plotting Pipeline Every statistical graph in Python (and conceptually in R) follows a strict **three-phase pipeline**: ### Phase 1: Data Preparation - Generate or import data using NumPy arrays, lists, or Pandas DataFrames. - For continuous functions: use `np.linspace(start, stop, num)` to create evenly spaced points. - For discrete data: use Python lists or `np.array([...])`. ### Phase 2: Plotting (Drawing the Figure) - Call the appropriate `pyplot` function on a **Figure** (the canvas) and **Axes** (the actual coordinate system). - Default line plot: `plt.plot(x, y)` connects (x, y) pairs with a continuous line. ### Phase 3: Controlling & Annotation - Apply stylistic overrides *before* displaying the graph. - The `plt.show()` command is the final "release" — nothing renders until this is called. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Rule:** The order of `plt.show()` matters. All control and text commands must appear **before** `plt.show()`, otherwise the figure window closes before modifications are applied. In Jupyter notebooks, `%matplotlib inline` is used to embed plots. ## 2.2 Key Controlling Parameters (Formula Sheet) The following table consolidates the **canonical controlling arguments** in `plt.plot()` and supporting functions. These are the parameters most frequently asked in KTU viva and written exams. | Parameter | Allowed Values | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | `color` / `c` | `'red'`, `'#FF0000'`, `RGB tuple` | Blue cycle | Line/marker color | | `linestyle` / `ls` | `'-'`, `'--'`, `':'`, `'-.'` | `'-'` (solid) | Line dash pattern | | `linewidth` / `lw` | Float (e.g., 2.5) | `1.5` | Line thickness in points | | `marker` | `'o'`, `'s'`, `'^'`, `'D'`, `'*'`, `'+'` | None | Point shape | | `markersize` / `ms` | Float | `6` | Marker diameter | | `markerfacecolor` / `mfc` | Color spec | Auto | Inner marker color | | `markeredgecolor` / `mec` | Color spec | Auto | Marker border color | | `alpha` | Float in `[0, 1]` | `1.0` | Transparency | | `label` | String | None | Legend entry text | | `xlim`, `ylim` | Tuple `(min, max)` | Auto | Axis range limit | | `xticks`, `yticks` | List of values | Auto | Tick positions | | `grid` | `True` / `False` | `False` | Show gridlines | | `figsize` | Tuple `(width, height)` in inches | `(6.4, 4.8)` | Canvas size | | `dpi` | Integer | `100` | Resolution | | `subplot` | `(rows, cols, index)` | `(1, 1, 1)` | Multi-panel layout | ## 2.3 Text-Adding Functions (Formula Sheet) | Function | Purpose | Example | |---|---|---| | `plt.title()` | Figure / Axes title | `plt.title("Sales 2024")` | | `plt.xlabel()` | X-axis label | `plt.xlabel("Quarter")` | | `plt.ylabel()` | Y-axis label | `plt.ylabel("Revenue (₹ Lakhs)")` | | `plt.suptitle()` | Overall figure title (for subplots) | `plt.suptitle("Annual Report")` | | `plt.legend()` | Display legend box | `plt.legend(loc='best')` | | `plt.text()` | Free-floating text at data coordinates | `plt.text(2, 5, "Peak")` | | `plt.annotate()` | Text with arrow pointing to a location | `plt.annotate("Max", xy=(2, 5), xytext=(3, 6), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->"))` | | LaTeX math | Wrap in `$...$` | `plt.title(r"$\sigma^2 = \mathrm{Var}(X)$")` | | `fontsize` | Argument to text functions | `fontsize=14` | | `fontweight` | `'bold'`, `'normal'`, `'heavy'` | `fontweight='bold'` | | `color` (text) | Any color spec | `color='darkred'` | ## 2.4 The Subplot Grid Formula For multi-panel scientific figures, KTU students must master the **`plt.subplot(rows, cols, index)`** function. The relationship between the index and the position is: $$ \text{index} = (r-1) \times \text{cols} + c $$ where $r$ is the row number (1 to `rows`) and $c$ is the column number (1 to `cols`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In signal processing, biomedical ECG analysis, and quality-control dashboards, the `subplot` grid is used to simultaneously display *time-domain*, *frequency-domain*, and *statistical-distribution* views of the same dataset — a standard requirement in KTU viva practicals. ## 2.5 The Legend Location Code The `loc` parameter in `plt.legend()` accepts these standard codes (commonly tested): | Code | Position | |---|---| | `'best'` | Automatic (default) | | `'upper right'` | Top-right corner | | `'upper left'` | Top-left corner | | `'lower left'` | Bottom-left corner | | `'lower right'` | Bottom-right corner | | `'center'` | Geometric center | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line walkthroughs** of canonical KTU lab programs. No step is skipped. ## 3.1 Experiment 2A: Basic Line Plot of a Statistical Function **Problem:** Plot $y = \sin(x)$ and $y = \cos(x)$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi]$ on the same figure, with proper labels, title, legend, and grid. ### Exhaustive Code Walkthrough ```python # Step 1: Import libraries import numpy as np # NumPy for numerical arrays import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Matplotlib for plotting # Step 2: Generate evenly spaced x-values x = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 100) # 100 points from 0 to 2π (inclusive) # - np.pi is the constant π ≈ 3.14159 # - 2*np.pi gives the full period of sine and cosine # - 100 points ensure a smooth curve # Step 3: Compute corresponding y-values y1 = np.sin(x) # y1 = sin(x), element-wise y2 = np.cos(x) # y2 = cos(x), element-wise # Step 4: Create the figure (controlling size and resolution) plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5), dpi=100) # - figsize=(8,5) means 8 inches wide × 5 inches tall # - dpi=100 ensures sharp rendering # Step 5: Plot the two curves with controlling parameters plt.plot(x, y1, color='blue', linestyle='-', linewidth=2.0, marker='o', markersize=4, label='sin(x)') # - color='blue' → blue solid line # - linestyle='-' → continuous line (default) # - linewidth=2.0 → thicker than default for visibility # - marker='o' → circular markers at each data point # - markersize=4 → small circles (not overpowering) # - label='sin(x)' → text used later by plt.legend() plt.plot(x, y2, color='red', linestyle='--', linewidth=2.0, marker='s', markersize=4, label='cos(x)') # - linestyle='--' → dashed line, distinguishes from sin(x) # - marker='s' → square markers # Step 6: Add the title plt.title("Trigonometric Functions: sin(x) and cos(x)", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold', color='darkblue') # - fontsize=14 → larger than default (12) # - fontweight='bold' → emphasizes the title # - color='darkblue' → aesthetic accent # Step 7: Add axis labels plt.xlabel("x (radians)", fontsize=12) plt.ylabel("Function value", fontsize=12) # Step 8: Add grid lines for readability plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.6) # - linestyle=':' → dotted grid (subtle) # - alpha=0.6 → 60% opaque (faint) # Step 9: Set custom tick marks at multiples of π/2 plt.xticks([0, np.pi/2, np.pi, 3*np.pi/2, 2*np.pi], ['0', 'π/2', 'π', '3π/2', '2π']) # Step 10: Add the legend plt.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=11, frameon=True, shadow=True) # - frameon=True → legend has a border box # - shadow=True → adds a 3D drop shadow # Step 11: Render the figure plt.tight_layout() # automatically adjusts padding to prevent label cutoff plt.show() # displays the interactive window ``` ### Output Description A 8×5 inch figure appears with a blue solid curve ($\sin x$) and a red dashed curve ($\cos x$), correctly intersecting at $\pi/4, 3\pi/4, 5\pi/4, 7\pi/4$. Title, axis labels, π-tick marks, dotted grid, and a shadowed legend box are all visible. --- ## 3.2 Experiment 2B: Controlling the Graph — Histogram with Overlaid PDF **Problem:** Generate $n = 1000$ samples from a Normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu=0, \sigma=1)$, draw a histogram with controlled bin width, and overlay the theoretical Probability Density Function (PDF). ### Theoretical PDF Formula The PDF of the Normal distribution is: $$ f(x \mid \mu, \sigma) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right) $$ For the standard normal ($\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$), this simplifies to: $$ \varphi(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-x^2/2} $$ ### Exhaustive Code Walkthrough ```python # Step 1: Imports import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from scipy.stats import norm # SciPy gives us the PDF directly # Step 2: Set seed for reproducibility np.random.seed(42) # Ensures same random numbers every run # Step 3: Generate 1000 normal samples samples = np.random.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=1000) # - loc=0 → mean μ = 0 # - scale=1 → standard deviation σ = 1 # - size=1000 → number of random draws # Step 4: Create figure plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5)) # Step 5: Draw the histogram (controlling density normalization) count, bins, ignored = plt.hist(samples, bins=30, density=True, color='skyblue', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7, label='Empirical (n=1000)') # - bins=30 → 30 equal-width intervals # - density=True → y-axis is probability density, not raw count # - edgecolor='black'→ bin edges outlined for clarity # - alpha=0.7 → semi-transparent so grid is visible # Step 6: Generate the theoretical PDF curve x_theory = np.linspace(-4, 4, 200) # x from -4σ to +4σ y_theory = norm.pdf(x_theory, loc=0, scale=1) # - norm.pdf(x, loc, scale) → evaluates φ(x) at each x # Step 7: Overlay the PDF plt.plot(x_theory, y_theory, color='red', linewidth=2.5, label='Theoretical PDF') # Step 8: Add text annotation for the mean plt.text(-3.5, 0.35, r'$\mu = 0,\ \sigma = 1$', fontsize=13, color='darkred', fontweight='bold') # - r'...' is a raw string; $...$ triggers LaTeX math mode in matplotlib # Step 9: Title, labels, legend, grid plt.title("Standard Normal Distribution: Empirical vs Theoretical", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold') plt.xlabel("x", fontsize=12) plt.ylabel("Probability Density", fontsize=12) plt.legend(loc='upper right', fontsize=11) plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.5) # Step 10: Set x-axis limits plt.xlim(-4, 4) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ### Analytical Insight As $n \to \infty$, the histogram bars will **converge to the red theoretical curve** (Glivenko–Cantelli theorem). This is a standard empirical-verification plot for KTU's Module 2 (Distributions & Density Estimation). --- ## 3.3 Experiment 2C: Subplots and Annotated Text **Problem:** Create a 2×2 grid showing four statistical plots of a single dataset: scatter, line, bar, and histogram. Add an arrow annotation pointing to the maximum value. ### Exhaustive Code Walkthrough ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Step 1: Synthetic dataset np.random.seed(7) months = np.arange(1, 13) # 1 to 12 sales = np.array([150, 170, 165, 190, 210, 250, 230, 245, 260, 280, 300, 320]) # 12 monthly values errors = np.random.randint(5, 20, size=12) # measurement errors # Step 2: Create the 2x2 grid fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(11, 8)) # - fig → the entire figure object # - axes → 2D array of Axes objects: axes[0,0], axes[0,1], axes[1,0], axes[1,1] # Step 3: Subplot (1,1) — Scatter plot with error bars axes[0, 0].scatter(months, sales, color='purple', s=60, alpha=0.8) axes[0, 0].errorbar(months, sales, yerr=errors, fmt='none', ecolor='gray', capsize=3) axes[0, 0].set_title("Scatter + Error Bars") axes[0, 0].set_xlabel("Month") axes[0, 0].set_ylabel("Sales (₹ '000s)") axes[0, 0].grid(True, linestyle=':') # Step 4: Subplot (1,2) — Line plot axes[0, 1].plot(months, sales, color='green', marker='o', linewidth=2, markersize=8) axes[0, 1].set_title("Trend Line") axes[0, 1].set_xlabel("Month") axes[0, 1].set_ylabel("Sales (₹ '000s)") axes[0, 1].grid(True) # Step 5: Subplot (2,1) — Bar plot axes[1, 0].bar(months, sales, color='orange', edgecolor='black') axes[1, 0].set_title("Bar Chart") axes[1, 0].set_xlabel("Month") axes[1, 0].set_ylabel("Sales (₹ '000s)") # Step 6: Subplot (2,2) — Histogram axes[1, 1].hist(sales, bins=6, color='salmon', edgecolor='black') axes[1, 1].set_title("Distribution of Sales") axes[1, 1].set_xlabel("Sales (₹ '000s)") axes[1, 1].set_ylabel("Frequency") # Step 7: ANNOTATION with arrow on the maximum point (December) max_idx = np.argmax(sales) # index of the maximum value axes[0, 1].annotate("Peak: Dec = 320", xy=(12, 320), # arrow head location xytext=(8, 280), # text location fontsize=11, color='darkred', fontweight='bold', arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred', lw=1.5)) # - xy → tip of the arrow # - xytext → where the text is placed # - arrowprops → dictionary controlling the arrow style # Step 8: Overall figure title fig.suptitle("Annual Sales 2024 — Multi-View Analysis", fontsize=15, fontweight='bold', color='navy') # Step 9: Auto-adjust layout plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96]) # leaves space for suptitle plt.show() ``` ### Numerical Trace | Month | Sales (₹ '000s) | Error | |---|---|---| | 1 | 150 | random 5–19 | | 2 | 170 | ... | | ... | ... | ... | | 12 | 320 (max) | ... | The `argmax` function returns index `11` (Python 0-indexed), corresponding to December. The annotation arrow lands at coordinates `(12, 320)`. --- ## 3.4 Experiment 2D: Saving the Figure to Disk (KTU Mandatory Skill) ```python # After plt.show() equivalent operations: plt.savefig("output_graph.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight') # - dpi=300 → print-quality resolution # - bbox_inches='tight' → trims excess whitespace # - supported formats: .png, .jpg, .pdf, .svg ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** `plt.savefig()` MUST be called **before** `plt.show()` if you want the saved file to include all custom modifications. The reverse order produces an empty file in some backends. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Plotting Pipeline Flowchart The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete workflow** from raw data to a publication-ready annotated statistical graph. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Data: NumPy array, list, or DataFrame] --> B[Data Preparation: np.linspace, np.arange, sampling] B --> C[Figure Creation: plt.figure or plt.subplots] C --> D[Plotting Call: plt.plot, scatter, bar, hist] D --> E[Controlling Phase: color, linestyle, marker, linewidth, figsize, dpi, xlim, ylim] E --> F[Text Addition: title, xlabel, ylabel, legend, text, annotate, LaTeX math] F --> G{Quality Check} G -->|Tweaks needed| E G -->|Satisfied| H[Output: plt.show and plt.savefig] H --> I[Final Publication-Ready Graph] ``` ## 4.2 Anatomy of a Matplotlib Figure (Block Architecture) This block diagram shows the **hierarchical object structure** of a Matplotlib figure — a favorite KTU viva question. ```mermaid flowchart TD Fig[Figure: Top-level canvas
figsize, dpi, facecolor] --> Axes1[Axes 1: Cartesian coordinate system] Fig --> Axes2[Axes 2: Subplot region] Axes1 --> Title[Title: plt.title] Axes1 --> XLabel[X-axis label: plt.xlabel] Axes1 --> YLabel[Y-axis label: plt.ylabel] Axes1 --> XTicks[X-axis ticks: plt.xticks] Axes1 --> YTicks[Y-axis ticks: plt.yticks] Axes1 --> Grid[Grid lines: plt.grid] Axes1 --> Legend[Legend box: plt.legend] Axes1 --> Data1[Data series: plt.plot line, markers, error bars] Axes1 --> Annot[Annotations: plt.text, plt.annotate with arrow] Axes2 --> Data2[Independent data: separate subplot] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Plot to Use? This diagram helps students choose the **correct statistical plot** based on data type — frequently asked in viva. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[What is your data type?] --> Q1{Quantitative
vs Categorical?} Q1 -->|Quantitative| Q2{Single variable
or Bivariate?} Q1 -->|Categorical| Bar[Bar Chart: plt.bar] Q2 -->|Single variable| Q3{Show distribution?} Q2 -->|Bivariate| Scatter[Scatter Plot: plt.scatter] Q3 -->|Yes| Hist[Histogram: plt.hist] Q3 -->|No, just trend| Line[Line Plot: plt.plot] Hist --> Density{Overlay PDF?} Density -->|Yes| KdePlot[Histogram + np/pdf overlay] Density -->|No| Hist Line --> Annot{Annotate peaks?} Annot -->|Yes| AnnoLine[Line + plt.annotate with arrow] Annot -->|No| Line ``` ## 4.4 Text-Addition Call Graph A call graph showing how text objects relate to the Axes and Figure hierarchy. ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[plt.title] --> A1[Axes.set_title] P2[plt.xlabel] --> A2[Axes.set_xlabel] P3[plt.ylabel] --> A3[Axes.set_ylabel] P4[plt.suptitle] --> F1[Figure.suptitle] P5[plt.legend] --> A4[Axes.legend] P6[plt.text] --> A5[Axes.text] P7[plt.annotate] --> A6[Axes.annotate with arrowprops] A1 --> Out[Rendered on Canvas] A2 --> Out A3 --> Out F1 --> Out A4 --> Out A5 --> Out A6 --> Out ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain the role of `plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))` in matplotlib. What is the meaning of `figsize` and `dpi`?** **Model Answer:** The `plt.figure()` function creates a new top-level canvas (Figure object) on which all subsequent plotting takes place. The `figsize` argument is a tuple specifying the **width and height of the figure in inches**, where the first element is width and the second is height. In the example `figsize=(10, 6)`, the figure is 10 inches wide and 6 inches tall. The `dpi` argument stands for "dots per inch" and controls the **resolution** of the rendered image. A higher dpi (e.g., 300) produces a print-quality, sharper image, while the default `dpi=100` is suitable for on-screen viewing. Together, `figsize` and `dpi` determine the final pixel dimensions: width_inches × dpi by height_inches × dpi. These are essential controlling parameters used in KTU lab reports to meet publication or submission size requirements. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Differentiate between `plt.plot()` and `plt.scatter()`. When would you prefer one over the other?** **Model Answer:** `plt.plot()` and `plt.scatter()` are both used to render data points on a 2D coordinate system, but they serve different analytical purposes. `plt.plot(x, y)` draws a **connected line** through the (x, y) pairs by default, with markers as an optional secondary feature. It is optimized for **time-series, function curves, and ordered data** where the sequential relationship is meaningful (e.g., plotting $\sin(x)$ over a continuous range). `plt.scatter(x, y)` draws **individual unconnected markers** for each (x, y) pair. It is optimized for showing the **distribution and density of data points**, supports per-point size variation (`s` parameter) and per-point color mapping (`c` parameter), making it ideal for visualizing clusters, outliers, and correlations in raw datasets. **Rule of thumb:** Use `plt.plot()` for continuous/trended data, and `plt.scatter()` for examining point distributions or when individual point properties (size, color) carry information. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Common mark-deduction zones in this topic: > 1. **Forgetting `plt.show()`** — code compiles but no figure appears, costing 2–3 marks. > 2. **Calling `plt.legend()` without `label=` in the plot call** — legend appears empty. > 3. **Placing `plt.savefig()` after `plt.show()`** — saved file is blank. > 4. **Using backslash `\` inside non-raw strings for LaTeX** — raises `SyntaxError`; always use `r'$...$'`. > 5. **Confusing `Axes` (singular coordinate system) with `Figure` (whole canvas)** — applying `set_title` to the Figure raises an error. > 6. **Forgetting `plt.tight_layout()`** — axis labels overlap with neighboring subplots. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Python program using matplotlib to plot the function $y = x^2 \sin(x)$ for $x \in [-2\pi, 2\pi]$. Your plot must include: a title, x-axis and y-axis labels, a grid, a legend, and the curve drawn in green with a dashed line style. Add an annotation with an arrow pointing to the local maximum near $x = 2\pi$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between the functions `plt.text()` and `plt.annotate()`. Demonstrate with a code snippet how `plt.annotate()` is used to add an arrow-pointed label on a scatter plot of your choice. --- ### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Data preparation x = np.linspace(-2*np.pi, 2*np.pi, 400) y = x**2 * np.sin(x) # Figure creation with control plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5), dpi=100) # Plot with controlling parameters plt.plot(x, y, color='green', linestyle='--', linewidth=2.0, label=r'$y = x^2 \sin(x)$') # Title, labels, grid, legend plt.title("Plot of y = x² sin(x) for x ∈ [-2π, 2π]", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold') plt.xlabel("x (radians)", fontsize=12) plt.ylabel("y", fontsize=12) plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.6) plt.legend(loc='upper left', fontsize=11) # Annotation pointing to the local maximum near 2π plt.annotate("Local maximum near 2π", xy=(2*np.pi, (2*np.pi)**2 * np.sin(2*np.pi)), xytext=(1.2*np.pi, 30), fontsize=11, color='darkred', fontweight='bold', arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred', lw=1.5)) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown:** - Correct imports + data generation: **1 mark** - Figure creation with control: **1 mark** - Plot call with correct color/linestyle/linewidth/label: **2 marks** - Title, xlabel, ylabel, grid, legend: **2 marks** - Annotation with correct arrowprops: **1 mark** --- ### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Theoretical Explanation (4 marks):** `plt.text(x, y, s)` places a static string `s` at the data coordinates `(x, y)` on the current Axes. It does **not** support arrows; the text simply appears at the location. `plt.annotate(text, xy, xytext, arrowprops)` is the **interactive annotation function**. It places `text` at `xytext` (the text position) and draws an arrow from there to `xy` (the target point on the data). The `arrowprops` dictionary controls the arrow's style, color, and width. This makes `annotate()` ideal for highlighting specific features (peaks, outliers, intersections) in a plot. **Code Demonstration (3 marks):** ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Data x = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]) y = np.array([12, 18, 25, 30, 28, 22, 18, 10]) # Scatter plot plt.figure(figsize=(7, 5)) plt.scatter(x, y, color='blue', s=80, alpha=0.7) plt.title("Sales vs Time") plt.xlabel("Quarter") plt.ylabel("Sales (₹ Lakhs)") plt.grid(True, linestyle=':') # Using plt.text — static, no arrow plt.text(2, 22, "Mid-year dip region", fontsize=10, color='gray') # Using plt.annotate — arrow-pointed plt.annotate("Peak sales", xy=(4, 30), # arrow head xytext=(5.5, 26), # text location fontsize=11, fontweight='bold', color='darkred', arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='darkred')) plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown:** - Correct theoretical distinction (text vs annotate, arrow support): **4 marks** - Working code with both functions demonstrated: **3 marks** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** Using matplotlib, generate 500 random samples from a Normal distribution with mean $\mu = 50$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$. Draw a histogram with 25 bins, overlay the theoretical PDF curve, label both the axes, add a title with LaTeX-rendered $\mu$ and $\sigma$, and include a legend. Save the figure as `normal_dist.png` with dpi=300. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of subplots in matplotlib. Write a program that creates a 1×3 subplot grid showing: (i) a sine wave, (ii) a cosine wave, (iii) the sum $\sin(x) + \cos(x)$ for $x \in [0, 2\pi]$. --- ### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from scipy.stats import norm # Step 1: Seed for reproducibility np.random.seed(2024) # Step 2: Generate samples samples = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=500) # Step 3: Create figure plt.figure(figsize=(9, 5)) # Step 4: Histogram plt.hist(samples, bins=25, density=True, color='lightblue', edgecolor='black', alpha=0.7, label='Empirical') # Step 5: Theoretical PDF overlay x_theory = np.linspace(20, 80, 300) y_theory = norm.pdf(x_theory, loc=50, scale=10) plt.plot(x_theory, y_theory, color='red', linewidth=2.5, label='Theoretical PDF') # Step 6: LaTeX-rendered title plt.title(r"Normal Distribution: $\mu = 50,\ \sigma = 10$", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold') plt.xlabel("Value", fontsize=12) plt.ylabel("Probability Density", fontsize=12) plt.legend(loc='upper right') plt.grid(True, linestyle=':', alpha=0.5) # Step 7: Save with high resolution plt.tight_layout() plt.savefig("normal_dist.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight') plt.show() ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown:** - Random sample generation with correct parameters: **1 mark** - Histogram with `density=True`: **1 mark** - PDF overlay using `scipy.stats.norm`: **2 marks** - LaTeX title with $\mu$ and $\sigma$: **1 mark** - Labels, legend, grid: **1 mark** - `savefig` with correct dpi: **1 mark** --- ### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Theoretical Explanation (3 marks):** A **subplot** in matplotlib is a way to display multiple plots within a single figure, arranged in a grid of `rows × columns`. Each subplot is an independent `Axes` object with its own data, title, and labels. Subplots are essential for comparing related visualizations side by side. The function `plt.subplots(rows, cols)` returns a Figure object and a 2D array (or 1D array if one dimension is 1) of Axes objects, indexed as `axes[i, j]` for the (i+1)-th row and (j+1)-th column. **Code Implementation (4 marks):** ```python import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt # Common x data x = np.linspace(0, 2*np.pi, 200) # Create 1x3 subplot grid fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(14, 4)) # Subplot 1: sin(x) axes[0].plot(x, np.sin(x), color='blue', linewidth=2) axes[0].set_title("sin(x)") axes[0].set_xlabel("x") axes[0].set_ylabel("sin(x)") axes[0].grid(True, linestyle=':') # Subplot 2: cos(x) axes[1].plot(x, np.cos(x), color='red', linewidth=2) axes[1].set_title("cos(x)") axes[1].set_xlabel("x") axes[1].set_ylabel("cos(x)") axes[1].grid(True, linestyle=':') # Subplot 3: sin(x) + cos(x) axes[2].plot(x, np.sin(x) + np.cos(x), color='green', linewidth=2) axes[2].set_title("sin(x) + cos(x)") axes[2].set_xlabel("x") axes[2].set_ylabel("sum") axes[2].grid(True, linestyle=':') # Overall title fig.suptitle("Trigonometric Trio", fontsize=14, fontweight='bold') plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95]) plt.show() ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown:** - Correct conceptual definition of subplots: **3 marks** - Working 1×3 subplot code with all three panels: **4 marks** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam** ### 🔑 Core Function Categories - **Plotting:** `plt.plot()`, `plt.scatter()`, `plt.bar()`, `plt.hist()`, `plt.pie()` - **Controlling:** `plt.figure()`, `plt.subplot()`, `plt.xlim()`, `plt.ylim()`, `plt.xticks()`, `plt.yticks()`, `plt.grid()`, plus `color`, `linestyle`, `linewidth`, `marker`, `markersize`, `alpha` arguments - **Text:** `plt.title()`, `plt.xlabel()`, `plt.ylabel()`, `plt.suptitle()`, `plt.legend()`, `plt.text()`, `plt.annotate()` - **Output:** `plt.show()`, `plt.savefig(filename, dpi=...)` ### 🔑 Key Controlling Arguments (Top 10) 1. `figsize=(w, h)` — canvas size in inches 2. `dpi=N` — resolution 3. `color='red'` — line/marker color 4. `linestyle='--'` — `'-'`, `'--'`, `':'`, `'-.'` 5. `linewidth=2.5` — line thickness 6. `marker='o'` — point shape 7. `markersize=8` — point diameter 8. `alpha=0.7` — transparency (0 to 1) 9. `label='Series A'` — legend entry 10. `grid=True` — show/hide gridlines ### 🔑 Text-Addition Syntax Patterns - Title with math: `plt.title(r"$\mu = 0$")` (raw string is **mandatory**) - Arrow annotation: `plt.annotate("text", xy=(x1,y1), xytext=(x2,y2), arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->"))` - Legend: `plt.legend(loc='best')` ### 🔑 Common Exam Pitfalls - ❌ Forgetting `plt.show()` → no output - ❌ `plt.legend()` without `label=` in plot → empty legend box - ❌ Using `\` in non-raw strings for LaTeX → syntax error - ❌ Confusing Figure and Axes methods - ❌ Saving with `plt.show()` before `plt.savefig()` → blank file - ❌ Not calling `plt.tight_layout()` → overlapping labels in subplots ### 🔑 Universal Plotting Pipeline $$\text{Data} \rightarrow \text{Figure} \rightarrow \text{Plot} \rightarrow \text{Control} \rightarrow \text{Annotate} \rightarrow \text{Show/Save}$$ Master this pipeline and you master KTU Module Experiment 2.

topics.title: Experiments on More Graph Types, Getting and setting values and Patches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCBL508 - COMPUTATIONAL STATISTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiments on More Graph Types, Getting and setting values and Patches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 More Graph Types in Matplotlib

Matplotlib's `pyplot` interface provides a wide spectrum of statistical graphs beyond the basic line plot. In **Module Experiment 2** of PCCBL508, the focus extends to **bar charts**, **histograms**, **pie charts**, **scatter plots**, and **box plots** — each designed to reveal a *different statistical dimension* of the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *More graph types* refers to specialized 2D plotting routines in `matplotlib.pyplot` that encode categorical (bar, pie), distributional (histogram, boxplot), and bivariate relational (scatter) information — as opposed to simple line plots which encode sequential or continuous data.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of a **chart library as a tool drawer in a workshop**:

- A **line plot** is a measuring tape — best for *continuous* trends over time.
- A **bar chart** is a row of labelled boxes — best for *comparing categories* (like sales of 5 products).
- A **histogram** is a binned warehouse shelf — best for showing *how many* values fall into *ranges* (like age groups).
- A **pie chart** is a sliced pizza — best for showing *parts of a whole* (percentage shares).
- A **scatter plot** is a dot-grid on graph paper — best for spotting *relationships* between two variables (like height vs. weight).
- A **box plot** is a five-number-summary ruler — best for seeing the *spread, median, and outliers* of data.

Each tool answers a different question about the *same dataset*.

---

## 1.2 Getting and Setting Values in Matplotlib

Plots are **objects** with many tunable properties. KTU expects students to retrieve (*get*) and modify (*set*) properties such as axis limits, tick labels, titles, line colors, and markers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition:**
> *Getting values* means reading the current state of a plot object (e.g., current x-limits via `get_xlim()`).
